<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> - **Part A:** 3-mark questions (answer in ~60–80 words, 1–2 paragraphs)
> - **Part B:** 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with internal choice between Question A and Question B from the same module.

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between hardwood and softwood. Give two examples of each used in shipbuilding.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Hardwood | Softwood |
|---|---|---|
| Botanical origin | Angiosperms (broad-leaved, enclosed seed) | Gymnosperms (coniferous, naked seed) |
| Structure | Has vessels (porous) + fibres | Only tracheids (non-porous) |
| Density | Generally higher (600–900 kg/m³) | Generally lower (400–600 kg/m³) |
| Strength | Generally higher bending & compressive strength | Generally lower strength but easier to work |
| Decay resistance | Variable; teak excellent | Variable; cedar naturally durable |

**Marine examples:**
- **Hardwoods:** Teak, Sal, Oak, Mahogany (for hulls, decks, keels)
- **Softwoods:** Deodar, Pine, Fir, Spruce (for masts, spars, light planking)

---

#### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`
**Define the term 'fiber saturation point' (FSP) of wood and state its significance in ship structural timber.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** The *Fiber Saturation Point (FSP)* is the moisture content of wood at which all the free water in the cell cavities has evaporated, but the cell walls are still completely saturated with bound water. **FSP ≈ 25–30% (typical 28%).**

**Significance in ship structures:**
1. **Below FSP**, wood begins to **shrink** as bound water leaves the cell walls — critical for dimensionally stable ship timbers (deck planks, keel pieces).
2. **Below FSP**, wood begins to **gain strength and stiffness** — proper seasoning below FSP is essential before installation.
3. **Above FSP**, the wood behaves as a *green material* — properties are independent of moisture content.
4. Ship timber is dried to an **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC) of 12–18%** before use to ensure dimensional stability in marine atmosphere.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** In Part B, the student is offered a *choice between Question A OR Question B* from the same module. Both are 14-mark questions with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Below are two fully-worked model questions.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Wood Question

**Q A(a).** `[7 marks, KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain with neat sketches: (i) the macro-structure of a tree trunk cross-section, and (ii) the three principal directions of wood (longitudinal, radial, tangential) and their relationship to mechanical properties.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**(i) Macro-structure of a tree trunk (5 marks with labelled diagram):**

The cross-section of a tree trunk (from inside out) consists of:

1. **Pith** — the central core (1–5 mm diameter), soft, the original growth point. (0.5 mark)
2. **Heartwood** — inner dead wood, dark-coloured, contains extractives (tannins, resins, oils). High natural durability. Preferred for marine structural use. (1 mark)
3. **Sapwood** — outer living wood, lighter in colour, conducts sap, more permeable but less decay-resistant. (1 mark)
4. **Cambium layer** — a thin layer of living cells between sapwood and bark; produces new wood (xylem) inward and new bark (phloem) outward. (1 mark)
5. **Bark** — outer protective layer, dead tissue, shields against mechanical damage and temperature extremes. (0.5 mark)
6. **Annual growth rings** — concentric circles; each ring = one year's growth, with *springwood* (lighter, lower density, larger cells) and *summerwood* (darker, higher density, smaller cells) layers. (1 mark)

> *[Sketch: a labelled cross-section showing all six features with appropriate arrows. Reference: any standard textbook illustration of log cross-section.]* (placeholder for student to draw)

**(ii) Three principal directions of wood (2 marks):**

| Direction | Definition | Property Ratio (approx.) |
|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal (L)** | Parallel to grain, along trunk axis | $E_L \approx 10\,000 - 15\,000$ MPa (strongest, stiffest) |
| **Radial (R)** | Perpendicular to growth rings, from pith to bark | $E_R \approx E_L / 15$ |
| **Tangential (T)** | Perpendicular to growth rings, tangent to annual ring | $E_T \approx E_L / 20$ (weakest) |

*Engineering implication:* Ship structural members (masts, keelsons, deck beams) must be aligned with grain along the principal load direction to maximize strength.

---

**Q A(b).** `[7 marks, KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**A wooden deck beam of a ship is simply supported over a span of 4.5 m. It carries a uniformly distributed load of 18 kN/m (including self-weight). The beam has a rectangular cross-section 180 mm wide × 320 mm deep. The allowable bending stress for the timber is 12 MPa, and the modulus of elasticity is 11 GPa. Find: (i) the maximum bending moment, (ii) the maximum bending stress developed, (iii) the maximum deflection, and (iv) verify the design is safe.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Span: $L = 4.5$ m
- UDL: $w = 18$ kN/m = 18 000 N/m
- Breadth: $b = 180$ mm = 0.180 m
- Depth: $h = 320$ mm = 0.320 m
- Allowable bending stress: $\sigma_{allow} = 12$ MPa
- Modulus of elasticity: $E = 11$ GPa = 11 × 10⁹ Pa

---

**(i) Maximum bending moment (1.5 marks):**

For a simply-supported beam under UDL:

$$M_{max} = \frac{w L^2}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{18\,000 \times (4.5)^2}{8} = \frac{18\,000 \times 20.25}{8} = \frac{364\,500}{8}$$

$$M_{max} = 45\,562.5 \text{ N·m} \approx 45.56 \text{ kN·m}$$

---

**(ii) Maximum bending stress (2 marks):**

Section modulus for rectangular section:

$$Z = \frac{b h^2}{6} = \frac{0.180 \times (0.320)^2}{6}$$

$$Z = \frac{0.180 \times 0.1024}{6} = \frac{0.018432}{6} = 3.072 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Maximum bending stress:

$$\sigma_{b,max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{45\,562.5}{3.072 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\sigma_{b,max} = 14.83 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 14.83 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**(iii) Maximum deflection (2 marks):**

Moment of inertia:

$$I = \frac{b h^3}{12} = \frac{0.180 \times (0.320)^3}{12} = \frac{0.180 \times 0.032768}{12} = 4.915 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

For simply-supported beam with UDL:

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 w L^4}{384 E I}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 18\,000 \times (4.5)^4}{384 \times 11 \times 10^9 \times 4.915 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{5 \times 18\,000 \times 410.0625}{384 \times 11 \times 10^9 \times 4.915 \times 10^{-4}}$$

Numerator: $5 \times 18\,000 \times 410.0625 = 36\,905\,625$

Denominator: $384 \times 11 \times 10^9 \times 4.915 \times 10^{-4} = 384 \times 5.4065 \times 10^6 = 2.076 \times 10^9$

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{36.906 \times 10^6}{2.076 \times 10^9} = 0.01778 \text{ m} = 17.78 \text{ mm}$$

---

**(iv) Safety verification (1.5 marks):**

**Strength check:**
$$\sigma_{b,max} = 14.83 \text{ MPa} \quad > \quad \sigma_{allow} = 12 \text{ MPa} \quad \therefore \text{ UNSAFE in bending}$$

**Deflection check (typical limit L/360):**
$$\delta_{allow} = \frac{L}{360} = \frac{4500}{360} = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta_{max} = 17.78 \text{ mm} \quad > \quad \delta_{allow} = 12.5 \text{ mm} \quad \therefore \text{ UNSAFE in deflection}$$

**Conclusion:** The beam is **unsafe** under the given loads. Either the cross-section must be increased (e.g., to 200 mm × 360 mm), or the span must be reduced, or a stronger timber species must be used.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Module Choice

**Q B(a).** `[7 marks, KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any seven common defects in timber used for ship construction. State how each defect affects the strength of the structural timber.**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1 mark per defect × 7 defects):**

| # | Defect | Description | Effect on Ship Timber Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Knots** | Base of a branch embedded in the trunk; *dead knots* are loose, *live knots* are tight | Distort grain locally; reduce bending strength up to 30–50% at the knot; cause stress concentrations |
| 2 | **Shakes** | Separation of wood along grain (cup, heart, star, ring shake) | Acts as pre-existing crack; drastically reduces shear and tensile strength parallel to grain |
| 3 | **Twisted / cross grain** | Fibres not parallel to the longitudinal axis | Reduces longitudinal strength; component under axial load behaves as misaligned column |
| 4 | **Checks & splits** | Longitudinal cracks from uneven drying | Reduces shear capacity; creates path for water ingress → accelerates decay |
| 5 | **Decay / rot** (dry rot, wet rot) | Fungal degradation of cell wall | Total loss of strength; severely decayed timber has near-zero load capacity |
| 6 | **Insect / marine borer attack** (shipworm, gribble) | Internal tunneling by organisms | Hollows out cross-section; reduces effective load-bearing area |
| 7 | **Warping** (bow, cup, twist, crook) | Distortion from uneven shrinkage | Geometric misalignment; introduces unintended eccentric loading |

> **Marine-specific note:** Marine borer attack is the most damaging for ship hull planking — *Teredo navalis* can completely destroy an untreated plank within 6–24 months in tropical waters.

---

**Q B(b).** `[7 marks, KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**A ship's mast is a cylindrical wooden column, 14 m long, fixed at the base and free at the top. The diameter of the mast is 250 mm. The modulus of elasticity of the timber along the grain is 10 GPa. Determine: (i) the effective length of the column, (ii) the slenderness ratio, (iii) the Euler's critical buckling load, and (iv) the factor of safety if the actual compressive load on the mast is 25 kN.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Length: $L = 14$ m
- Boundary condition: Fixed–Free, so effective length factor $K = 2.0$
- Diameter: $d = 250$ mm = 0.250 m
- Modulus of elasticity: $E = 10$ GPa = 10 × 10⁹ Pa
- Actual load: $P = 25$ kN = 25 000 N

---

**(i) Effective length (1 mark):**

$$L_e = K L = 2.0 \times 14 = 28 \text{ m}$$

---

**(ii) Slenderness ratio (1.5 marks):**

For a circular section, radius of gyration:

$$r = \frac{d}{4} = \frac{0.250}{4} = 0.0625 \text{ m}$$

Slenderness ratio:

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{28}{0.0625} = 448$$

---

**(iii) Euler critical buckling load (2.5 marks):**

Moment of inertia of circular section:

$$I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times (0.250)^4}{64}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi \times 3.9063 \times 10^{-3}}{64} = \frac{0.012272}{64} = 1.9176 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

Euler's critical load:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 10 \times 10^9 \times 1.9176 \times 10^{-4}}{(28)^2}$$

Numerator: $\pi^2 \times 10 \times 10^9 \times 1.9176 \times 10^{-4} = 9.8696 \times 1.9176 \times 10^6 = 1.893 \times 10^7$

Denominator: $(28)^2 = 784$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{1.893 \times 10^7}{784} = 24\,143 \text{ N} \approx 24.14 \text{ kN}$$

---

**(iv) Factor of safety (2 marks):**

$$FOS = \frac{P_{cr}}{P_{actual}} = \frac{24\,143}{25\,000} = 0.966$$

$$\boxed{FOS = 0.97 \quad (\text{LESS than 1.0})}$$

---

**Conclusion:** The mast is **UNSAFE** — it will buckle under the design load (FOS < 1). Recommended remedial actions:
1. Reduce unsupported length by adding **stays and shrouds** (e.g., $L_e$ reduced from 28 m to 8 m → $P_{cr}$ rises by factor of $(28/8)^2 = 12.25$).
2. Increase diameter to at least 300 mm.
3. Use a timber with higher $E$ (e.g., select-grade teak, $E = 14$ GPa).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in WOOD questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MOR with MOE.** Modulus of Rupture = failure stress (max bending stress). Modulus of Elasticity = stiffness from slope of linear portion. They have the same units (MPa) but completely different physical meaning.
> 2. **Wrong moisture content formula.** Some students compute $(W_w - W_d)/W_w$ — this is the *wet-basis* MC, but IS / ASTM standard MC is **dry-basis** = $(W_w - W_d)/W_d$.
> 3. **Forgetting effective length factor.** Buckling problems require $L_e = K L$ with correct K (fixed-free: K = 2.0, fixed-pinned: K = 0.7, fixed-fixed: K = 0.5, pinned-pinned: K = 1.0). Direct substitution of L gives wrong P_cr by factor of 4.
> 4. **Mixing SI units** — kN vs N, mm vs m. Exam markers deduct 0.5–1 mark per inconsistency.
> 5. **Not drawing labelled diagrams** for "explain with sketch" questions. A blank page loses 2–3 marks even if the answer text is correct.
> 6. **Forgetting boundary condition for Euler column.** Always check **slenderness ratio** vs critical value; if λ < λ_critical, Euler formula is invalid and Johnson / Rankine formula should be used.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Wood (for last-minute KTU prep):**
>
> - **Wood =** anisotropic, hygroscopic, cellular composite of cellulose + hemicellulose + lignin.
> - **Three principal directions:** Longitudinal (L) > Radial (R) > Tangential (T) in stiffness and strength.
> - **FSP ≈ 25–30%** (avg 28%) — threshold below which shrinkage and strength gain occur.
> - **EMC ≈ 12–18%** in marine atmosphere — target moisture content for ship timber.
> - **Standard test MC = 12%** for all reported strength values in design codes.
> - **Density of seasoned wood:** 400–900 kg/m³; floats because less than water (1025 kg/m³).
> - **Hardwood ≠ hard, Softwood ≠ soft** — classification is botanical (angiosperm vs gymnosperm), not by actual hardness.
> - **Heartwood > Sapwood** for marine use (more extractives, more decay-resistant).
> - **Five principal strength tests:** Bending (MOR & MOE), Compression (∥ and ⊥), Tension (∥), Shear (∥), Hardness (Janka).
> - **Compressive strength perpendicular to grain ≈ 1/5 to 1/10 of parallel-to-grain** — bearing failures common under bolted connections if bearing area is too small.
> - **Seasoning methods:** Air, Kiln, Solar, Chemical. Improper seasoning → warping, checks, splits, honeycombing.
> - **Marine hazards:** Fungi (rot), Marine borers (Teredo, Limnoria), Bacterial decay.
> - **Preservation:** Natural (heartwood), Pressure (CCA, creosote), Surface (paint, varnish). Marine-grade timber must be **seasoned + treated**.
> - **Common defects:** Knots, shakes, cross-grain, checks/splits, decay, borer attack, warping.
> - **Key formulas (memorize):**
>   - $MC = (W_w - W_d)/W_d \times 100$
>   - $S = (D_g - D_d)/D_g \times 100$
>   - $\sigma_b = M/Z$ with $Z = bh^2/6$
>   - $\delta_{max} = 5wL^4/(384EI)$ (simply supported, UDL)
>   - $P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2$ with $L_e = K L$
> - **Effective length factors (K):** Fixed-Fixed = 0.5, Fixed-Pinned = 0.7, Pinned-Pinned = 1.0, Fixed-Free = 2.0.
> - **Marine timbers (must know):** **Teak** (best, gold-standard), **Sal** (strong, durable), **Deodar** (softwood, good for masts), **Pine** (cheap, requires treatment), **Oak** (strong, needs treatment in seawater), **Mahogany** (joinery), **Iroko** (African teak substitute).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shipbuilding Steel** is a hot-rolled, fine-grained structural carbon-manganese steel specifically manufactured to meet the stringent toughness, weldability, and strength requirements imposed by **classification societies** (such as LR, ABS, DNV, BV, IRS) for the construction of merchant vessels, naval ships, and offshore structures. It is defined by its controlled chemical composition (low carbon equivalent), guaranteed minimum yield strength ($R_{eH}$), tensile strength ($R_m$), and impact energy absorption (Charpy V-notch at specified sub-zero temperatures).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** Steel remains the *primary* structural material for ~90% of the world fleet because it offers the optimal balance of **strength, ductility, weldability, availability, and cost**. Any student of ship structures *must* master its grades, properties, and certification routes.

## 1.2 Standard Classification Metrics (Must be Memorised)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value (Hull Plates) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Minimum Yield Strength | $R_{eH}$ | **235 MPa** (Grade A) |
| High-Tensile Yield Strength | $R_{eH}$ | **315 MPa, 355 MPa, 390 MPa** (AH/DH/EH 32/36/40) |
| Minimum Tensile Strength | $R_m$ | **400 – 520 MPa** |
| Minimum Elongation | $A_{5}$ | **22%** |
| Charpy Impact Energy (Long. at 0°C) | $KV$ | **27 J** (Grade A) |
| Charpy Impact Energy (Trans. at –20°C) | $KV$ | **27 J / 34 J** (Grades D/E & HTS) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** Think of steel like the **bones of a human body**. Just as bones must be strong (not break under load), ductile (not snap like glass), and consistent in quality (no weak spots that could fail catastrophically), shipbuilding steel is engineered to do exactly the same for a vessel battling slamming waves, cargo loads, and fatigue over 25–30 years of service. *Normal steel from a construction site will fail this test — only certified ship steel passes.*

## 1.3 Why Not Just Use "Any" Steel?

A ship hull experiences **brittle fracture risk** in cold ocean waters, **fatigue loading** from waves, and **welding-induced residual stresses**. Ordinary mild steel becomes brittle below 0°C and may crack catastrophically (like the *Liberty Ships* of WWII). Hence, classification societies enforce **toughness testing, ultrasonic examination, and certified rolling practices** on every plate.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chemical Composition Logic (Why Each Element?)

The mechanical behaviour of hull steel is *engineered* through its alloying recipe. Each element serves a specific purpose:

- **Carbon (C) — max 0.21% (Grade A), ≤ 0.18% (HTS):** Provides strength, but excessive carbon increases hardness and *reduces weldability* (causes hardening in the Heat Affected Zone, HAZ, leading to cold cracking).
- **Manganese (Mn) — 0.60–1.70%:** Boosts strength *and* toughness; a "cheap" strengthener. Mn/C ratio ≥ 3 is required to suppress grain-boundary sulphide films.
- **Silicon (Si) — ≤ 0.50%:** De-oxidiser during steelmaking (kills dissolved oxygen).
- **Sulphur (S) — ≤ 0.035%** and **Phosphorus (P) — ≤ 0.035%:** Strictly controlled impurities. High S causes *hot shortness* (cracking during rolling); high P causes *cold shortness* (brittle fracture at low temperatures).
- **Aluminium (Al) — ≥ 0.015% (killed steel):** Grain refiner; produces fine-grained steel with superior toughness.
- **Niobium (Nb), Vanadium (V), Titanium (Ti) — microalloying (HTS only):** Form carbides/nitrides that pin grain boundaries, enabling **TMCP** (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing) to achieve high strength *without* high carbon.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Carbon Equivalent (CEV) Matters:** Weldability is governed by:
> $$CEV = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$
> For ship steel, **CEV ≤ 0.40%** is mandatory (Grade A) and **≤ 0.38%** for HTS. Exceeding this makes cold cracking in welds almost inevitable.

## 2.2 Mechanical Properties — The 'Why' Behind Each Test

| Property | Test Method | What it Tells the Naval Architect |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Yield Strength $R_{eH}$ | Tensile test (ISO 6892-1) | Maximum stress the steel can sustain without permanent (plastic) deformation. **Dictates scantlings** (plate thickness via $t = f(R_{eH})$). |
| Tensile Strength $R_m$ | Tensile test | Ultimate load before fracture; defines the **safety margin** before collapse. |
| Elongation $A_{5}$ | Tensile test (gauge $5.65\sqrt{S_0}$) | Ductility / forming ability; needed for cold bending, flanging, and to absorb impact. |
| Charpy Impact $KV$ | Charpy V-notch (ISO 148-1) | **Toughness at service temperature**; prevents brittle fracture. |
| CTOD $\delta$ | Fracture-mechanics test | Crack-tip opening displacement; for thick plates in critical zones. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains *all* the high-yield formulas you need for Module 1 derivations and Part-B problems. **No pipe symbols are used in cell values** to keep the table parser-safe.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Axial stress from applied force $F$ on cross-section $A$ | MPa |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain (elongation) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $R_{eH} = \dfrac{F_{y}}{A_0}$ | Yield strength = yield load / original area | MPa |
| 4 | $R_m = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | MPa |
| 5 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | Young's Modulus of ship steel (constant $\approx$ 205 GPa) | GPa |
| 6 | $CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$ | IIW Carbon Equivalent for weldability | % |
| 7 | $A_{5} = \dfrac{L_u - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Percentage elongation after fracture | % |
| 8 | $t = \dfrac{s \cdot k}{\lambda}$ (simplified) | Plate thickness $t$ in terms of stress $s$, factor $k$, and $\lambda$ | mm |
| 9 | $KV_{req} = 27 \text{ J}$ (longitudinal, 0°C) | Minimum Charpy V-notch for Grade A | J |
| 10 | $T_{27J} = 0 \text{ °C (Gr.A); } -20 \text{ °C (Gr.D); } -40 \text{ °C (Gr.E)}$ | Transition temperature for 27 J impact | °C |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Every line on a ship's **General Arrangement (GA)** drawing is backed by steel grade selection. The **bottom shell** (immersed in cold water) uses **Grade D or E**; the **side shell** uses **Grade A/B**; **high-stress regions** (hatch corners, sheer strake, bilge strake) use **HTS (AH32 / DH36 / EH40)**. The selection directly determines the **scantlings** (plate thickness, stiffener sizes) per IACS Common Structural Rules (CSR).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Derivation: Relating Plate Thickness to Steel Grade

The basic hull-girder bending stress in a ship's bottom plating is given by:

$$\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Where:
- $M$ = hull girder bending moment (Nm)
- $y$ = distance from neutral axis (m)
- $I$ = second moment of area ($m^4$)

For a rectangular plate of width $b$ and thickness $t$, the section modulus is:

$$Z = \dfrac{b \cdot t^{2}}{6}$$

And the bending stress simplifies to:

$$\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{6M}{b \cdot t^{2}}$$

Rearranging to solve for required plate thickness:

$$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6M}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$$

And the allowable stress is taken as a fraction of the yield strength (typically $\eta = 0.6$ per CSR-B):

$$\sigma_{allow} = \eta \cdot R_{eH}$$

Therefore, **the final governing equation becomes:**

$$t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6M}{b \cdot \eta \cdot R_{eH}}}$$

> **Engineering Insight (The 'Why'):** Notice that $t$ is **inversely proportional to $\sqrt{R_{eH}}$**. Doubling the yield strength (e.g., from Grade A = 235 MPa to HTS = 390 MPa) reduces plate thickness by only $\sqrt{2/3.9} \approx 29\%$. Hence, HTS is used *strategically* in high-stress zones to **save weight**, not in bulk hull plating (where the cost premium is not justified).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Exam Style)

**Problem:** A bottom shell plate is subjected to a hull girder bending moment $M = 2.5 \times 10^{6}$ Nm per metre width. Calculate the required thickness if (a) Grade A steel ($R_{eH} = 235$ MPa) is used, and (b) Grade AH32 steel ($R_{eH} = 315$ MPa) is used. Take $\eta = 0.6$ and $b = 1$ m.

### Solution (a) — Grade A:

$$t_{A} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 0.6 \times 235 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{A} = \sqrt{\dfrac{15.0 \times 10^{6}}{141.0 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{A} = \sqrt{0.10638}$$

$$t_{A} = 0.3262 \text{ m} \approx 326 \text{ mm}$$

### Solution (b) — Grade AH32:

$$t_{AH32} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 2.5 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 0.6 \times 315 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{AH32} = \sqrt{\dfrac{15.0 \times 10^{6}}{189.0 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{AH32} = \sqrt{0.07937}$$

$$t_{AH32} = 0.2817 \text{ m} \approx 282 \text{ mm}$$

### Weight Saving Calculation:

$$\Delta t = 326 - 282 = 44 \text{ mm saved per metre width}$$

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{44}{326} \times 100 = 13.5\%$$

> **Conclusion:** By upgrading from Grade A to AH32 in highly stressed zones, the designer saves ~13.5% steel weight in that region — significant for a 300 m long hull.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Shipbuilding Grades

| Grade | $R_{eH}$ (MPa) | $R_m$ (MPa) | Charpy Test Temp | Typical Use Case | Relative Cost |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **A** | 235 | 400–520 | 0 °C (Long.) | Mild regions, decks, superstructure | 1.0 (baseline) |
| **B** | 235 | 400–520 | 0 °C (Long.) | Side shell, ordinary bulkheads | 1.0 |
| **D** | 235 | 400–520 | –20 °C (Trans.) | Bottom plates in temperate waters | 1.1 |
| **E** | 235 | 400–520 | –40 °C (Trans.) | Bottom plates in cold seas (Baltic, North Atlantic) | 1.25 |
| **AH32** | 315 | 440–590 | 0 °C (Trans.) | Sheer strake, hatch coamings | 1.4 |
| **DH36** | 355 | 490–620 | –20 °C (Trans.) | High-stress hull regions | 1.6 |
| **EH40** | 390 | 510–660 | –40 °C (Trans.) | Ice-class vessels, arctic ships | 2.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Exam:** Always state *both* the yield strength *and* the impact test temperature in your answer. Examiners deduct marks if only one is mentioned.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Steel Grade Selection Logic for a Ship Hull

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ship Structural Design Begins] --> B{Identify Hull Region}
    B --> C1[Bottom Shell Plating]
    B --> C2[Side Shell Plating]
    B --> C3[Upper Deck]
    B --> C4[High Stress Sheer Strake]
    B --> C5[Arctic / Ice Belt]

    C1 --> D1{Service Water Temperature}
    D1 -- Above 0 deg C --> E1[Use Grade B Normalised]
    D1 -- 0 to -20 deg C --> E2[Use Grade D Normalised]
    D1 -- Below -20 deg C --> E3[Use Grade E Normalised]

    C2 --> F1[Use Grade A or B as Rolled]

    C3 --> F1

    C4 --> G1{Stress Level}
    G1 -- High Bending --> H1[Use HTS AH32 or DH36 TMCP]
    G1 -- Extreme Bending --> H2[Use HTS EH40 TMCP]

    C5 --> I1[Use Grade E plus EH40 in Ice Belt]

    E1 --> J1[Verify CEV and Charpy 27 J at 0 deg C]
    E2 --> J2[Verify CEV and Charpy 27 J at -20 deg C]
    E3 --> J3[Verify CEV and Charpy 27 J at -40 deg C]
    F1 --> J1
    H1 --> J2
    H2 --> J3
    I1 --> J3

    J1 --> K1[Class Society Certification: LR / ABS / DNV / BV]
    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Mill Test Certificate MTC Issued]
    L1 --> M1[Steel Approved for Shipbuilding]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Manufacturing & Quality Control Pipeline for Hull Steel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Iron Ore and Scrap] --> S2[Basic Oxygen Furnace BOF]
    S3[Ferro Alloys Mn Si Al] --> S2
    S2 --> S4[Continuous Casting of Slabs]
    S4 --> S5{Rolling Route}
    S5 -- As Rolled --> S6[Normalising Furnace]
    S5 -- Controlled --> S7[TMCP Thermo Mechanical]
    S6 --> S8[Mechanical Testing Lab]
    S7 --> S8
    S8 --> S9[Tensile Test Rm and ReH]
    S8 --> S10[Charpy Impact at Specified Temp]
    S8 --> S11[Ultrasonic Testing for Internal Flaws]
    S8 --> S12[Chemical Analysis Spectrometer]
    S9 --> S13{All Results Pass Spec}
    S10 --> S13
    S11 --> S13
    S12 --> S13
    S13 -- Yes --> S14[Stamp with Grade Mark and Class Symbol]
    S13 -- No --> S15[Reject and Re-melt]

```

## 4.3 Visual: Microstructural Concept (Text-Based Schematic)

```
  +-------------------------------------+
  |   SHIP STEEL MICROSTRUCTURE (IDEAL) |
  +-------------------------------------+

  |   [F] = Ferrite (soft, ductile)     |
  |   [P] = Pearlite (strong, lamellar) |
  |   [G] = Grain boundary (fine, Al-killed)

  |    F  P  F  P  F                    |
  |   ---+---+---+---+---   Grain line 1

  |   ---+---+---+---+---   Grain line 2
  |    F  P  F  P  F                    |

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Pattern]**

### Q1. Define *Charpy V-notch impact test*. Why is it mandatory for ship steel?
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Charpy V-notch impact test (ISO 148-1) is a standardised test in which a small specimen with a V-shaped notch is struck by a pendulum hammer, and the **energy absorbed in fracturing** the specimen at a specified temperature is recorded in Joules. This energy ($KV$) is a direct measure of the material's **toughness** — its ability to absorb sudden dynamic loads without brittle fracture. It is mandatory for ship steel because a vessel operates in cold seawater, and a brittle fracture at low temperature can propagate at speeds up to 1500 m/s, splitting the hull catastrophically (as in the WWII Liberty Ship failures). Classification societies therefore demand $KV \geq 27$ J at the service temperature for the relevant grade.

---

### Q2. What is the **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)**? State its limit for Grade A hull steel.
**(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV), as defined by the **IIW formula**, is a numerical index that quantifies the combined effect of carbon and alloying elements on the **weldability** of steel:

$$CEV = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni + Cu}{15}$$

For Grade A hull steel, the **maximum permissible CEV is 0.40%** (and 0.38% for high-tensile grades). Exceeding this limit causes excessive hardening in the weld Heat-Affected Zone (HAZ), leading to hydrogen-induced cold cracking.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** (Answer any one full question, with sub-parts a & b for 7 marks each)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify the various grades of shipbuilding steel used for hull construction, listing typical yield strengths, tensile strengths, and Charpy test temperatures for each grade. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bottom plate in a bulk carrier is subjected to a bending moment $M = 1.8 \times 10^{6}$ Nm per metre width. Using Grade A steel ($R_{eH} = 235$ MPa), calculate the required plate thickness. If the designer upgrades to Grade DH36 ($R_{eH} = 355$ MPa), what is the percentage saving in plate weight? Take $\eta = 0.6$ and $b = 1$ m. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

Classification of shipbuilding steel grades per IACS UR Z23 / Z25 / Z27:

| Grade | $R_{eH}$ | $R_m$ | Charpy Test |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | 27 J at 0 °C (Long.) |
| B | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | 27 J at 0 °C (Long.) |
| D | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | 27 J at –20 °C (Trans.) |
| E | 235 MPa | 400–520 MPa | 27 J at –40 °C (Trans.) |
| AH32 | 315 MPa | 440–590 MPa | 31 J at 0 °C (Trans.) |
| DH32 | 315 MPa | 440–590 MPa | 31 J at –20 °C (Trans.) |
| EH32 | 315 MPa | 440–590 MPa | 31 J at –40 °C (Trans.) |
| AH36 | 355 MPa | 490–620 MPa | 34 J at 0 °C (Trans.) |
| DH36 | 355 MPa | 490–620 MPa | 34 J at –20 °C (Trans.) |
| EH36 | 355 MPa | 490–620 MPa | 34 J at –40 °C (Trans.) |
| AH40 | 390 MPa | 510–660 MPa | 39 J at 0 °C (Trans.) |
| DH40 | 390 MPa | 510–660 MPa | 39 J at –20 °C (Trans.) |
| EH40 | 390 MPa | 510–660 MPa | 39 J at –40 °C (Trans.) |

Grades A–E are **ordinary-strength** (mild steel); the 32/36/40 series are **high-tensile steels (HTS)**. The letter prefix (A/D/E) denotes the **impact test temperature**; the suffix (32/36/40) denotes the **yield strength** in ksi ÷ 10 (legacy notation, 32 → 315 MPa, etc.).

#### Model Solution (b):

**Step 1:** Required thickness for Grade A:

$$t_{A} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6M}{b \cdot \eta \cdot R_{eH}}}$$

$$t_{A} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 1.8 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 0.6 \times 235 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{A} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10.8 \times 10^{6}}{141.0 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{0.07660}$$

$$t_{A} = 0.2768 \text{ m} \approx 277 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2:** Required thickness for Grade DH36:

$$t_{DH36} = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 1.8 \times 10^{6}}{1 \times 0.6 \times 355 \times 10^{6}}}$$

$$t_{DH36} = \sqrt{\dfrac{10.8 \times 10^{6}}{213.0 \times 10^{6}}} = \sqrt{0.05070}$$

$$t_{DH36} = 0.2252 \text{ m} \approx 225 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3:** Weight (per unit area) is proportional to thickness. Hence:

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{277 - 225}{277} \times 100 = \dfrac{52}{277} \times 100 \approx 18.77\%$$

**Answer:** Thickness for Grade A = **277 mm**; thickness for Grade DH36 = **225 mm**; **weight saving ≈ 18.8%**.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the significance of **TMCP (Thermo-Mechanical Controlled Processing)** in modern ship steel production. How does it differ from conventional normalising? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define **yield strength**, **ultimate tensile strength**, and **percentage elongation**. A 20 mm diameter tensile specimen of Grade A steel fractured at a maximum load of 145 kN. Calculate (i) the ultimate tensile strength, and (ii) the percentage elongation if the original gauge length was 100 mm and final length was 122 mm. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution (a):

**TMCP** is a modern steelmaking process in which the slab is subjected to precisely controlled **deformation (rolling) and temperature** schedules to achieve fine grain size and superior mechanical properties *without* relying on high carbon content. The sequence involves:

1. **Slab Reheating** to ~1200 °C (lower than conventional).
2. **Rough Rolling** in the austenite recrystallisation zone.
3. **Finish Rolling** in the non-recrystallisation zone (below ~950 °C), imparting accumulated strain that produces very fine pancake-shaped austenite grains.
4. **Accelerated Cooling** directly after rolling, suppressing grain growth and producing an ultra-fine ferrite-pearlite or bainitic microstructure.

**Difference from Normalising:**

| Aspect | Conventional Normalising | TMCP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carbon required for strength | High (0.18–0.21%) | Low (0.10–0.14%) |
| Weldability | Marginal | Excellent |
| Process | Reheat to 900 °C, air cool | Controlled roll + accelerated cool |
| Energy consumption | High (reheating) | Low (single pass) |
| Toughness | Good | Superior (fine grain) |
| Cost | Lower for mild grades | Higher; offset by less alloying |

**Significance:** TMCP allows the production of **EH40 (390 MPa yield)** with $C \leq 0.12\%$ — impossible by conventional normalising. This dramatically improves weldability, reduces preheating requirements, and is essential for modern high-tensile hull plates used in LNG carriers, aircraft carriers, and ice-class vessels.

#### Model Solution (b):

**Definitions:**

- **Yield Strength ($R_{eH}$):** The stress at which the material begins to deform plastically (i.e., permanent deformation occurs). It is the maximum stress a designer *routinely* permits in service.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength ($R_m$):** The maximum stress the material can withstand; the peak of the stress–strain curve, after which necking begins.
- **Percentage Elongation ($A$):** The ratio of the increase in gauge length after fracture to the original gauge length, expressed as a percentage; a measure of ductility.

**Calculations:**

Cross-sectional area of specimen:
$$A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.020)^{2}}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^{2}$$

(i) **Ultimate Tensile Strength:**

$$R_m = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{145 \times 10^{3}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R_m = 4.615 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 461.5 \text{ MPa}$$

(ii) **Percentage Elongation:**

$$A_{5} = \dfrac{L_u - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{122 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 22\%$$

**Answer:** $R_m = \mathbf{461.5 \text{ MPa}}$; Elongation $= \mathbf{22\%}$ (which matches the minimum specified for Grade A — specimen has passed!).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Writing "$R_m = 461.5$" without "MPa" → **–1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing $R_{eH}$ and $R_m$:** Examiners note the distinction: $R_{eH}$ is yield, $R_m$ is ultimate. Mixing them → **–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not specifying test temperature for Charpy:** "27 J" alone is incomplete. You must say "**27 J at 0 °C (Longitudinal)**" for Grade A → **–1 mark**.
> 4. **Skipping the conversion step in calculations:** Jumping from formula to final answer without showing substitution → **lose method marks**.
> 5. **Using vertical bars `|x|` in your answer sheet tables** can confuse the KTU digitised OCR scanner. Use `\vert x \vert` notation or write "abs(x)" to be safe.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin This!)

- ✅ **Ship steel = controlled-rolling, Al-killed, fine-grained, certified by classification societies.**
- ✅ **Two families:** Ordinary-strength (Grades A/B/D/E, $R_{eH} = 235$ MPa) and High-Tensile (AH/DH/EH 32/36/40, $R_{eH} = 315/355/390$ MPa).
- ✅ **Three numbers to memorise per grade:** Yield strength, Tensile strength range, Charpy test temperature.
- ✅ **Prefix letter (A/D/E) = impact test temperature (0, –20, –40 °C).**
- ✅ **Suffix number (32/36/40) = yield strength tier.**
- ✅ **CEV ≤ 0.40% (Grade A), ≤ 0.38% (HTS) — guards weldability.**
- ✅ **E = 205 GPa** is the universal Young's Modulus for ship steel (use it unless told otherwise).
- ✅ **Plate thickness $t \propto 1/\sqrt{R_{eH}}$** — doubling yield saves only ~30% thickness.
- ✅ **TMCP** = modern route to high strength *with* low carbon (better weldability).
- ✅ **Liberty Ships** (1942–53) = historical lesson on why Charpy testing is non-negotiable.
- ✅ Always end numerical answers with a **boxed final answer** including units.
- ✅ For HTS, transverse specimens require 27 J minimum, but the *grade-specified* value is 31 J (32-series), 34 J (36-series), 39 J (40-series).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Mechanical Stress?

In the context of ship structures, every plate, stiffener, frame, and girder of a vessel is subjected to forces arising from hydrostatic pressure, wave slamming, cargo loads, and self-weight. The internal resistance generated per unit area inside the material to counter these external loads is termed **Stress**.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Stress ($\sigma$)** is defined as the internal restoring force per unit cross-sectional area developed within a deformable body when an external load is applied. Mathematically,
> $$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$
> where $\Delta F$ is the internal force acting on an infinitesimally small area $\Delta A$.

The standard SI unit is **N/m² (Pascal, Pa)**, and for ship-building steel the working values usually lie in the range of **MPa (10⁶ Pa)** to **GPa (10⁹ Pa)**.

### 1.2 What is Strain?

When a load is applied, the ship's structural member deforms. The ratio of this deformation to the original dimension is called **Strain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain ($\varepsilon$)** is a dimensionless quantity representing the relative change in dimension (length, angle, or volume) of a body under an applied load.
> $$\varepsilon = \lim_{\Delta L \to 0} \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$
> Here $L_0$ is the original gauge length and $\Delta L$ is the change in length.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rubber band** being stretched between your fingers:
- The harder you pull (more force), the more it stretches.
- The "pulling effort per cross-section" is like **stress**.
- The "amount of stretching relative to its original length" is like **strain**.
- If you plot *pull per strand (stress)* on the vertical axis and *stretch ratio (strain)* on the horizontal axis, you obtain a **Stress–Strain Diagram** — the fingerprint of how a material behaves under load.

For a **ship's hull girder** in heavy seas, the deck plating behaves like this rubber band: it stretches under wave bending, returns to shape in calm water, and may permanently deform in extreme storm conditions. The stress-strain diagram tells the naval architect exactly *when* this transition from "safe stretching" to "permanent damage" occurs.

### 1.4 Types of Stress (KTU 2024 Classification)

| # | Type | Symbol | Definition |
|---|------|--------|------------|
| 1 | Normal Stress (Tensile) | $\sigma_t$ | Force acting perpendicular and pulling outward |
| 2 | Normal Stress (Compressive) | $\sigma_c$ | Force acting perpendicular and pushing inward |
| 3 | Shear Stress | $\tau$ | Force acting parallel to the surface |
| 4 | Bearing Stress | $\sigma_b$ | Compressive stress over a contact area (e.g., rivets) |

### 1.5 Types of Strain

| # | Type | Symbol | Description |
|---|------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | Linear (Normal) Strain | $\varepsilon$ | Change in length per unit length |
| 2 | Shear Strain | $\gamma$ | Change in angle (radians) |
| 3 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v$ | Change in volume per unit volume |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Stress–Strain Curve — A Step-by-Step Walkthrough

For a typical **mild steel** specimen (the predominant hull material in shipbuilding) tested in a Universal Testing Machine (UTM), the engineering stress–strain diagram reveals five distinct regions. Each region has a specific physical meaning and a corresponding KTU-relevant term.

#### Region 1: Limit of Proportionality (Point A)
- The stress is **directly proportional** to the strain.
- Material obeys **Hooke's Law** strictly.
- The curve is a **straight line** through the origin.

#### Region 2: Elastic Limit (Point B)
- Up to this point, the material will return to its original shape upon unloading.
- No **permanent (plastic) deformation** is observed.
- For ship steels, the elastic limit is very close to the proportionality limit.

#### Region 3: Yield Point (Point C — Upper Yield, C′ — Lower Yield)
- Beyond this point, the material begins to **deform plastically** without any increase in stress.
- For mild steel, a distinct **yield plateau** appears.
- This is the most critical value for ship structural design — the designer ensures the working stress stays well below the yield stress.

#### Region 4: Strain-Hardening / Plastic Region (C to D)
- The material gains strength as it is deformed further (**work hardening**).
- The curve rises gradually toward the maximum load.

#### Region 5: Ultimate Stress Point (Point D)
- The **maximum stress** the material can withstand.
- Beyond this, **necking** begins — the cross-section reduces locally.

#### Region 6: Fracture / Breaking Point (Point E)
- The specimen **ruptures** completely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The names "Elastic Limit" and "Proportionality Limit" are *not* the same point. The proportionality limit is purely mathematical (validity of Hooke's Law), whereas the elastic limit is the largest stress for full recovery of shape. For most ship-building steels they are nearly coincident, but the distinction is examinable.

### 2.2 Hooke's Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Hooke's Law (1660):** Within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain.
> $$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$
> where **E** is the **Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)**, measured in **GPa**.

For ship-building mild steel, **E ≈ 200 GPa**. For marine grade aluminium alloys (used in fast ferries and naval craft), **E ≈ 70 GPa**.

### 2.3 Poisson's Ratio

When a bar is stretched, it becomes thinner laterally. The ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain is constant (within elastic limit) and is termed **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)**.

$$\nu = -\frac{\text{Lateral Strain}}{\text{Longitudinal Strain}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$$

For steels, **$\nu \approx 0.30$**. For marine aluminium alloys, **$\nu \approx 0.33$**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a must-memorize reference. Every equation here has appeared (or is highly likely to appear) in KTU University Examinations for OENST614.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | KTU Application |
|---|----------|---------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | Normal Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Hull girder bending |
| 2 | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Pa | Shear in riveted/bolted joints |
| 3 | Linear Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Dimensionless | Hull deflection |
| 4 | Shear Strain | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{y}$ | Radians | Plate distortion |
| 5 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Pa | Linear elastic analysis |
| 6 | Shear Modulus | $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | Pa | Torsional rigidity of shafts |
| 7 | Poisson's Ratio | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{long}}$ | Dimensionless | Biaxial stress in plating |
| 8 | Volumetric Strain | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Dimensionless | Triaxial sea-pressure |
| 9 | Relation E-G-ν | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ | Pa | Interconversion |
| 10 | Factor of Safety | $FoS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Dimensionless | Marine design codes |
| 11 | Strain Energy Density | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma^2}{2E}$ | J/m³ | Impact loading |
| 12 | True Stress | $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$ | Pa | Necking region |
| 13 | Toughness (Modulus) | $U_{T} = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f}\sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | J/m³ | Ductile vs brittle |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Marine Engineering

The stress-strain diagram is not a textbook curiosity — it directly governs:

- **Material Selection:** Naval architects select marine grade steel (e.g., **AH36, DH36, EH36** with yield strength **≥ 355 MPa**) over ordinary mild steel for hulls.
- **Plate Forming:** Cold-bending and line-heating processes rely on knowing the elastic limit precisely to avoid cracking.
- **Failure Prediction:** Sudden brittle fracture in Liberty Ships (WWII) was traced to low-temperature embrittlement — a property visible on the stress-strain curve at low temperatures.
- **Welding Procedures:** Heat Affected Zones (HAZ) must not exceed yield stress; residual stress estimates come from stress-strain data.
- **Crashworthiness:** High-speed craft and submarines use strain-energy density (toughness) from the area under the curve as a crash-energy absorber criterion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Bar to Stress

Consider a uniform cylindrical bar of initial length **$L_0$** and initial cross-sectional area **$A_0$**, subjected to an axial tensile force **F** at its ends.

**Step 1 — Internal Force Equilibrium:**
By Newton's third law, an internal restoring force of equal magnitude **F** acts on every imagined cross-section inside the bar, balancing the external load.

**Step 2 — Definition of Average Stress:**
The average internal force per unit area across any section is

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 3 — Limiting Definition (Tractable Form):**
As we consider an infinitesimally small area element $dA$ on which force $dF$ acts, the stress at a *point* becomes

$$\sigma = \lim_{dA \to 0}\frac{dF}{dA}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Simplification:**
For uniform bars, the stress is assumed uniform across the section, giving the standard form used in ship design:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: From Deformation to Strain

**Step 1 — Measure Original Length:**
Let the gauge length be $L_0$ (the original, unloaded length).

**Step 2 — Measure Elongation:**
Under load, the new length is $L_f = L_0 + \Delta L$.

**Step 3 — Define Engineering Strain:**
The change in length per unit original length is

$$\varepsilon_{eng} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Step 4 — True Strain (used in necking region):**
For large deformations, the true strain is defined incrementally:

$$\varepsilon_{true} = \int_{L_0}^{L_f}\frac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right) = \ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

**Step 5 — True Stress (volume constancy in plastic region):**
Assuming plastic deformation occurs at constant volume ($A_0 L_0 = A_f L_f$):

$$\sigma_{true} = \frac{F}{A_f} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{L_f}{L_0} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Hooke's Law and Modulus of Elasticity

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation:**
Within the elastic limit, experimental observation (Robert Hooke, 1660) shows that the elongation $\Delta L$ is proportional to the applied force F.

$$\Delta L \propto F$$

**Step 2 — Introducing Geometry:**
For a bar of length $L_0$ and area A:

$$\Delta L = \frac{F L_0}{A E}$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging:**

$$\frac{F}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation:**
The slope of the straight-line portion of the stress-strain curve equals Young's Modulus **E**. A steeper slope = stiffer material.

### 3.4 Derivation: Relationship Between E, G, and $\nu$

**Step 1 — Energy Equivalence (Pure Shear Case):**
The strain energy density in pure shear is given by

$$u = \frac{1}{2}\tau\gamma$$

**Step 2 — Energy Equivalence (Pure Tension Case):**
The strain energy density in uniaxial tension is

$$u = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

**Step 3 — Relating $\tau$ and $\sigma$ via Principal Stresses:**
For a state of pure shear, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$. The maximum shear stress equals the principal stress magnitude.

**Step 4 — Combining and Eliminating Energy:**
Substituting and equating strain energy densities (for an isotropic material):

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 5 — Bulk Modulus Relation (Bonus):**

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

where **K** is the Bulk Modulus, important for submarine pressure hulls subjected to triaxial sea pressure.

### 3.5 Worked Example (Numerical — KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A tie-bar in the deck of a bulk carrier is 2.0 m long with a circular cross-section of 25 mm diameter. It carries an axial tensile load of 80 kN. The bar stretches by 1.6 mm under the load. Given E = 200 GPa, calculate:
> (a) The stress in the bar.
> (b) The strain in the bar.
> (c) Verify Hooke's Law.
> (d) The lateral contraction, assuming $\nu = 0.30$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress:**
The cross-sectional area is

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

The stress is

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.629 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 162.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0} = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Part (c) — Verification of Hooke's Law:**

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{162.9 \times 10^{6}}{8.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.036 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$$

Since this matches the given E (within rounding), Hooke's Law is verified.

**Part (d) — Lateral Contraction:**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -0.30 \times 8.0 \times 10^{-4} = -2.4 \times 10^{-4}$$

The reduction in diameter is

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -2.4 \times 10^{-4} \times 25 = -6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -6.0 \, \mu\text{m}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Always state the units explicitly in numerical answers. A number without units gets **zero marks** in the final step of a calculation.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Material Behaviour

| Property | Mild Steel | Marine Al Alloy | Cast Iron | High-Strength Ship Steel (EH36) |
|----------|-----------|----------------|-----------|-------------------------------|
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 200 | 70 | 100 | 200 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 250 | 240 | — (no yield) | 355 |
| Ultimate Strength (MPa) | 410 | 310 | 200 | 490–620 |
| Elongation at fracture (%) | 25 | 12 | 0.6 | 21 |
| Behaviour | Ductile | Ductile | Brittle | Ductile + High strength |
| Typical Ship Use | Hull plating (older) | Superstructure, fast craft | Stern frames, bearings | Modern hulls, ice-class ships |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Stress-Strain Curve Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **regions of the engineering stress-strain curve** for a ductile material such as mild ship-building steel, along with the *physical event* and *KTU terminology* corresponding to each region.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ORIGIN O: Zero load zero deformation"] --> B["REGION 1: Proportional Limit Point A — Hooke Law valid stress = E times strain"]
    B --> C["REGION 2: Elastic Limit Point B — Full recovery upon unloading"]
    C --> D["REGION 3: Upper Yield Point C — Plastic flow begins"]
    D --> E["REGION 3b: Lower Yield Plateau C to C prime — Luders bands propagate"]
    E --> F["REGION 4: Strain Hardening Zone C prime to D — Stress rises with strain"]
    F --> G["REGION 5: Ultimate Stress Point D — Maximum load capacity Necking begins"]
    G --> H["REGION 6: Fracture Point E — Specimen ruptures"]
    A --> A
    B -.-> B1["Slope = Young Modulus E"]
    D -.-> D1["Design limit for ship structures"]
    G -.-> G1["Safety factor evaluated here"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a *distinct physical state* of the test specimen. The dotted arrows indicate the *engineering significance* of that state. This maps the abstract curve to a physical narrative, which is exactly how KTU board questions are framed in the 14-mark problems.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress-Strain Path

This diagram explains *how* the stress-strain data flows from the test specimen back to the design office, mirroring the actual workflow used in classification societies (DNV, Lloyd's Register, ABS).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Universal Testing Machine UTM"] --> S2["Load Cell and Extensometer"]
    S2 --> S3["Raw Data: Force versus Elongation"]
    S3 --> S4["Convert to Stress versus Strain"]
    S4 --> S5["Plot Engineering Stress Strain Curve"]
    S5 --> S6["Extract Young Modulus E"]
    S5 --> S7["Extract Yield Stress sigma y"]
    S5 --> S8["Extract Ultimate Stress sigma u"]
    S5 --> S9["Compute Toughness Area under curve"]
    S6 --> S10["Material Database for Ship Design"]
    S7 --> S10
    S8 --> S10
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11["Hull Scantling Calculation"]
    S10 --> S12["Classification Society Approval"]
```

### 4.3 Ductile vs Brittle Behaviour (Comparative Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DUCT["DUCTILE MATERIAL e.g. Mild Steel AH36"]
        D1["Long elastic region"]
        D2["Distinct yield point"]
        D3["Large plastic deformation"]
        D4["High toughness"]
        D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4
    end
    subgraph BRIT["BRITTLE MATERIAL e.g. Cast Iron"]
        B1["Short or no plastic region"]
        B2["No distinct yield point"]
        B3["Sudden fracture"]
        B4["Low toughness"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4
    end
    DUCT -. Warning signs visible. .-> BRIT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** When asked to *compare ductile and brittle materials*, the examiner looks for **four** key differences: (i) presence/absence of yield point, (ii) amount of plastic deformation, (iii) area under the curve (toughness), and (iv) fracture mode. Skipping any one of these costs a sub-mark.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the following terms with suitable units: (i) Stress, (ii) Strain, (iii) Young's Modulus.*

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Stress** is the internal restoring force per unit area developed inside a body when subjected to external loads. It is given by $\sigma = F/A$. The SI unit is the Pascal (Pa) or N/m², with practical ship-design values expressed in MPa or GPa.

(ii) **Strain** is the ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension. It is given by $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$. Strain is a **dimensionless** quantity (no units).

(iii) **Young's Modulus (E)** is the slope of the stress-strain curve within the proportional (elastic) region. It represents the stiffness of the material and is given by $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$. Its unit is the same as stress, i.e., Pa, with values in GPa for metals.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Poisson's Ratio? State its typical value for ship-building steel.*

**Model Answer:**

**Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain when a material is subjected to uniaxial loading within the elastic limit.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

It is a dimensionless quantity. For ship-building mild steel, the typical value of Poisson's Ratio is **0.30**, and for marine-grade aluminium alloys it is approximately **0.33**. Poisson's Ratio lies between 0 and 0.5 for most engineering materials, with 0.5 representing an incompressible material.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Format)

> **KTU 2024 Note:** Each 14-mark question typically has two sub-parts (7 + 7 marks). The model answers below are written in the same step-by-step format that a topper would use.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Paper]**

**(a) Draw a fully labelled engineering stress–strain diagram for a ductile material such as mild steel. Mark and explain the significance of the following points: (i) Proportional Limit, (ii) Elastic Limit, (iii) Upper and Lower Yield Points, (iv) Ultimate Stress Point, (v) Fracture Point. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The stress-strain diagram for a ductile material (mild steel) is drawn below (see SECTION 4.1 for the Mermaid block representation of the same).

The significance of each point is explained as follows:

**(i) Proportional Limit (Point A):**
This is the maximum stress up to which the stress is directly proportional to the strain. Hooke's Law ($\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$) is valid up to this point. The slope of the line from the origin to this point gives Young's Modulus E.

**(ii) Elastic Limit (Point B):**
This is the maximum stress up to which the material regains its original shape and size completely upon removal of the load. No permanent deformation is induced below this point. For ship-building steel, the elastic and proportional limits are nearly coincident.

**(iii) Upper and Lower Yield points (Points C and C′):**
The **upper yield point (C)** is the stress at which plastic deformation begins without any noticeable increase in stress. The stress then drops slightly to a **lower yield point (C′)**, after which deformation continues at a nearly constant stress — this is the **yield plateau** associated with **Lüders band propagation** in mild steel. This is the most critical design value for ship structures.

**(iv) Ultimate Stress Point (Point D):**
This is the maximum stress the material can withstand, computed on the original cross-sectional area. After this, **necking** begins — the cross-section reduces locally and true stress continues to rise even as engineering stress falls.

**(v) Fracture Point (Point E):**
The point at which the specimen actually breaks. The elongation at fracture is a measure of the material's **ductility**.

---

**(b) A mild steel specimen of diameter 20 mm and gauge length 50 mm is tested in tension. The specimen yields at a load of 78.5 kN and fractures at a load of 110 kN. Given the elongation at fracture is 12 mm and reduction in area is 40%, calculate: (i) Yield Stress, (ii) Ultimate Tensile Stress, (iii) Percentage Elongation, (iv) Percentage Reduction in Area. Comment on the ductility of the material. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- Original diameter $d_0 = 20$ mm = 0.020 m
- Gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm
- Yield load $F_y = 78.5$ kN = 78,500 N
- Fracture (ultimate) load $F_u = 110$ kN = 110,000 N
- Elongation at fracture $\Delta L_f = 12$ mm
- Reduction in area = 40%

**Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d_0^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.020)^2 = 3.142 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Yield Stress:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{78{,}500}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.498 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Ultimate Tensile Stress:**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{F_u}{A_0} = \frac{110{,}000}{3.142 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.501 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} \approx 350 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Percentage Elongation:**

$$\% \text{ Elongation} = \frac{\Delta L_f}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{12}{50} \times 100 = 24\%$$

**(iv) Percentage Reduction in Area:**

$$\% RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = 40\% \text{ (given)}$$

**Comment on Ductility:**
With 24% elongation at fracture and 40% reduction in area, the material is **highly ductile**. It can undergo significant plastic deformation before fracture, making it suitable for ship hull construction where energy absorption (toughness) is required during extreme wave loading.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Alternative]**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question Paper]**

**(a) Define Hooke's Law. Derive the relationship $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ between Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$). State typical values for mild steel. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Hooke's Law (Definition):**
Within the elastic limit, the stress in a material is directly proportional to the strain produced.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where **E** is the constant of proportionality called **Young's Modulus of Elasticity**.

**Derivation of $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$:**

Consider an elemental cubic block of side 'a' of an isotropic homogeneous material. Apply a tensile stress $\sigma_x$ on the faces perpendicular to the x-axis.

**Step 1 — Strains in x, y, z directions:**
From Hooke's Law in 3D (generalized form):
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$$
With $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$:
$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}$$
$$\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Distortion due to pure shear:**
Apply a pure shear stress $\tau$ on the same cube (no normal stress). The shear strain is:
$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G}$$

**Step 3 — Shear as a combination of tension and compression:**
By stress transformation, a pure shear state is equivalent to principal stresses $\sigma_1 = +\tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ acting at 45°. So the normal strain along the principal direction is:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2) = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 4 — Shear strain as change in angle:**
The shear strain $\gamma$ is the change in the right angle between the two faces, which by geometry equals:
$$\gamma = 2\varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

**Step 5 — Equating with Hooke's shear law $\tau = G\gamma$:**
$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma} = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

**Typical Values for Mild Steel:**
- E = 200 GPa
- G = 80 GPa (approximately)
- $\nu = 0.30$

Verification: $E = 2 \times 80 \times (1 + 0.30) = 208$ GPa ≈ 200 GPa ✓

---

**(b) A 1.5 m long steel rod of 30 mm diameter is subjected to an axial tensile load of 90 kN. If E = 200 GPa and $\nu = 0.30$, find: (i) Stress, (ii) Strain, (iii) Elongation, (iv) Change in diameter. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.030)^2 = 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Stress:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{90{,}000}{7.069 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.273 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa} = 127.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Strain:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{127.3 \times 10^{6}}{200 \times 10^{9}} = 6.365 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(iii) Elongation:**

$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = 6.365 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.5 = 9.55 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.955 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Change in Diameter:**

Lateral strain:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -0.30 \times 6.365 \times 10^{-4} = -1.909 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in diameter:

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -1.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = -5.73 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} \approx -5.73 \, \mu\text{m}$$

The diameter reduces by approximately 5.73 micrometres under the tensile load.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Stress-Strain Diagram Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Omission:** Writing "stress = 250" without "MPa" or "N/mm²" — KTU examiners deduct **at least 0.5 to 1 mark** per missing unit in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing True and Engineering Stress:** Engineering stress uses original area $A_0$; true stress uses instantaneous area $A_f$. Mixing them up in a 14-mark numerical is the **single biggest mark-loss trap**.
> 3. **Hooke's Law Application Outside Elastic Limit:** Hooke's Law is valid *only* up to the proportionality limit. Applying $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ at the yield or ultimate point is a **fatal conceptual error**.
> 4. **Poisson's Ratio Sign:** Lateral strain under tension is *negative* (contraction). Writing a positive value for $\varepsilon_{lat}$ will lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing the Diagram:** For 7-mark "draw and explain" questions, *no diagram* (or an unlabelled diagram) typically fetches **only 1–2 marks out of 7**. Always draw, label every point, and write the explanation alongside.
> 6. **Forgetting % in Percentage Elongation/RA:** A value of "24" instead of "24%" loses 0.5 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Stress** = internal force per unit area, in **Pa** (or MPa / GPa in practice).
- **Strain** = change in length per unit original length; **dimensionless**.
- **Hooke's Law** $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ is valid **only up to the proportional limit**.
- **Young's Modulus (E)** = slope of the linear elastic portion of the stress-strain curve; a measure of **stiffness** (not strength).
- **Yield Stress** = the design strength; ship steels like AH36 use $\sigma_y \geq 355$ MPa.
- **Ultimate Stress** = maximum engineering stress; beyond this, necking begins.
- **Fracture Point** = the point of rupture; elongation at fracture measures **ductility**.
- **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** ≈ 0.30 for steels, 0.33 for marine aluminiums; dimensionless; ranges 0 to 0.5.
- **Shear Modulus G** related to E and $\nu$ by $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$.
- **Toughness** = area under the entire stress-strain curve = ability to absorb energy.
- **Resilience** = area under the elastic portion only = recoverable strain energy.
- **True stress** = $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$; true strain = $\ln(1 + \varepsilon_{eng})$.
- **Strain energy density** $U = \sigma^2 / (2E)$ — vital for impact and slamming load analysis on ship plating.
- **Factor of Safety** = $\sigma_{yield} / \sigma_{working}$; KTU typically uses values between **1.5 and 3.0** for ship primary structure.
- For **ductile vs brittle** comparison, always mention: (1) yield point, (2) plastic region size, (3) toughness, (4) fracture mode.
- **Always** include units, draw diagrams, and state the underlying assumption (Hooke's Law validity, uniform stress, original area, etc.) in KTU answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
