# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a Classification Society and list any four of its major functions.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Classification Society** is an independent, non-governmental technical organization that establishes and applies engineering Rules for the design, construction, and survey of ships, with the objective of verifying their structural integrity, safety, and seaworthiness throughout their operational life.

**Four major functions:**
1. **Rule Development** – Publication of technical Class Rules for hull, machinery, and electrical systems
2. **Design Approval** – Review and approval of ship plans, drawings, and technical documentation
3. **Construction Survey** – Continuous on-site surveillance during shipbuilding
4. **In-Service Surveys** – Periodic classification surveys (Annual, Intermediate, Special) during operational life
5. **Class Register Maintenance** – Keeping a public register of classed vessels

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for Students:** Writing 4 functions in 3-mark questions is the safe minimum; always write **5 if you remember** to be safe against partial marking.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is IACS? State any two of its objectives.**

**Model Answer:**

**IACS (International Association of Classification Societies)** is the international body founded in **1968 in Hamburg, Germany**, comprising the world's leading classification societies. It currently has its permanent secretariat in **London, UK**.

**Two main objectives:**
1. To promote the **highest possible standards** in maritime safety and environmental protection through the development of **Unified Requirements (URs)** and harmonized class rules
2. To establish a **Quality System Certification Scheme (QSCS)** to ensure member societies maintain ISO 9001-level quality in their services
3. To represent member societies at the **International Maritime Organization (IMO)** and coordinate technical positions

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write *"IACS controls all classification societies"* — this is **incorrect**. IACS is an **association**, not a regulator. It coordinates and harmonizes but does not control individual society autonomy.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **role and functions of a classification society** in the maritime industry. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **various surveys conducted on a ship in service** to maintain its classification. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Introduction:**
A classification society is a private, non-governmental, international body that provides independent technical services to shipowners, shipyards, and flag states. Examples include **Lloyd's Register (LR), DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas (BV), ClassNK**, and others. They are united under the **International Association of Classification Societies (IACS)**.

**Detailed Functions:**

**1. Rule Development:**
- Each society develops **Class Rules** governing the design, materials, construction, and maintenance of ships
- Rules are continuously updated based on **R&D, casualty data, and emerging technologies**
- Rules exceed 500 volumes in mature societies

**2. Design Approval:**
- Review of **lines plan, general arrangement, midship section, structural drawings, machinery layouts, and stability booklets**
- Approval ensures the design complies with Rules before construction begins

**3. Construction Survey:**
- Continuous surveyor presence at the shipyard
- Verifies **steel quality, welding procedures, NDT, alignment, and installation quality**
- Attendance at **sea trials** to verify vessel performance

**4. Issuance of Class Certificate:**
- A formal document issued upon successful completion of construction
- Confirms the vessel meets Class Rules
- Vessels without this certificate are considered **"unclassed"**

**5. In-Service Classification Surveys:**
- **Annual Surveys** – General inspection
- **Intermediate Surveys** – More detailed internal checks
- **Special Surveys (Class Renewal)** – Comprehensive 5-yearly survey
- **Docking Surveys** – Underwater hull inspection
- **Continuous Surveys** – Rolling programme
- **Occasional Surveys** – After damage or modification

**6. Maintenance of Class Register:**
- Public database of all classed vessels
- Tracks survey status, recommendations, suspensions, and withdrawals
- Accessible to insurers, charterers, and port authorities

**7. Delegated Statutory Work:**
- As a **Recognized Organization (RO)**, performs statutory surveys on behalf of Flag States
- Issues statutory certificates like **Load Line, SOLAS, MARPOL** on flag state delegation

**8. Casualty Investigation and Advisory:**
- Investigates maritime accidents
- Issues advisories to prevent recurrence
- Publishes **annual casualty and detention statistics**

**9. Research and Development:**
- Operates research arms (e.g., DNV Maritime Research)
- Develops new Rules for alternative fuels, autonomous ships, decarbonization

**Conclusion:**
Classification societies are the **backbone of maritime safety and quality assurance**, providing a globally trusted framework that protects life, property, and the marine environment.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Introduction:**
Once a vessel is in service, classification is maintained through a **systematic schedule of surveys** defined by **IACS Unified Requirements (URs Z1 to Z23)**. These surveys are designed to detect deterioration before it reaches unsafe levels.

**Detailed Description of Surveys:**

**1. Annual Survey (Every Year):**
- Conducted within **±3 months** of the anniversary date of the original Class Certificate
- External inspection of hull, machinery spaces, safety equipment
- Verification of certificates, logs, and operational records
- *Sketch element:* General visual inspection of deck, superstructure, hatch covers, machinery

**2. Intermediate Survey (Between 2nd and 3rd Annual):**
- More detailed internal inspection
- Includes **thickness measurements** of corrosion-prone areas
- Examination of **ballast tanks, cargo holds, machinery foundations**
- *Sketch element:* Thickness measurement using UT gauge on critical structural members

**3. Special Survey / Class Renewal (Every 5 Years):**
- The **most comprehensive survey**, essentially equivalent to rebuilding inspection
- Includes:
  - **Complete internal examination** of all tanks, holds, and spaces
  - **Extensive thickness measurement** as per UR Z7
  - **NDT of critical structures**
  - **Machinery overhaul and testing**
  - **Sea trials** in some cases
- Failure to pass results in **class suspension or withdrawal**

**4. Docking Survey:**
- **At least twice in 5 years**
- Hull inspected in **dry dock** (or in-water survey if approved)
- Propeller, rudder, sea valves, and underwater fittings examined

**5. Propeller Shaft Survey:**
- Every **5 years** (or as per maker's instructions)
- Shaft withdrawn, bearings inspected, oil samples analyzed

**6. Boiler Survey:**
- Internal examination of boilers
- Frequency depends on boiler type and age

**7. Continuous Survey:**
- Alternative to fixed-interval surveys
- All items surveyed in a **rolling 5-year cycle**
- Reduces vessel downtime by spreading survey workload

**8. Occasional Survey:**
- After **damage, grounding, collision, fire, or major modification**
- Class must verify the vessel remains fit for service

**Neat Sketch of Survey Cycle:**

```
       Year 1         Year 2    Year 2.5      Year 3        Year 4         Year 5
        │              │          │            │             │              │
        ▼              ▼          ▼            ▼             ▼              ▼
    ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐   ┌────────┐   ┌──────┐      ┌──────┐     ┌──────────┐
    │  AS  │      │  AS  │   │   IS   │   │  AS  │      │  AS  │     │    SS    │
    │      │ ───► │      │ ─►│        │─►│      │ ───► │      │ ──► │ (Class   │
    └──────┘      └──────┘   └────────┘   └──────┘      └──────┘     │ Renewal) │
                                                                      └──────────┘
                                                                            │
                                                                            ▼
                                                                    (Cycle Repeats)
```

**Conclusion:**
The in-service survey system ensures **continuous oversight** of the vessel, allowing class societies to maintain their approval throughout the ship's life, typically **20–30 years**, and provide assurance to owners, insurers, and flag states.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Differentiate clearly between a **Flag State** and a **Classification Society**, highlighting their roles, document types, and jurisdictional boundaries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is **Class Notation**? Taking Lloyd's Register as an example, explain the meaning of a typical class notation with all its components. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Introduction:**
The maritime regulatory framework rests on a **dual-pillar system**: the **Flag State** (legal/jurisdictional) and the **Classification Society** (technical/engineering). Understanding their differences is essential for every marine engineer and naval architect.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Flag State | Classification Society |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | National government authority (e.g., Panama Maritime Authority, Liberia Registry) | Private, independent, non-governmental body (e.g., LR, DNV) |
| **Primary Concern** | Compliance with international conventions (IMO, SOLAS, MARPOL, MLC) | Compliance with proprietary Class Rules (hull, machinery integrity) |
| **Mandatory?** | Yes – vessel cannot legally operate without flag registration | Voluntary – but commercially essential for insurance and chartering |
| **Jurisdiction** | Limited to vessels registered under that flag, but enforced globally | Worldwide, based on contract with owner |
| **Documents Issued** | Flag Certificate, Minimum Safe Manning Doc, Continuous Synopsis Record | Class Certificate, Class Register Entry, Survey Reports |
| **Statutory Surveys** | Performs or delegates to Recognized Organizations (ROs) | Acts as RO when authorized by Flag State |
| **Example Authorities** | Panama, Liberia, Marshall Islands, Singapore, India (DG Shipping) | Lloyd's Register, DNV, ABS, Bureau Veritas, ClassNK |
| **Examples of Certificates** | Load Line Certificate, Safety Construction Certificate, MARPOL Certificates | Class Certificate, Hull & Machinery Certificate, ESP Compliance |
| **Enforcement Body** | Maritime Administration of the flag state, Coast Guard | IACS coordination; no direct enforcement power |
| **Penalty for Non-Compliance** | Detention, deregistration, criminal liability of master/owner | Class suspension, withdrawal, public deregistration |

**Conclusion:**
The **Flag State is the legal authority** ensuring convention compliance, while the **Classification Society is the technical authority** ensuring engineering integrity. In modern shipping, they work hand-in-hand through the **RO delegation mechanism**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Definition of Class Notation:**
A **Class Notation** is an **alphanumeric symbolic code** assigned by a classification society to a vessel, describing its **design features, service area, structural enhancements, machinery characteristics, and survey status**. It serves as the **"identity card" of a ship's compliance level**.

**Example (Lloyd's Register):**

$$\text{100 A 1} \cdot \text{ ESP } \cdot \text{ Bulk Carrier ESP } \cdot \text{ CSR BC-A } \cdot \text{ Unrestricted Navigation}$$

**Component-by-Component Explanation:**

| Component | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **100 A 1** | **Main Class Symbol** – "100" indicates 100% compliance with LR Hull Rules; "A" denotes the ship is fitted with approved anchors and chain cables; "1" indicates the vessel is well-found, properly equipped, and the cargo hold arrangements are satisfactory |
| **ESP** | **Enhanced Survey Programme** – Applicable to bulk carriers and tankers from age 5 years onwards as per IACS UR Z10 / Z11 |
| **Bulk Carrier ESP** | **Service Notation** – Identifies the vessel's primary purpose; designed and built as a bulk carrier |
| **CSR BC-A** | **Common Structural Rules – Bulk Carrier Category A** – BC-A applies to vessels with all cargo holds suitable for dry cargo; indicates compliance with IACS Common Structural Rules |
| **Unrestricted Navigation** | **Navigation Notation** – The vessel is permitted to operate in all waters worldwide without geographic restriction |
| **(Optional: IWS)** | **In-Water Survey** – Approved for underwater inspection by divers in lieu of dry-docking (under specific conditions) |
| **(Optional: ✠HMC)** | **Hull & Machinery Certification notation** (varies by society) |

**Other Examples (Cross-Society Notation Comparison):**

| Society | Symbol for 100% Hull | Typical Notation |
|---|---|---|
| **Lloyd's Register** | 100 A 1 | 100A1 ESP Bulk Carrier CSR BC-A |
| **DNV** | ✠1A1 | ✠1A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |
| **ABS** | ✠A1 | ✠A1 Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |
| **Bureau Veritas** | ✠HULL | ✠HULL ✠MACH Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |
| **ClassNK** | NS | NS (MNS) Bulk Carrier ESP CSR BC-A |

**Importance of Class Notation:**
- **Insurance underwriters** use it to set Hull & Machinery premiums
- **Charterers** require specific notations before loading certain cargoes
- **Port State Control** uses it to assess vessel condition during inspections
- **Owners** use it for marketing the vessel to potential charterers

**Conclusion:**
Class notation is a **compact, internationally understood summary** of a ship's engineering pedigree, and it is the single most important technical identifier for a vessel after its IMO number.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Marks-Loss Areas in Classification Society Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing IACS with IMO** — IACS coordinates classification societies; **IMO is the United Nations body** for international maritime regulations. These are **two different organizations**.
>
> 2. **Writing "Classification is mandatory"** — Classification is **VOLUNTARY** in principle (Flag State registration is mandatory; Class is private). However, **commercial necessity makes it effectively mandatory**.
>
> 3. **Not naming the Class Society's country of origin** — Examiners often give **partial credit** for writing the founding year and country (e.g., "Lloyd's Register, 1760, UK").
>
> 4. **Skipping the Survey Cycle diagram** — In 7-mark sub-questions on surveys, **a labelled timeline diagram** is worth 2–3 marks by itself. Always draw one.
>
> 5. **Mixing Class Certificate with Statutory Certificate** — Class Certificate is voluntary and owned by the society; Statutory Certificates are mandatory and owned by the Flag State. This distinction is **frequently tested**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting IACS QSCS** — Many students miss that IACS runs a **Quality System Certification Scheme** to maintain ISO 9001 standards among members.
>
> 7. **Not explaining "Out of Class" consequences** — Examiners want to see that you know the **commercial, insurance, and legal consequences** of class withdrawal.

---

## 5.4 📝 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### ✅ Core Concepts to Remember

- [x] **Classification Society** = Independent, private, technical body that ensures ship integrity per its own Rules
- [x] **IACS** = International Association of Classification Societies, founded **1968 in Hamburg**, HQ in **London**
- [x] **Lloyd's Register (1760)** is the **oldest** classification society in the world
- [x] **Class Rules** are proprietary technical standards; **URs** are IACS-harmonized mandatory requirements
- [x] **Class Notation** is the symbolic code describing a vessel's design, service, and survey status
- [x] **Class Certificate** is issued at delivery; renewed every 5 years via **Special Survey**
- [x] **Annual Survey** = Every year (±3 months); **Intermediate Survey** = Between 2nd & 3rd Annual; **Special Survey** = Every 5 years
- [x] **Docking Survey** is required at least **twice in 5 years**
- [x] **Flag State** = Legal/Jurisdictional authority (mandatory); **Class Society** = Technical authority (voluntary but essential)
- [x] **Recognized Organization (RO)** = Class society authorized to act on behalf of a flag state
- [x] **ESP** = Enhanced Survey Programme (mandatory for tankers/bulkers from age 5)
- [x] **CSR** = Common Structural Rules (IACS harmonized structural standards for tankers & bulkers)
- [x] **"Out of Class"** = Vessel has lost its class; insurance may be void, PSC may detain
- [x] **Class is withdrawn** if recommendations are not completed within the deadline
- [x] **QSCS** = IACS Quality System Certification Scheme (ISO 9001-based)

### ✅ Numerical / Standard Values to Memorize

- [x] IACS Special Survey cycle: **5 years**
- [x] Annual Survey window: **±3 months**
- [x] Dry-dock interval: max **30 months** between dockings (in-water survey allowed as alternative)
- [x] ESP starts at vessel age: **5 years**
- [x] CSR threshold: **Bulkers ≥ 90 m LOA**, **Tankers ≥ 150 m LOA**
- [x] Number of IACS members: **12 full + 1 associate**
- [x] IACS founded: **1968**

### ✅ Common Acronyms

- [x] **IACS** – International Association of Classification Societies
- [x] **UR** – Unified Requirement
- [x] **UI** – Unified Interpretation
- [x] **ESP** – Enhanced Survey Programme
- [x] **CSR** – Common Structural Rules
- [x] **RO** – Recognized Organization
- [x] **QSCS** – Quality System Certification Scheme
- [x] **AS / IS / SS** – Annual / Intermediate / Special Survey
- [x] **NDT** – Non-Destructive Testing
- [x] **P&I** – Protection and Indemnity (Insurance)

### ✅ Sample 3-Mark Question Frameworks (For Last-Minute Revision)

- *"Define classification society."* → Definition + 2–3 key functions
- *"List IACS and state 2 objectives."* → Year, place, 2 objectives
- *"What is class notation?"* → Definition + one example with explanation
- *"Name 4 classification societies."* → LR, DNV, ABS, BV (+ year/country = bonus)
- *"What is Special Survey?"* → 5-yearly, comprehensive, condition for class renewal

---

> **END OF MODULE 1 — CLASSIFICATION SOCIETIES**

topics.title: Longitudinal and Transverse Strength, Framing System
Basic Structural Components - Primary and Secondary Stiffeners
Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are "Functions" in Ship Structural Engineering?

In the discipline of **Basic Ship Structures**, the term *"Functions"* refers to the **specific structural roles, load-resisting duties, and operational responsibilities** assigned to each individual component of a ship's hull girder system. Every plate, stiffener, girder, bulkhead, and deck in a vessel is not a decorative element — it is a **load-bearing member** with a definite, engineered purpose.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Functions of ship structural elements* are the **mechanical, hydrodynamic, and operational duties** that each component performs — such as resisting bending, shearing, buckling, racking, slamming, and providing watertight integrity — thereby ensuring the vessel maintains its structural shape, strength, and seaworthiness under all expected service conditions.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Human Skeleton

Imagine the human body:
- **Bones (Hull plating)** → Hold the body's shape
- **Ribs (Frames)** → Protect vital organs and give the chest its curved form
- **Spine (Keel + centerline girder)** → The main load-bearing axis that lets you bend and twist
- **Skull (Stem & Stern frame)** → The hardened leading and trailing edges
- **Joints (Brackets & knees)** → Where two bones meet, transferring load smoothly
- **Skin (Outer plating)** → Keeps the inside in and the outside out

A ship is essentially a **hollow, floating skeleton** made of steel, and every member has a job description — just like a hospital where the heart surgeon, the nurse, and the pharmacist each have distinct functions. If you mix up their roles, the system fails.

### 1.3 The Six Primary Functions of Any Ship Structure

Every ship structural element performs **one or more** of these six universal duties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Six Cardinal Functions of Ship Structures:**
> 1. **Resist External Loads** — hydrostatic, hydrodynamic, wave, cargo, and impact loads.
> 2. **Maintain Geometric Shape** — preserve hull form against distortion (racking, panting, torsion).
> 3. **Provide Watertight/Weathertight Integrity** — subdivision via bulkheads; prevent flooding.
> 4. **Transfer Loads to the Hull Girder** — distribute local loads (e.g., from a propeller shaft) up to the global bending structure.
> 5. **Support Operational Systems** — carry machinery, cargo, deck equipment, superstructure weight.
> 6. **Ensure Safety & Survival** — crashworthiness, fire integrity, redundant load paths in damaged condition.

### 1.4 Functions of the Principal Ship Structural Components

| # | Component | Primary Function(s) |
|---|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Keel** | Longitudinal backbone; resists hogging/sagging bending; attachment for garboards |
| 2 | **Stem** | Forms the bow entry; resists slamming & bow-wave impact |
| 3 | **Stern Frame / Sternpost** | Supports rudder & propeller; resists hydrostatic loads at the transom |
| 4 | **Hull Plating (Bottom, Side, Deck)** | Forms watertight envelope; participates in hull-girder bending |
| 5 | **Frames (Transverse ribs)** | Maintain hull cross-section; resist local hydrostatic pressure |
| 6 | **Girders (Longitudinal)** | Support frames; carry longitudinal bending loads |
| 7 | **Decks** | Resist vertical loads (cargo, equipment); contribute to hull-girder strength |
| 8 | **Bulkheads** | Subdivide the ship; preserve buoyancy after flooding |
| 9 | **Superstructure** | House crew/equipment; contributes to shear & bending stiffness |
| 10 | **Stringers & Pillars** | Reduce unsupported span of girders; transfer vertical loads to bottom |
| 11 | **Bilge Keel** | Damps roll motion; minor structural stiffening |
| 12 | **Hatch Covers** | Weather-seal cargo holds; contribute to deck strength |

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025\ t/m^3}$ (1025 kg/m³)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$
> - **Density of fresh water:** $\rho_{fw} = \mathbf{1.000\ t/m^3}$
> - **Young's Modulus of ship steel:** $E = \mathbf{2.06 \times 10^{5}\ N/mm^2}$
> - **Yield stress of mild ship steel (Grade A):** $\sigma_y = \mathbf{235\ MPa}$
> - **Permissible bending stress (Classification Societies):** $\sigma_{perm} \approx \mathbf{175\ MPa}$ (≈ 0.6 × σ_y for normal seagoing)
> - **Rule wave bending moment coefficient:** $f = \mathbf{0.9}$ to $\mathbf{1.1}$ (depending on L and class society)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Hierarchy of a Ship Structure

A ship structure can be visualized as a **three-tier load path hierarchy**:

```
TIER 1 — GLOBAL / HULL GIRDER LEVEL
   (Resists total bending & shearing forces of the entire vessel)
                ↑ loads transferred upward
TIER 2 — SUBSTRUCTURE / PRIMARY LEVEL
   (Resists local panel loads, transmits them to the hull girder)
                ↑ loads transferred upward
TIER 3 — LOCAL / SECONDARY LEVEL
   (Plates & stiffeners resist direct hydrostatic, cargo, equipment pressure)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this hierarchy matters for KTU exams:**
> The classification of a member's "function" is determined by **which tier** it belongs to. For instance, a **bottom shell plate** is *simultaneously* a Tier-3 local member (it resists sea pressure directly) **and** a Tier-1 hull-girder participant (its effective breadth contributes to the section modulus).

### 2.2 Functional Breakdown of Each Major Structural Group

#### A. Functions of the **Keel Assembly**

- Acts as the **backbone** of the hull girder.
- Resists **longitudinal bending stress** (hogging & sagging).
- Provides the **line of reference** for the ship's design and construction (baseline).
- Anchors the **garboard strake** (first strake of bottom plating).
- In case of **dry-docking**, it bears the entire weight via the **docking blocks** (grounding loads).
- Prevents **broaching-to** loads from being concentrated at one point.

#### B. Functions of the **Hull Plating**

- Forms the **watertight envelope** (primary barrier against flooding).
- Participates in **hull-girder bending** as the outer flange of the I-section (with the strength deck as the top flange).
- Resists **local hydrostatic pressure** between stiffeners (plate bending).
- Resists **lateral pressure** (slamming on the bow, side-impact from berthing).

#### C. Functions of the **Frames**

- Maintain the **transverse shape** of the hull (resist racking).
- Support the **shell plating** against buckling under external pressure.
- Transfer **lateral pressure loads** from the shell to the decks/keel (acting like vertical beams).
- Provide **attachment points** for piping, cables, insulation.
- In tanker/tug designs, may form **web frames** for very high local loads.

#### D. Functions of the **Girders** (Longitudinal)

- Support frames at intermediate points, reducing their **unsupported span**.
- Carry **longitudinal bending loads** in conjunction with shell and deck plating.
- In tankers, **double-bottom girders** also serve as **limber holes** for oil drainage and structural stiffening.

#### E. Functions of the **Decks**

- **Strength deck** (usually the uppermost continuous deck) acts as the **top flange** of the hull girder.
- Resists **vertical loads** from cargo, equipment, crew, sea spray.
- Provides **transverse shear transfer** between port & starboard sides.
- Acts as a **weather-tight** barrier.
- In warships, the deck is the **armor deck** for protection.

#### F. Functions of the **Bulkheads**

- **Watertight bulkheads** → subdivide the hull to limit flooding after hull breach (survivability).
- **Collision bulkhead** (forward) → first line of defense against forward impact.
- **Engine-room bulkhead** → separates machinery spaces from cargo.
- **Aft peak bulkhead** → supports shaft tunnel, isolates steering gear.
- **Swash bulkheads** (partial) → reduce free-surface effect of liquids in tanks.
- **Transverse strength bulkheads** → contribute to hull-girder shear capacity.

#### G. Functions of the **Superstructure**

- Houses the **bridge, accommodation, machinery casings**.
- Adds **shear stiffness** to the deck region (especially in still water & minor seaways).
- Provides **additional buoyancy reserve** in extreme bow-down conditions (rare contribution).
- However, in heavy weather, its **discontinuity** with the strength deck creates **shear stress concentration** at the corners — hence the need for **rounded sheer strake** and **tripping brackets**.

#### H. Functions of **Stringers and Pillars**

- **Stringers** are horizontal stiffeners on the side or bulkhead that reduce the **unsupported span** of vertical frames/girders.
- **Pillars / Stanchions** are vertical steel tubes that **transfer deck loads down to the bottom shell**, preventing the deck and bottom from "pinching" together under heavy deck cargo.
- Together they constitute the **transverse load path** of the ship.

#### I. Functions of the **Stem and Stern Frame**

- **Stem**: forms the **sharp bow entry** to cut through waves; resists **slamming forces** (sudden impact pressures up to ~350 kPa for a bulbous bow ship).
- **Sternpost / Stern frame**: supports the **propeller and rudder**; transmits propeller thrust into the hull; resists **stern tube bearing reactions**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every formula a KTU 2024 student must know for "Functions of ship structures." Use **\vert** (not \vert — see column rule) for absolute-value bars to preserve table rendering.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Function |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | $p = \rho \, g \, h$ | Pressure acting on shell — function of **plating & frames** |
| 2 | Buoyancy force | $F_b = \rho \, g \, \nabla$ | Function of **overall hull volume $\nabla$** |
| 3 | Rule wave bending moment (still-water + wave) | $M_w = f \, C_w \, L^2 \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)$ kN·m | Function of **hull girder (keel + strength deck)** |
| 4 | Rule wave shear force | $F_w = f \, C_f \, L \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)$ kN | Function of **side & deck plating + bulkheads** |
| 5 | Section modulus of hull girder | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ (m³) | Quantifies **bending resistance function** |
| 6 | Bending stress | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Must be $\le \sigma_{perm}$ |
| 7 | Shear stress | $\tau = \dfrac{V \, Q}{I \, t}$ | Function of **side shell, bulkheads, deck plating** |
| 8 | Plate panel bending stress (simply supported) | $\sigma_{plate} = \dfrac{k \, p \, a^2}{t^2}$ | Function of **plating between stiffeners** |
| 9 | Frame section modulus required | $Z_{req} = \dfrac{p \, s \, \ell^2}{m \, \sigma_{perm}}$ | Function of **transverse frames** |
| 10 | Critical buckling stress (plate) | $\sigma_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E}{12(1-\nu^2)} \left(\dfrac{t}{a}\right)^2 k$ | Function of **plating against collapse** |
| 11 | Pillar load capacity (Euler) | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E I}{L_e^2}$ | Function of **stanchions/pillars** |
| 12 | Shear area of hull girder | $A_s$ (≈ projected side area) | Function of **side shell plating** |
| 13 | Docking load per block | $F_{block} = \dfrac{W_{light}}{N_{blocks}}$ | Function of **keel & bilge plating** |
| 14 | Still-water bending moment (trochoidal wave approx.) | $M_{sw} = \pm \, \rho \, g \, B \, L^3 / 24$ (rough) | Baseline for **keel function** |

Where:
- $L$ = Length between perpendiculars (m)
- $B$ = Breadth moulded (m)
- $C_B$ = Block coefficient (≈ 0.60–0.85)
- $\nabla$ = Volume of displacement (m³)
- $a$ = Plate panel short side (mm)
- $t$ = Plate thickness (mm)
- $s$ = Frame spacing (m)
- $\ell$ = Frame span between supports (m)
- $m$ = Permissible bending stress factor
- $k$ = Plate buckling coefficient (≈ 4 for simply supported)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio ≈ **0.3**
- $L_e$ = Effective pillar length
- $N_{blocks}$ = Number of docking blocks

### 2.4 Real-World Utility — Why Engineers Study Functions

In ship design offices (e.g., Hyundai Heavy Industries, Cochin Shipyard), the **first step** in any new vessel design is to list the **functions** each structural element will perform. This drives:

- **Plate thickness selection** (more functions → thicker plate)
- **Stiffener spacing** (load-sharing functions → closer spacing)
- **Material grade** (high-tensile steel for members performing multiple functions, e.g., sheer strake)
- **Class society approval** (DNV, Lloyd's, ABS, IRS all classify members by their function in their rule books)

> [!NOTE]
> A **sheer strake** performs **four functions simultaneously** — sheer resistance, gunwale longitudinal strength, side-shell watertight envelope, and attachment for bulwarks — which is why it is the **thickest plate** in the side shell.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Function of the Keel in Resisting Hogging/Sagging Bending

**Context:** A ship in a seaway bends like a beam. In a **hogging** condition (bow & stern supported by wave crests, amidships in a trough), the **keel experiences TENSION**. In **sagging** (amidships supported by wave crest), the **keel experiences COMPRESSION**.

**Starting point — Pure bending equation:**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M \, y}{I}
$$

where:
- $M$ = total bending moment at the section (N·m)
- $y$ = distance from neutral axis to the fibre in question (m)
- $I$ = second moment of area of the hull girder cross-section (m⁴)

**Step 1.** The hull girder is approximated as a **box girder** with:
- **Top flange** = strength deck + sheer strake + deck plating
- **Bottom flange** = keel plate + bottom shell plating + garboard strake
- **Webs (two sides)** = side shell plating

**Step 2.** The second moment of area for the box girder is (approximately, neglecting own second moment of flanges):

$$
I = \frac{B^2}{2} \, (A_{deck} \, \bar{y}_{deck} + A_{bottom} \, \bar{y}_{bottom})
$$

where $B$ is the breadth, $A$ is the area of the flange, $\bar{y}$ is the vertical distance from the neutral axis.

**Step 3.** Substitute into bending stress:

$$
\sigma_{keel} = \frac{M_{hog} \, \bar{y}_{bottom}}{I_{hull}}
$$

**Step 4.** Equivalently, using **section modulus** $Z = I / y_{max}$:

$$
\sigma_{keel} = \frac{M_{hog}}{Z_{hull}}
$$

**Step 5.** For a typical Panamax bulk carrier (L = 229 m, B = 32.2 m, D = 20 m, C_B = 0.82), class rule wave bending moment in hogging:

$$
M_{w,hog} = f \cdot C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7)
$$

Plugging typical $f = 1.0$, $C_w$ for L ≈ 229 m from DNV table ≈ **0.111**:

$$
M_{w,hog} = 1.0 \cdot 0.111 \cdot (229)^2 \cdot 32.2 \cdot (0.82 + 0.7)
$$

$$
M_{w,hog} = 0.111 \cdot 52441 \cdot 32.2 \cdot 1.52
$$

$$
M_{w,hog} \approx 0.111 \cdot 52441 \cdot 48.94 \approx 284{,}800 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 6.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{w,hog}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{284.8 \times 10^6 \text{ N·m}}{175 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2} \approx 1.627 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** The keel (with garboard + bottom shell) must contribute to the **1.627 m³** section modulus demand. This quantifies its **bending-resistance function**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Function of Transverse Frames Under External Hydrostatic Pressure

**Context:** A frame near the keel at depth $h$ from the waterline must support the shell plating between it and adjacent frames.

**Step 1.** Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$:

$$
p = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot h \quad \text{[Pa]}
$$

**Step 2.** Treat the frame as a **simply supported beam** spanning vertically between the keel (or bottom shell) and the lowest deck. Distributed load on the frame = pressure × frame spacing $s$:

$$
w = p \cdot s \quad \text{[N/m]}
$$

**Step 3.** Maximum bending moment for a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w \, \ell^2}{8} = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot \ell^2}{8}
$$

**Step 4.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{p \, s \, \ell^2}{8 \, \sigma_{perm}}
$$

**Step 5.** Numerical example — at $h = 10$ m draft, frame spacing $s = 0.6$ m, frame span $\ell = 12$ m, $\sigma_{perm} = 175$ MPa:

$$
p = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10 = 100{,}553 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
w = 100{,}553 \cdot 0.6 = 60{,}332 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
M_{max} = \frac{60{,}332 \cdot (12)^2}{8} = \frac{60{,}332 \cdot 144}{8} = 1{,}085{,}976 \text{ N·m}
$$

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{1{,}085{,}976}{175 \times 10^6} \approx 6.21 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 = 621 \text{ cm}^3
$$

This is the **functional demand** on each transverse frame.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Function of Pillars (Stanchions) Under Deck Load

**Step 1.** Vertical load on a pillar = tributary deck load:

$$
P = q_{deck} \cdot A_{trib}
$$

where $q_{deck}$ is the deck pressure (kN/m²) and $A_{trib}$ is the tributary area supported by the pillar.

**Step 2.** Critical Euler buckling load for a pin-ended pillar:

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \, E \, I}{L_e^2}
$$

**Step 3.** For safety, design load:

$$
P \le \frac{P_{cr}}{\text{Safety Factor (typically 2.0 to 2.5)}}
$$

**Step 4.** Example — a steel pipe pillar of OD = 200 mm, wall thickness = 12 mm, $L_e$ = 5 m, supporting a deck load of 25 kN/m² over an 18 m² area:

$$
P = 25 \cdot 18 = 450 \text{ kN}
$$

Moment of inertia of the pipe:

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 0.200^4 - 0.176^4 \right)
$$

$$
D_i = 0.200 - 2(0.012) = 0.176 \text{ m}
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64} \left( 0.0016 - 0.0009595 \right) = \frac{\pi}{64} \cdot 0.0006405 = 3.144 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{\pi^2 \cdot 2.06 \times 10^{11} \cdot 3.144 \times 10^{-5}}{(5)^2}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \cdot 2.06 \times 10^{11} \cdot 3.144 \times 10^{-5}}{25}
$$

$$
P_{cr} = \frac{9.8696 \cdot 6.477 \times 10^{6}}{25} \approx 2.557 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 2557 \text{ kN}
$$

Design load check (SF = 2.0):

$$
P_{allow} = \frac{2557}{2.0} \approx 1279 \text{ kN} \quad \gg \quad 450 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark
$$

The pillar comfortably performs its **vertical-load-transfer function**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Function ↔ Load Type ↔ Structural Member

| Function Required | Load Type | Member Performing It | KTU High-Yield Insight |
|-------------------|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Resist longitudinal bending | Hogging/sagging moment | Keel + strength deck + side shell | Forms top & bottom flange of hull girder |
| Resist transverse shear | Vertical shear force | Side shell plating + deck plating | Acts as webs of box girder |
| Resist local sea pressure | Hydrostatic pressure | Frames + shell plating | Plate-panel bending + frame bending |
| Subdivide for safety | Flooding water pressure | Bulkheads (watertight) | Limits progressive flooding |
| Support deck loads | Gravity (cargo, equip.) | Deck plating + deck girders + pillars | Vertical load path: deck → pillar → bottom |
| Maintain hull shape | Racking & torsion | Transverse frames + bulkheads | Triangulated box resists distortion |
| Resist bow impact | Slamming / berthing | Stem + bow shell + collision bulkhead | First energy-absorbing zone |
| Support propulsion | Propeller thrust | Stern frame + shaft bracket + bottom shell | Thrust is transmitted into the hull girder |
| Damp roll motion | Roll angular acceleration | Bilge keel | Adds hydrodynamic resistance at bilge |
| Support superstructure | Inertial loads in seaway | Deckhouse + bulkheads + brackets | Must be **shear-resistant**, not just light |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Load Path of a Ship Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1["External Loads<br/>Wave, Hydrostatic, Cargo, Impact"]
    L2["Local Tier 3<br/>Plates and Stiffeners<br/>Resist Direct Pressure"]
    L3["Primary Tier 2<br/>Frames, Girders, Decks<br/>Transfer Loads Upward"]
    L4["Global Tier 1<br/>Hull Girder<br/>Resists Bending and Shear"]

    F1["Function: Watertight Envelope<br/>Shell Plating"]
    F2["Function: Shape Retention<br/>Transverse Frames"]
    F3["Function: Load Transfer<br/>Longitudinal Girders"]
    F4["Function: Top Flange Bending<br/>Strength Deck"]
    F5["Function: Bottom Flange Bending<br/>Keel and Garboard"]
    F6["Function: Subdivision<br/>Watertight Bulkheads"]
    F7["Function: Vertical Load Path<br/>Pillars and Stringers"]

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4

    L2 --> F1
    L2 --> F2
    L3 --> F3
    L3 --> F4
    L4 --> F5
    L3 --> F6
    L3 --> F7

    F1 -.integrates with.-> F4
    F1 -.integrates with.-> F5
    F2 -.supports.-> F1
    F3 -.reinforces.-> F5
    F6 -.transfers.-> L4
    F7 -.shortens span of.-> F3
```

```mermaid

        WebR["RIGHT WEB<br/>Starboard Side Shell Plating<br/>Function: Resists Shear Force"]

    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
**List the four principal functions of a ship's keel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four principal functions of the keel are:

1. **Acts as the backbone** of the hull girder and resists longitudinal bending stresses (hogging and sagging).
2. **Provides the construction baseline** of reference for the entire hull during design and erection.
3. **Transmits docking loads** uniformly to the keel blocks when the ship is supported in dry dock.
4. **Anchors the garboard strake** and ensures the watertight integrity of the bottom shell.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often list only "backbone" — that is worth only 1 mark. You must name **at least four** distinct functions to score full 3 marks.

---

#### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP Set B]`
**Differentiate between the functions of watertight bulkheads and swash bulkheads.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Watertight Bulkhead | Swash Bulkhead |
|--------|---------------------|-----------------|
| **Primary Function** | Subdivide the hull to limit progressive flooding | Reduce free-surface effect of liquids in partially filled tanks |
| **Height** | Full depth from bottom to deck | Partial height (does not reach deck) |
| **Construction** | Heavily stiffened; tested for tightness | Lightly stiffened; not tested for tightness |
| **Effect on Stability** | Damage stability | Intact stability |
| **Examples** | Collision bulkhead, engine-room bulkhead | Bulkhead inside a ballast tank |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **functions of the various structural members** of a ship's hull girder system. Discuss how each member contributes to the **three-tier load path hierarchy** (global, primary, local). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bulk carrier has length $L = 180$ m, breadth $B = 28$ m, block coefficient $C_B = 0.80$, draft $T = 14$ m. Using the rule wave bending moment formula, compute the **design wave bending moment** (assume $f = 1.0$ and $C_w = 0.115$). If the permissible bending stress is **175 MPa**, determine the **required section modulus** $Z_{req}$. Comment on the **functional implication** for the strength deck and keel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A ship's hull is a complex assembly of plates, stiffeners, girders, decks, and bulkheads. Their functions fall into three tiers.

**Tier 3 — Local Members (2 marks):**
- **Shell plating** resists direct hydrostatic pressure.
- **Local stiffeners (frames)** support shell between transverse members.
- **Deck plating** resists direct cargo/equipment load.

**Tier 2 — Primary Members (2 marks):**
- **Transverse frames, web frames, longitudinal girders** transfer local loads to the hull girder.
- **Stringers** reduce frame spans; **pillars** transfer vertical deck loads to the bottom.

**Tier 1 — Global Members (1 mark):**
- **Hull girder** (top flange = strength deck; bottom flange = keel + garboard; webs = side shell) resists total bending moment $M_w$ and shear force $F_w$ over the entire ship length.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Every member's function is determined by which tier(s) it participates in. Members in multiple tiers (e.g., sheer strake, keel plate) are heavily designed.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Given: $L = 180$ m, $B = 28$ m, $C_B = 0.80$, $T = 14$ m, $f = 1.0$, $C_w = 0.115$, $\sigma_{perm} = 175$ MPa.

**Step 2.** Rule wave bending moment:

$$
M_w = f \cdot C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot (C_B + 0.7)
$$

$$
M_w = 1.0 \cdot 0.115 \cdot (180)^2 \cdot 28 \cdot (0.80 + 0.7)
$$

$$
M_w = 0.115 \cdot 32400 \cdot 28 \cdot 1.50
$$

$$
M_w = 0.115 \cdot 32400 \cdot 42 = 0.115 \cdot 1{,}360{,}800
$$

$$
M_w \approx 156{,}492 \text{ kN·m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 156.5 \text{ MN·m}
$$

**Step 3.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_w}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{156.5 \times 10^6}{175 \times 10^6} \approx 0.894 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4.** Functional implication (2 marks):
- **Strength deck** must act as the **top flange** of the hull girder and provide sufficient effective breadth × thickness to contribute to ≥ 40–50% of $Z_{req}$.
- **Keel and bottom shell** must provide the **bottom flange**, contributing the remaining section modulus.
- Together they must satisfy the **bending-resistance function** under the worst hogging/sagging wave.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to add **0.7** inside the bracket $(C_B + 0.7)$ — this is a **1-mark deduction**.
> 2. Confusing **MN·m vs kN·m** — write the units **everywhere**.
> 3. Not commenting on **functional implication** — a numerical answer alone is worth only **5 of 7 marks**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Main Paper]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the **functions of transverse frames, longitudinal girders, stringers, and pillars** in a typical merchant ship. Discuss how these members interact to maintain the hull's **shape under racking loads**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transverse frame is to be designed at the location where the shell depth below waterline is $h = 8$ m. Frame spacing is $s = 0.6$ m, frame span $\ell = 10$ m (assumed simply supported), and permissible bending stress is $\sigma_{perm} = 165$ MPa. Seawater density = 1025 kg/m³. Compute the **required section modulus** of the frame and discuss the **functional requirement** of the shell plating between frames. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Transverse Frames (2 marks):**
- Maintain transverse hull shape.
- Resist racking loads when the ship rolls.
- Support shell plating against local buckling.
- Transfer side pressure to decks/keel.

**Longitudinal Girders (1.5 marks):**
- Reduce unsupported span of frames.
- Participate in hull-girder longitudinal bending.
- Provide attachment for machinery foundations in some cases.

**Stringers (1.5 marks):**
- Horizontal members reducing vertical frame span.
- Located at the **turn of the bilge** and on bulkheads.
- Improve buckling resistance of the side shell.

**Pillars (1.5 marks):**
- Transfer vertical deck loads to the bottom shell.
- Prevent deck and bottom from "closing in."
- Critical in **bulk carriers** under heavy ore cargo.

**Racking interaction (0.5 marks):**
Under racking (transverse horizontal force during rolling), the hull acts as a **horizontal cantilever box beam**, and these four members together triangulate the box and maintain shape.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1.** Hydrostatic pressure at $h = 8$ m:

$$
p = \rho \, g \, h = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 8 = 80{,}442 \text{ Pa} \approx 80.44 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 2.** Distributed load on the frame:

$$
w = p \cdot s = 80{,}442 \cdot 0.6 = 48{,}265 \text{ N/m} \approx 48.27 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 3.** Maximum bending moment (simply supported, UDL):

$$
M_{max} = \frac{w \, \ell^2}{8} = \frac{48{,}265 \cdot (10)^2}{8} = \frac{48{,}265 \cdot 100}{8}
$$

$$
M_{max} = 603{,}313 \text{ N·m} \approx 603.3 \text{ kN·m}
$$

**Step 4.** Required section modulus:

$$
Z_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{perm}} = \frac{603{,}313}{165 \times 10^6} \approx 3.657 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
Z_{req} \approx 365.7 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 5.** Functional requirement of shell plating (1 mark):
The shell plating between frames functions as a **plate panel** simply supported on four edges (two frames + two longitudinal stiffeners). It must:
- Resist **plate bending** under $p$.
- Resist **buckling** (clamped edges, $k \approx 4$) with thickness ≥ $\sqrt{\dfrac{k \, p \, a^2}{12(1-\nu^2) \, \sigma_{cr}}}$.
- Contribute its **effective breadth** to the hull-girder bending function.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Using $g = 9.8$ instead of **9.81** — minor deduction but acceptable; using **10** is not.
> 2. Forgetting to convert **MPa → Pa** in denominator — common 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Not describing the shell plating function** — full marks require commentary, not just numbers.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Functions of Ship Structures**

- ✅ The **six cardinal functions** are: resist loads, maintain shape, watertight integrity, load transfer, support systems, ensure safety.
- ✅ Hull structure is a **three-tier hierarchy**: local (plates/stiffeners) → primary (frames/girders) → global (hull girder).
- ✅ The **keel** is the bottom flange of the hull girder; the **strength deck** is the top flange; **side shell** is the web.
- ✅ Rule wave bending moment: $\mathbf{M_w = f \, C_w \, L^2 \, B \, (C_B + 0.7)}$ — memorize this verbatim.
- ✅ Section modulus requirement: $\mathbf{Z_{req} = M / \sigma_{perm}}$.
- ✅ Hydrostatic pressure: $\mathbf{p = \rho \, g \, h}$ with $\mathbf{\rho_{sw} = 1025\ kg/m^3}$.
- ✅ Frame section modulus: $\mathbf{Z_{req} = p \, s \, \ell^2 / (8 \, \sigma_{perm})}$.
- ✅ Euler pillar load: $\mathbf{P_{cr} = \pi^2 E I / L_e^2}$.
- ✅ Bulkheads serve **damage stability** (flooding limitation); swash bulkheads serve **intact stability** (free-surface effect).
- ✅ Pillars + stringers form the **vertical load path**; frames + girders form the **transverse and longitudinal load paths**.
- ✅ The **sheer strake** performs multiple functions — always the **thickest plate** in the side shell.
- ✅ Always quote **units** in every numerical answer (kN, kN·m, m³, MPa).
- ✅ Always include the **functional implication** in 14-mark answers, not just the numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transversely and Longitudinally Framed Double Bottom
Side Shell Plating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Side Shell Plating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 – Side Shell Plating

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Side Shell Plating** is the outer skin plating forming the sides (port and starboard) of a ship's hull girder, bounded vertically by the **Sheer Strake** at the upper edge (deck sheer line) and the **Bilge Strake** at the lower edge (curved transition region between the bottom and side shell). It is a primary load-bearing component of the hull envelope, contributing directly to the **longitudinal hull girder strength** and resisting **lateral hydrostatic/wave-induced pressure** as well as **slamming and impact loads** in the bow and stern regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The side shell plating is treated as a series of **strake-wise rectangular orthogonally-stiffened plate panels** supported on four edges by longitudinal stiffeners (frames) and transverse web frames/girders. Design is governed by **Classification Society Rules** (IACS members such as Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas, ABS, IRS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey steel building** with concrete walls. The vertical outer walls carry both vertical loads (self-weight, floors) and horizontal loads (wind). Now replace the concrete with steel plates, the floor joists with ship frames, and the wind with seawater pressure — that is exactly what the **side shell plating** does. Each steel "wall" is subdivided into horizontal bands called **strakes** (like courses of bricks), and each band is reinforced by vertical "studs" (frames) and horizontal "beams" (girders) so that the plate doesn't buckle under water pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Metric:** Plating thickness in merchant ships ranges from **10 mm (small craft)** to **50+ mm (VLCC side shell)**.
> - **Material Grade:** Mild Steel (Grade A, B, D, E per IACS UR S6) or **High Tensile Steel (HTS)** of grades AH32, DH36, EH36, FH36 with yield strength up to **355 MPa**.

### 1.3 Position in Hull Structure

The side shell plating is structurally continuous with:
- **Sheer Strake (Top):** Uppermost strake, often thicker (typically 1.0–1.5 mm extra) as it acts as the upper flange of the hull girder.
- **Bilge Strake (Bottom):** Curved strake joining the bottom shell, again thicker due to high stress concentration.
- **Bottom Shell Plating:** Connected at the bilge.
- **Deck Plating:** Connected at the sheer line.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Strake-wise Subdivision of Side Shell

The side shell is divided horizontally into multiple **strakes** (each ~1.0–2.5 m vertical height) to:
1. Facilitate plate rolling in available mill sizes.
2. Permit staggered **butt joints** (offset seams improve fatigue life).
3. Allow the plating near neutral axis to be thinner and far ends thicker — economising steel.

**Staggering Rule (KTU/IACS):** Butt joints in adjacent strakes must be offset by at least **one frame spacing** to avoid creating a continuous transverse weak line.

### 2.2 Design Loads on Side Shell Plating

| Load Type | Description | Magnitude Reference |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Static Hydrostatic Pressure** | Calm sea, head-to-head wave at L/20 | $p = \rho g h$ |
| **Dynamic Wave Pressure** | SLOSH / Green-water / Bow flare impact | $p_{dyn} = C_d \cdot \rho V^2 / 2$ |
| **Slamming Pressure** | Bow emergence region (forward 0.25L) | Up to **350 kPa** (IACS S21) |
| **Cargo / Internal Pressure** | Tank boundaries, ballast | $p_{tank} = \rho_i g h_i$ |
| **Sloshing Pressure** | Partially filled tanks | IACS S21 / S22 |

### 2.3 Plate Panel Behaviour — The Core Engineering Concept

A side shell **plate panel** is bounded by:
- **Long edges** → Longitudinal frames (e.g., side frames, longitudinals)
- **Short edges** → Transverse web frames / bulkheads

The panel is loaded by transverse pressure $p$, producing:
- **Plate bending** (out-of-plane deflection $w$)
- **In-plane hull-girder stresses** ($\sigma_x$ from bending moment, $\sigma_y$ from shear)
- **Buckling** of the compressed panel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Aspect Ratio ($\alpha$):** $\alpha = a/b$ where $a$ = long edge, $b$ = short edge. Critical for determining **boundary condition factor** in yield check.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae are the **core exam-relevant** equations for side shell plating design in KTU ESE 2024.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $p = \rho g h$ | Static hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | kPa |
| 2 | $t = s \sqrt{p / (\sigma_{perm} \cdot k)}$ | Net plate thickness (simplified CSR) | mm |
| 3 | $t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{corrosion}$ | Net + corrosion allowance ($t_{cor} = $ 0.5–2.0 mm) | mm |
| 4 | $b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s$ | Effective breadth of plating acting with stiffener | mm |
| 5 | $\chi = \frac{2.4}{\alpha^2} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1.4}{\alpha + 1.8}\right)$ for $\alpha \ge 1$ | Effective breadth factor (IACS CSR) | – |
| 6 | $\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y / 1.0$ (yield) or $\sigma_y / 1.6$ (buckling) | Permissible stress (CSR) | MPa |
| 7 | $SM_{req} = M / \sigma_{perm}$ | Required section modulus of stiffener + effective plating | cm³ |
| 8 | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{M_{sw}}{Z_{eff}} \cdot 10^3$ | Hull girder bending stress in side shell | MPa |
| 9 | $M_{sw} = C_w \cdot L^2 \cdot B \cdot C_B$ | Still-water bending moment (CSR) | kN·m |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}$ | Slenderness ratio of plate (column buckling check) | – |

> **Reserved-symbol note:** All pipe symbols within expressions above are rendered using `$\vert$` to preserve markdown safety.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hull-girder longitudinal strength:** Side shell acts as the **web/upper-flange** of the hull girder.
- **Crashworthiness:** Side shell is the **first barrier** in collision/grounding; its thickness governs the **Pmin** (minimum energy dissipation) per IMO MSC.1/Circ.1455.
- **Fatigue hot-spots:** Side shell near hatch corners, side openings, and bilge region are **class-critical** for fatigue.
- **Corrosion strategy:** Side shell may be **coated + cathodic-protected** (sacrificial Zn/Al anodes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Breadth Factor $\chi$ (IACS CSR Basis)

The **effective breadth** concept accounts for **shear-lag** in plating attached to a stiffener — the stress is maximum at the stiffener and decays toward mid-panel.

Starting from the elastic shear-lag solution for a simply-supported plate strip:

$$
\sigma_x(y) = \sigma_{max} \cdot \left[1 - \dfrac{(y/s)^{2.4}}{\text{const}}\right]
$$

Integrating over the spacing $s$ and equating the mean stress to the effective uniform stress:

$$
\sigma_{eff} \cdot b_{eff} = \int_{-s/2}^{s/2} \sigma_x(y) \, dy
$$

After non-dimensionalising with aspect ratio $\alpha = a/b$, the **IACS CSR closed-form** effective breadth factor for the side shell (a ≥ b) is:

$$
\chi = \dfrac{b_{eff}}{b} = \dfrac{2.4}{\alpha^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{\alpha + 1.8}\right)
$$

For $\alpha < 1$ (a < b), interchange the roles of $a$ and $b$ in the formula.

**Boundary condition:** $\chi \le 1.0$ (effective breadth never exceeds the actual spacing).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Problem:** A side shell plate panel in a bulk carrier has frame spacing $s = 0.7$ m and is supported on web frames at $l = 2.1$ m. The panel lies at a depth of $h = 6$ m below the design waterline. Steel grade: AH36 ($\sigma_y = 355$ MPa). Determine the **net required plate thickness** using the simplified CSR formula, with a corrosion allowance $t_{cor} = 1.5$ mm. Sea-water density $\rho = 1.025$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1: Calculate hydrostatic pressure $p$**

$$
p = \rho g h = 1.025 \times 9.81 \times 6
$$

$$
p = 60.33 \, \text{kPa} = 0.0603 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2: Identify permissible stress for plate bending (yield check)**

For plate bending, the permissible bending stress is the yield stress:

$$
\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y = 355 \, \text{MPa}
$$

(For buckling check, the permissible stress would be reduced; we apply yield criterion for the net plate first.)

**Step 3: Apply the simplified CSR plate thickness formula**

$$
t_{net} = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{perm}}}
$$

Substitute numerical values (use $s$ in mm and $p$, $\sigma_{perm}$ in MPa, with the factor $k=1$):

$$
t_{net} = 700 \, \text{mm} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.0603}{355}}
$$

First compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{0.0603}{355} = 1.699 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Take the square root:

$$
\sqrt{1.699 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01304
$$

Therefore:

$$
t_{net} = 700 \times 0.01304 = 9.13 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4: Add corrosion allowance to obtain gross thickness**

$$
t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{cor} = 9.13 + 1.5 = 10.63 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5: Adopt next standard plate thickness (IACS mill series)**

Standard ship plate thicknesses (mm): …, 10, 11, 12, 12.5, 14, …

Adopted thickness:

$$
\boxed{t_{adopted} = 11 \, \text{mm}}
$$

**Step 6: Verify slenderness for column-type buckling**

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{b}{t} \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}}
$$

With $b = s = 700$ mm, $t = 11$ mm, $E = 2.06 \times 10^5$ MPa:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{700}{11} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{355}{206000}}
$$

Compute inside the root:

$$
\dfrac{355}{206000} = 1.723 \times 10^{-3} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \sqrt{1.723 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.04151
$$

$$
\lambda = 63.64 \times 0.04151 = 2.64
$$

Since $\lambda = 2.64 < 1.0 \cdot \text{limit (CSR limit for plating) } \approx 3.0$, the panel is acceptable. (If $\lambda$ were too high, the thickness would need to be increased.)

**Step 7: Section modulus check on the panel as a beam strip**

For a unit width plate strip (1.0 m wide, 11 mm thick):

$$
SM_{plate} = \dfrac{1000 \times 11^2}{6} = \dfrac{1000 \times 121}{6} = 20{,}166.67 \, \text{mm}^3 / \text{m} = 20.17 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{m}
$$

Bending moment per metre width (simply supported):

$$
M = \dfrac{p \cdot s^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.0603 \times 0.7^2}{8} = \dfrac{0.0295}{8} = 0.003694 \, \text{MPa·m} = 3.694 \, \text{kN·m/m}
$$

Bending stress in plate:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{3.694 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm/m}}{20{,}166.67 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{m}} = 183.2 \, \text{MPa}
$$

Since $183.2 < 355$ MPa → **Plate is safe in bending** by a margin of 1.94.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above is the **net** plate check. **Hull-girder bending stress** $\sigma_x$ must be superimposed before final sizing. KTU ESE problems often combine transverse plate bending with longitudinal hull-girder stress (see CSR Pt 1, Ch 4).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Side Shell Plating in Hull Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SL["SHEER STRAKE (Top - Thicker)"]
        SS["Top strake of side shell\nActs as upper hull girder flange\nTypically 1.0-1.5 mm thicker"]
    end

    subgraph M1["UPPER SIDE STRATE (A)"]
        US["Plating between sheer and 2nd tier\nConnected to side longitudinals"]
    end

    subgraph M2["MID SIDE STRATE (B)"]
        MS["Plating near neutral axis\nThinnest zone in many designs"]
    end

    subgraph M3["LOWER SIDE STRATE (C)"]
        LS["Plating near bilge\nIncreasing thickness toward bilge"]
    end

    subgraph BL["BILGE STRAKE (Curved Transition)"]
        BS["Curved plate at hull knuckle\nHighest thickness - stress hotspot"]
    end

    subgraph BSPL["BOTTOM SHELL PLATING"]
        BT["Plates forming bottom of hull\nConnected at keel"]
    end

    SL --> M1
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> BL
    BL --> BSPL

    SF["SIDE FRAMES / LONGITUDINALS\n(Vertical stiffeners between strakes)"] -.-> M1
    SF -.-> M2
    SF -.-> M3

    WF["WEB FRAMES / GIRDERS\n(Horizontal transverse supports)"] ===> SL
    WF ===> M1
    WF ===> M2
    WF ===> M3
    WF ===> BL

    LP["HYDROSTATIC + WAVE PRESSURE\n(Acts on outer face)"] --> SS
    LP --> US
    LP --> MS
    LP --> LS
    LP --> BS

    classDef strate fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1a4d80,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stiff fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class SS,US,MS,LS,BS,BT strate
    class SF,WF stiff
    class LP load
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Design Logic for Side Shell Plate Thickness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Start: Define Load Case"] --> B["Compute Design Pressure p\n(hydrostatic + dynamic + slamming)"]
    B --> C["Determine Frame Spacing s\nand Web Frame Spacing l"]
    C --> D["Choose Steel Grade\n(Yield Stress sigma_y)"]
    D --> E["Compute Net Thickness\nt_net = s * sqrt(p / sigma_perm)"]
    E --> F{"t_net + Corrosion\nwithin mill series?"}
    F -- No --> G["Step up to next\nstandard thickness"]
    F -- Yes --> H["Adopt t_gross"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Effective Breadth Check\nb_eff = chi * s"]
    I --> J["Slenderness / Buckling Check\nlambda less than CSR limit"]
    J --> K{"Acceptable?"}
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> L["Finalize: Plate + Stiffener\nSection Modulus Check"]
    L --> M["End: Plating Schedule"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Plate Panel Bending

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Panel["Plate Panel: a x b (long x short)"]
        P1["Top long edge: Longitudinal frame"]
        P2["Bottom long edge: Longitudinal frame"]
        P3["Left short edge: Web frame / Bulkhead"]
        P4["Right short edge: Web frame / Bulkhead"]
    end
    P1 -.Simply Supported.-> P3
    P3 -.Simply Supported.-> P2
    P2 -.Simply Supported.-> P4
    P4 -.Simply Supported.-> P1

    PR["Uniform Lateral Pressure p (kPa)"] ==> P1
    PR ==> P2
    PR ==> P3
    PR ==> P4

    DM["Deflection w(x,y)\nMax at panel centre"] -.-> Panel
    BM["Bending Moment M = p*b^2/8\nper unit width"] -.-> Panel
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model question pattern]**

**Q1. Define side shell plating. Name the strakes that bound it vertically.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Side shell plating is the outer plating on the port and starboard sides of a ship's hull that forms part of the watertight skin and contributes to hull girder strength. Vertically, it is bounded by the **sheer strake** at the top (at the deck sheer line) and the **bilge strake** at the bottom (at the curvature connecting the side shell to the bottom shell). Together with adjacent plating, it transmits hydrostatic and hydrodynamic loads to the framing system. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. What is the "effective breadth" concept in side shell plating design?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The effective breadth concept accounts for **shear-lag** in plating attached to a longitudinal stiffener. Due to shear deformation, the in-plane longitudinal stress in the plate is maximum at the stiffener and reduces toward the middle of the panel. The **effective breadth** $b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s$ (where $\chi$ is a factor depending on panel aspect ratio $\alpha$ and boundary conditions) represents an equivalent uniform width of plate that, when stressed uniformly, produces the same axial force as the actual non-uniform stress distribution. This value is then combined with the stiffener to compute the section modulus and hull girder stress. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 model question pattern: Choice-based, 14 marks with sub-parts]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q(a). With a neat schematic, explain the side shell plating arrangement of a typical bulk carrier. Identify the strakes and explain why the sheer and bilge strakes are made thicker. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*Arrangement:* The side shell plating of a bulk carrier is divided horizontally into a series of strakes between the sheer strake (top) and the bilge strake (bottom). From top to bottom these are typically the **sheer strake**, **upper side strakes**, **middle side strakes**, **lower side strakes**, and the **bilge strake**. Each strake is connected to the next by horizontal **seams** and individual plates within a strake are joined by vertical **butts**. (3 marks)

*Staggering:* Butts in adjacent strakes are offset by at least one frame spacing to avoid a continuous transverse weak joint. (1 mark)

*Sheer strake thickness:* The sheer strake is the uppermost strake, located at the deck sheer line. It acts as the **upper flange of the hull girder** and therefore carries very high longitudinal hull-girder stress (sagging and hogging). Additionally, it is exposed to **green-sea loading and deck-edge impact**, and provides the upper termination for longitudinal framing. Hence it is made 1.0–1.5 mm thicker than adjacent strakes. (1.5 marks)

*Bilge strake thickness:* The bilge strake is the curved plate at the knuckle between the side shell and the bottom shell. It is a region of **high stress concentration** due to (i) sharp curvature causing stress raise, (ii) high combined hull-girder stresses, and (iii) **slamming and impact loading** in the forward region. Hence a higher thickness is adopted. (1.5 marks)

**Q(b). A side shell plate in a single-skin vessel has frame spacing 750 mm. The design pressure at the plate's location is 0.08 MPa. Steel grade is AH32 with $\sigma_y = 315$ MPa. Determine the net and gross plate thickness using the simplified CSR formula, with a corrosion allowance of 2.0 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Given:
- Frame spacing: $s = 750$ mm
- Design pressure: $p = 0.08$ MPa
- Yield stress: $\sigma_y = 315$ MPa
- Corrosion allowance: $t_{cor} = 2.0$ mm

**Step 1:** Apply the simplified CSR plate thickness formula:

$$
t_{net} = s \sqrt{\dfrac{p}{\sigma_{perm}}}
$$

For yield check, $\sigma_{perm} = \sigma_y = 315$ MPa:

$$
t_{net} = 750 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{0.08}{315}}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{0.08}{315} = 2.540 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 3:** Square root:

$$
\sqrt{2.540 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.01594
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply:

$$
t_{net} = 750 \times 0.01594 = 11.95 \, \text{mm} \approx 12.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5:** Gross thickness:

$$
t_{gross} = t_{net} + t_{cor} = 12.0 + 2.0 = 14.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 6:** Adopt the next standard mill size (the mill series includes 12, 14, 16 mm…):

$$
\boxed{t_{adopted} = 14 \, \text{mm}}
$$

*(Full marks: 1 mark for the formula selection, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for adding corrosion allowance, 1 mark for standardisation.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

**Q(a). Explain the factors influencing the thickness of side shell plating in a merchant ship. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Design Pressure (3 marks):** The thickness is directly proportional to $\sqrt{p}$ where $p$ is the maximum combined hydrostatic + dynamic + slamming pressure at the plate's location. Plates deeper in the hull, in the slamming region, or in ballast/cargo tank boundaries require higher thickness.
2. **Frame Spacing (1 mark):** A larger frame spacing $s$ increases the unsupported plate edge length, requiring higher thickness. The CSR formula $t = s\sqrt{p/\sigma}$ shows the direct proportionality.
3. **Material Grade (1 mark):** Higher-tensile steels (AH32, DH36, EH36) allow higher permissible stress, which reduces required thickness. Mild steel requires more thickness for the same load.
4. **Corrosion Allowance (1 mark):** A 0.5–2.0 mm corrosion margin (depending on service: ballast tanks, cargo oil, sea-side) is added to the net thickness.
5. **Hull Girder Stress (1 mark):** Side shell participates as the upper flange of the hull girder; the longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$ is superimposed on transverse bending, and the combined stress must remain below the permissible. This can govern plate thickness in midship region.

**Q(b). A side shell plate panel has long-edge spacing $a = 2.4$ m (between web frames) and short-edge spacing $b = 0.8$ m (between longitudinal frames). Calculate the effective breadth factor $\chi$ using the IACS CSR formula. If the longitudinal stiffener section modulus is 80 cm³ and the permissible stress is 220 MPa, compute the section modulus required to be provided by the plate (effective width basis) for a hull girder stress of 120 MPa. The plate thickness is 12 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute aspect ratio:

$$
\alpha = \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{2.4}{0.8} = 3.0
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the IACS CSR effective breadth factor formula (for $\alpha \ge 1$):

$$
\chi = \dfrac{2.4}{\alpha^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{\alpha + 1.8}\right)
$$

Substitute $\alpha = 3.0$:

$$
\chi = \dfrac{2.4}{9.0}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{3.0 + 1.8}\right)
$$

$$
\chi = 0.2667 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{4.8}\right)
$$

$$
\chi = 0.2667 \times (1 - 0.2917) = 0.2667 \times 0.7083 = 0.1889
$$

Since $\chi$ must be capped at 1.0, $\chi = 0.1889$ is acceptable (and well below 1.0).

**Step 3:** Effective breadth:

$$
b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s = 0.1889 \times 800 = 151.1 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4:** Section modulus contributed by the plate:

$$
SM_{plate} = \dfrac{b_{eff} \cdot t^2}{6} = \dfrac{151.1 \times 12^2}{6} = \dfrac{151.1 \times 144}{6} = 3626.4 \, \text{mm}^3 = 3.63 \, \text{cm}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Total force-equivalent required SM for the axial hull girder stress check.

**Required additional SM from plate to share stress with stiffener:**

The stiffener alone carries $120 \times 80 = 9600 \, \text{N·cm} = 96 \, \text{kN·cm}$ in axial force. Adding the plate in parallel:

Total force the combination must carry (per IACS CSR, total force = $\sigma \times A$ approach, not SM approach). Alternatively, for the SM check (bending):

The plate's contribution of $3.63 \, \text{cm}^3$ is **insufficient** to significantly reduce the required stiffener section modulus; in practice, the stiffener's effective SM including effective plating is checked against the bending moment demand.

For a bending moment demand, suppose the moment per spacing is $M = 9.6$ kN·m at the section. Required SM:

$$
SM_{req} = \dfrac{M}{\sigma_{perm}} = \dfrac{9.6 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}}{220 \, \text{N/mm}^2} = 43{,}636 \, \text{mm}^3 = 43.64 \, \text{cm}^3
$$

The stiffener contributes 80 cm³, which is sufficient by itself, so the effective plate provides additional reserve.

**Step 6:** Final answer — the **effective breadth factor is 0.189**, the effective breadth is 151 mm, and the plate's SM contribution is 3.63 cm³, which supplements the stiffener.

*(1 mark for aspect ratio, 2 marks for χ formula and substitution, 1 mark for $b_{eff}$, 1 mark for plate SM, 2 marks for interpretation/required SM.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Mixing units:** $s$ in mm, $p$ in MPa, $\sigma$ in MPa → forgetting the dimensionless ratio. Always compute the ratio $p/\sigma$ as a pure number before taking the root.
> 2. **Forgetting corrosion allowance:** KTU answer key specifically deducts **2 marks** if $t_{cor}$ is omitted in a numerical.
> 3. **Not stating the standard mill series adopted:** Examiners expect a sentence like "Adopting 12 mm from IACS mill series" — losing **1 mark** if skipped.
> 4. **Confusing net vs gross thickness:** "Required thickness" in the problem usually means **gross** in KTU papers. Read carefully!
> 5. **Aspect ratio direction:** $\alpha = a/b$ where $a$ is the longer side. Reversing this gives a wrong $\chi$ — losing **2 marks**.
> 6. **Missing diagram labels:** In 7-mark descriptive questions, an unlabelled sketch attracts only **partial credit** (typically half marks for content + half for diagram).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Side shell plating = outer skin on port/starboard, between **sheer strake** (top) and **bilge strake** (bottom).
- **Key strakes:** Sheer (thicker), Upper/Middle/Lower side strakes, Bilge (thicker).
- **Primary loads:** Lateral hydrostatic pressure, dynamic wave pressure, slamming, hull-girder longitudinal stress.
- **Core formula (memorise):** $t = s\sqrt{p/\sigma_{perm}}$ and $b_{eff} = \chi \cdot s$ with $\chi = \dfrac{2.4}{\alpha^2}\left(1 - \dfrac{1.4}{\alpha+1.8}\right)$.
- **Corrosion allowance:** Always add $t_{cor}$ (0.5–2.0 mm) → **net + corrosion = gross**; adopt next standard mill thickness.
- **Sheer & bilge strakes:** Made 1.0–1.5 mm thicker than adjacent strakes due to high hull-girder stress and slamming/curvature effects.
- **Butt joints:** Must be staggered across strakes by at least one frame spacing.
- **Material grades:** Mild steel (Grade A–E) and HTS (AH32, DH36, EH36, FH36) with $\sigma_y$ up to **355 MPa**.
- **Design sequence (must learn):** Loads → Pressure → Net thickness → + Corrosion → Standardise → Effective breadth → Slenderness → Section modulus check.
- **Effective breadth concept:** Shear-lag → stress concentration at stiffener → use $\chi$ ≤ 1.0.
- **Hull girder participation:** Side shell acts as the upper flange — longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$ must be combined with plate bending.
- **Classification Society:** All thickness rules trace back to IACS CSR Pt 1, Ch 4 / Pt 1, Ch 7 (for buckling).
- **Sketch must include:** Strakes, frames (longitudinals), web frames/girders, pressure arrows, label sheer & bilge strakes.
- **Unit discipline:** $s$ in mm; $p$ in MPa (or convert from kPa by /1000); $\sigma$ in MPa; $t$ in mm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
