Bulkheads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bulkheads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulkhead**: A **vertical transverse or longitudinal plate wall** inside a ship's hull, extending from the shell plating (or tank top) up to the deck (or tank top below), used to subdivide the ship's internal volume into compartments. Bulkheads provide **structural strength**, **transverse/longitudinal rigidity**, and most critically, **watertight subdivision** to prevent flooding in case of hull breach (SOLAS Convention).

In the context of **Basic Ship Structures (OENST614)**, bulkheads form the **secondary structural skeleton** of the ship. They resist racking forces, support the hull against bending, prevent the spread of fire, and limit flooding progression.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a ship as a **multi-story apartment building** floating on water. Just as a building has **firewalls and load-bearing partition walls** between rooms, a ship has **bulkheads**.

- The **outer hull** is like the building's exterior brick wall.
- The **bulkheads** are like the **internal partition walls** between rooms — they keep a fire (or flood) from spreading from one room to another.
- The **collision bulkhead** is like the **firewall of the lobby** — it protects the main living quarters from a crash in the entrance (the bow).
- Just as a partition wall is reinforced with **studs (gussets/stiffeners)** and sheathed in **plasterboard (plating)**, a bulkhead is constructed of **steel plating stiffened by vertical and horizontal stiffeners**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric / Standard**: Classification society rules (e.g., **Lloyd's Register, DNV, Bureau Veritas**) require that the minimum number of transverse watertight bulkheads equals **4** for cargo ships (Collision Bulkhead + Engine Room Bulkhead + Aft Peak Bulkhead + one additional bulkhead depending on ship length per SOLAS Chapter II-1).

## 1.3 Bulkhead Classification Overview

Bulkheads can be categorized along **three orthogonal axes**:

| Classification Axis | Types |
|---|---|
| **By Orientation** | Transverse (athwartships) & Longitudinal (fore-aft) |
| **By Watertightness** | Watertight (WT) & Non-watertight (NWT) / Dust |
| **By Function** | Collision, Engine Room, Shaft Tunnel, Cofferdam, Partial, Swash, Stool, Flat, Peak |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Classification of Bulkheads

### 2.1.1 Collision Bulkhead (Forward Peak Bulkhead)
- **Location**: Aft of the forepeak tank, typically within **5% to 7% of the length between perpendiculars (LBP)** from the forward perpendicular (FP).
- **Function**: Prevents flooding of the entire ship in case of bow collision.
- **Regulation**: **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 12** mandates this for all ships.
- **Key feature**: Must be watertight up to the freeboard deck. **No doors, manholes, or openings** are permitted below the bulkhead deck.

### 2.1.2 Engine Room Bulkhead
- **Location**: One bulkhead forward and one aft of the machinery space (forming a watertight enclosure around the engine room).
- **Function**: Contains fires and flooding in the high-risk machinery compartment.
- **Key feature**: May have **watertight doors** (Class A-60 fire-rated).

### 2.1.3 Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead
- A partial bulkhead forming the tunnel that houses the propeller shaft from the engine room to the stern tube.

### 2.1.4 Swash Bulkhead
- **Partial bulkhead** (does not extend to the deck) installed transversely in tanks to **dampen sloshing** of liquid cargo/fuel during rolling.

### 2.1.5 Cofferdam Bulkhead
- An **empty double bulkhead** between two adjacent tanks (e.g., fuel oil and freshwater) to prevent contamination.

## 2.2 Structural Arrangement of a Watertight Bulkhead

A typical bulkhead comprises **three principal components**:

1. **Plating (Shell of the Bulkhead)** — the continuous steel plate that provides watertightness and shear resistance.
2. **Stiffeners (Girders)** — vertical or horizontal rolled sections that prevent buckling of the plating.
3. **End Connections (Boundary Brackets)** — welded brackets at the top and bottom connecting the stiffeners to the deck/tank top.

## 2.3 Corrugated vs. Flat Bulkheads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrugated Bulkheads** are increasingly preferred in modern tankers and bulk carriers over flat stiffened bulkheads because they eliminate the need for separate stiffeners, reducing fabrication cost and weight.

| Feature | Flat Stiffened Bulkhead | Corrugated Bulkhead |
|---|---|---|
| **Stiffening** | Separate T-bars or angles welded to plate | Built-in trapezoidal/curved corrugations |
| **Material Use** | Higher (plate + stiffeners) | Lower (no separate stiffeners) |
| **Fabrication** | Labour-intensive | Mechanically pressed corrugations |
| **Scantlings (DNV)** | Rule-based on stiffener section modulus | Rule-based on corrugation geometry |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Used**: $t$ = plating thickness (mm), $s$ = spacing (mm), $l$ = unsupported span (mm), $p$ = design pressure (kN/m²), $Z$ = section modulus (cm³), $W$ = load per unit area (kN/m²).

### 2.4.1 Plating Thickness (Lloyd's Register / DNV Style Empirical Rule)

$$t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p} \quad \text{(mm)}$$

Where $k$ is a coefficient based on stiffener spacing and material yield (typically $k \approx 0.018$ for mild steel).

### 2.4.2 Stiffener Section Modulus (Required)

$$Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma_{\text{perm}}} \quad \text{(cm³)}$$

Where:
- $m$ = bending moment coefficient (table value based on end fixity, e.g., $m = 8$ for simply supported with rigid ends)
- $\sigma_{\text{perm}}$ = permissible bending stress (~175 N/mm² for mild steel)

### 2.4.3 Shear Area Check (Web Shear)

$$A_{\text{web,req}} = \frac{V_{\text{max}}}{\tau_{\text{perm}}}$$

Where $\tau_{\text{perm}} \approx 100$ N/mm² for mild steel webs.

### 2.4.4 Pressure on a Bulkhead (Hydrostatic Head in a Tank)

$$p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h \quad \text{(kN/m² or kPa)}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of liquid (seawater = **1025 kg/m³**, fuel oil = **~850 kg/m³**, freshwater = **1000 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- $h$ = head of liquid from bulkhead top to bottom (m)

### 2.4.5 Euler Buckling Stress (For Vertical Stiffeners on Tall Bulkheads)

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{\pi^{2} \cdot E \cdot I}{A \cdot l_{e}^{2}}$$

Where $E = 2.06 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² for steel, $I$ = moment of inertia, $l_{e}$ = effective length (depends on end condition).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Design Pressure on a Deep Tank Bulkhead

Consider a **deep ballast tank** with the bulkhead extending vertically from the **tank top** to the **deck**. We need to find the **maximum design pressure** acting on the bulkhead plating at its lowest point.

### Setup
- Let the tank depth from the underside of deck to the tank top be $H$ (meters).
- The tank is filled with seawater of density $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.
- Specific weight $\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 1025 \times 9.81$ N/m³.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1**: Apply the fundamental hydrostatic pressure formula.

The pressure at depth $h$ below the free surface of the liquid is:

$$
p(h) = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = \gamma \cdot h
$$

**Step 2**: Identify the depth at the bottom of the tank.

At the bottom of the bulkhead, $h = H$. Therefore the maximum pressure is:

$$
p_{\max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H
$$

**Step 3**: Insert numerical values for seawater.

Substituting $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ and $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
p_{\max} = 1025 \times 9.81 \times H
$$

$$
p_{\max} = 10055.25 \times H \quad \text{(N/m² or Pa)}
$$

**Step 4**: Convert to standard shipbuilding units (kN/m²).

Since $1$ kN = $1000$ N:

$$
p_{\max} = 10.055 \times H \quad \text{(kN/m²)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> For seawater, a useful rule-of-thumb is: **pressure increases by ~10 kN/m² for every 1 m of tank depth**.

**Step 5**: Apply a design margin (Classification Society factor).

Classification societies often apply a **design factor $f$** (typically $f = 1.05$ to $1.10$) to account for dynamic loads (sloshing):

$$
p_{\text{design}} = f \cdot p_{\max} = 1.05 \times 10.055 \times H \approx 10.56 \times H \quad \text{(kN/m²)}
$$

**Step 6**: Determine the required plating thickness using the empirical rule from §2.4.1.

Using $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p}$ with $k = 0.018$ and stiffener spacing $s = 600$ mm:

$$
t = 600 \times 0.018 \times \sqrt{10.56 \cdot H}
$$

$$
t = 10.8 \cdot \sqrt{10.56 \cdot H} \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

**Step 7**: Example Numerical Evaluation — Tank Depth 8 m.

For $H = 8$ m:

$$
p_{\text{design}} = 10.56 \times 8 = 84.48 \quad \text{(kN/m²)}
$$

$$
t = 10.8 \times \sqrt{84.48} = 10.8 \times 9.19 = 99.3 \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

Rounding up to standard plate thickness: **$t = 100$ mm**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Bulkhead Selection Decision Tree

| Ship Type & Compartment | Recommended Bulkhead Type | Plating Orientation | Watertight? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forepeak of any ship | Collision (flat stiffened or corrugated) | Vertical plating | YES (SOLAS Reg. 12) |
| Engine room forward boundary | Watertight WT with A-60 fire insulation | Vertical | YES |
| Cargo hold boundaries (bulk carrier) | Corrugated WT bulkhead | Vertical | YES |
| Inside cargo tanks (tanker) | Corrugated longitudinal bulkheads | Vertical | YES |
| Shaft tunnel ceiling | Flat partial bulkhead | Horizontal | YES (local) |
| Inside a deep ballast tank | Corrugated swash bulkhead | Vertical | YES (dampens sloshing) |
| Accommodation separation | Non-watertight partial bulkhead | Vertical | NO |
| Between fuel oil & freshwater tanks | Cofferdam (empty space) | Vertical | YES |

## 3.3 Boundary Conditions for Bulkhead Stiffeners

| Boundary Condition | Fixity Coefficient $m$ | Effective Length $l_{e}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Both ends simply supported | $m = 8$ | $l_{e} = 1.0 \cdot l$ |
| One end fixed, other simply supported | $m = 11.4$ | $l_{e} = 0.7 \cdot l$ |
| Both ends fully fixed (rigid brackets) | $m = 24$ | $l_{e} = 0.5 \cdot l$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **real shipbuilding**, the end connections use welded **brackets (gussets)** which provide partial fixity. Most classification societies adopt **$m \approx 12$ to $16$** as a compromise between simply supported and fully fixed conditions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define a bulkhead and explain the function of a collision bulkhead.**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A bulkhead is a vertical plate wall inside a ship used to divide the hull into compartments, providing structural strength and watertight subdivision.

A **collision bulkhead** is a watertight transverse bulkhead located at the forward end of the ship, typically within **5%–7% of LBP** from the forward perpendicular. Its function is to **prevent flooding of the entire ship** in the event of a bow collision. It must extend watertight up to the freeboard deck, and **no doors, manholes, or openings** are permitted below the bulkhead deck, in accordance with **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 12**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for collision bulkhead function and SOLAS detail)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Differentiate between a flat stiffened bulkhead and a corrugated bulkhead.**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Flat Stiffened Bulkhead | Corrugated Bulkhead |
|---|---|---|
| Stiffening | Separate T-bars/angles welded to the plate | Built-in trapezoidal/curved corrugations |
| Weight | Higher (plate + stiffeners) | Lower (no separate stiffeners) |
| Fabrication | Labour-intensive | Mechanically pressed |
| Applications | Older ships, collision bulkheads | Modern tankers, bulk carriers |

*(3 marks: 1 for stating the difference + 2 for the comparative table or distinguishing points)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of watertight bulkheads found in a typical merchant ship with neat sketches. Describe the regulations governing their number and location as per SOLAS. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A deep ballast tank has its bulkhead extending from the tank top to the deck with a vertical height of **8 m**. The bulkhead is made of flat stiffened mild steel construction with vertical stiffeners spaced at **600 mm**. Using:
- Density of seawater = 1025 kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Design factor $f = 1.05$
- Plate coefficient $k = 0.018$
- Permissible bending stress $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 175$ N/mm²

Calculate: **(i)** Maximum design pressure on the plating, **(ii)** Required plating thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Watertight Bulkhead Types & SOLAS Regulations (7 Marks)

**Types of WT Bulkheads (any 4 expected, 1.5 marks each):**

1. **Collision Bulkhead** – Forward, 5–7% LBP from FP. No openings allowed below bulkhead deck.
2. **Engine Room Bulkhead** – Forward and aft of machinery space. Contains A-60 fire-rated WT doors.
3. **Peak Bulkheads** – Forward and aft peaks. Usually subdivided by swash plates for fuel/water.
4. **Shaft Tunnel Bulkhead** – Partial bulkhead forming tunnel for propeller shaft.
5. **Cofferdam Bulkhead** – Empty double bulkhead between incompatible tanks.
6. **Transverse WT Bulkheads between cargo holds** – Required per SOLAS based on subdivision index.

**SOLAS Regulations (2 marks):**

- **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 12**: Defines watertight bulkheads, their number, and location.
- **Regulation 13**: Subdivision and damage stability requirements.
- **Subdivision Index (R)**: For cargo ships, $R \geq 0.5$ for 1-compartment flooding, $R \geq 0.7$ for 2-compartment flooding (depending on length and service).

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Compute the design pressure (3 marks).**

Given: $H = 8$ m, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $f = 1.05$.

$$
p_{\max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 8
$$

$$
p_{\max} = 80442 \quad \text{Pa} = 80.44 \quad \text{kN/m²}
$$

Applying design factor:

$$
p_{\text{design}} = f \cdot p_{\max} = 1.05 \times 80.44 = 84.46 \quad \text{(kN/m²)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute plating thickness (4 marks).**

Using $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p}$ with $s = 600$ mm and $k = 0.018$:

$$
t = 600 \times 0.018 \times \sqrt{84.46}
$$

$$
t = 10.8 \times 9.19
$$

$$
t = 99.3 \quad \text{(mm)}
$$

Rounded up to standard shipbuilding plate gauge: **$t = 100$ mm**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the constructional details of a corrugated bulkhead with a neat sketch. List **three advantages** of corrugated bulkheads over flat stiffened bulkheads. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **required section modulus** of a vertical stiffener on a transverse bulkhead in a cargo hold of a bulk carrier. Given:
- Design pressure $p = 25$ kN/m²
- Stiffener spacing $s = 700$ mm
- Unsupported span between brackets $l = 3500$ mm
- Bending moment coefficient $m = 12$
- Permissible bending stress $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 175$ N/mm²

If a **T-section (T150×75×9/12)** with section modulus $Z = 75$ cm³ is provided, check whether it is adequate. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Corrugated Bulkhead Construction (7 Marks)

**Constructional Details (4 marks):**
- The bulkhead plating is pressed into a **series of trapezoidal or curved corrugations** running vertically (or horizontally).
- Each corrugation has a **flange (top)**, **web (side)**, and **flange (bottom)**, forming a built-up stiffener.
- Typical corrugation pitch $P = 600$–$1000$ mm; depth $D = 150$–$300$ mm; angle $\alpha = 60°$–$90°$.
- Welded directly to the deck above and tank top/double bottom below using **edge preparations (full penetration welds)**.

**Three Advantages (3 marks — 1 each):**
1. **No separate stiffeners required** — saving material and fabrication cost.
2. **Lower weight** for the same buckling and bending strength.
3. **Higher out-of-plane stiffness** — better resistance to sloshing and cargo pressure.

---

#### Part (b) — Section Modulus Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Required Section Modulus (4 marks).**

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma_{\text{perm}}}
$$

Convert units to N and mm:
- $p = 25 \times 10^{-3}$ N/mm²
- $s = 700$ mm
- $l = 3500$ mm
- $m = 12$
- $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 175$ N/mm²

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{0.025 \times 700 \times (3500)^{2}}{12 \times 175} \quad \text{(mm³)}
$$

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{0.025 \times 700 \times 12{,}250{,}000}{2100}
$$

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{214{,}375{,}000}{2100} = 102{,}083 \quad \text{(mm³)}
$$

Converting to cm³ (1 cm³ = 1000 mm³):

$$
Z_{\text{req}} = 102.08 \quad \text{(cm³)}
$$

**Step 2: Comparison (3 marks).**

Provided section modulus: $Z_{\text{provided}} = 75$ cm³.

Compare:

$$
Z_{\text{provided}} = 75 \quad \text{(cm³)} < Z_{\text{req}} = 102.08 \quad \text{(cm³)}
$$

**Conclusion**: The T150×75×9/12 section is **NOT adequate**. A larger section must be selected. A suitable standard section is e.g., **T200×100×11/14** with $Z \geq 103$ cm³.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Most students forget to convert kN/m² to N/mm² before substituting into the formula. This single mistake results in losing **3 marks** out of 7 in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing boundary conditions** — Always state whether the stiffener is *simply supported*, *fixed*, or *partially fixed*. The value of $m$ changes drastically (8 vs. 24).
> 3. **SOLAS Regulation misquoting** — Examiners expect the **regulation number (Chapter II-1, Reg. 12)** along with the rule, not just "per SOLAS".
> 4. **Skipping sketches in part (a) of 14-mark questions** — Even a small labelled sketch of the bulkhead location **earns 2 extra marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- [x] **Bulkhead** = vertical plate wall subdividing the ship into compartments.
- [x] **Three classification axes**: Orientation (transverse/longitudinal), Watertightness (WT/NWT), Function (collision/engine room/swash/cofferdam).
- [x] **Collision bulkhead** is at the forward end, within **5%–7% of LBP**, regulated by **SOLAS Ch. II-1, Reg. 12**. No openings below bulkhead deck.
- [x] **Engine room bulkhead** surrounds the machinery space; may have A-60 fire-rated WT doors.
- [x] **Swash bulkhead** = partial vertical plate inside tanks to dampen liquid sloshing.
- [x] **Cofferdam** = empty double bulkhead between incompatible fluid tanks (e.g., fuel oil and freshwater).
- [x] **Corrugated bulkheads** are preferred in modern ships — lighter, faster to fabricate, no separate stiffeners needed.
- [x] **Flat stiffened bulkheads** use rolled T-sections or angles welded to the plate; still used for collision bulkheads.
- [x] **Plating thickness rule**: $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p}$ (mm), where $p$ is in kN/m².
- [x] **Stiffener section modulus rule**: $Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{p \cdot s \cdot l^{2}}{m \cdot \sigma_{\text{perm}}}$ — always convert units to N/mm².
- [x] **Boundary fixity coefficient** $m$: 8 (simply supported), 11.4 (one end fixed), 24 (both ends fully fixed) — ships typically use $m = 12$.
- [x] **Seawater hydrostatic rule**: pressure increases by **~10 kN/m² per metre** of depth.
- [x] **Standard permissible stresses** (mild steel): $\sigma_{\text{perm}} \approx 175$ N/mm², $\tau_{\text{perm}} \approx 100$ N/mm².
- [x] **Material density (for pressure calcs)**: Seawater = **1025 kg/m³**, Fuel oil ≈ **850 kg/m³**, Freshwater = **1000 kg/m³**.
- [x] **SOLAS subdivision index** $R \geq 0.5$ (1-compartment) and $R \geq 0.7$ (2-compartment) for most cargo ships.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Watertight Doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Watertight Doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **WATERTIGHT DOOR (WTD):** A structural access door fitted in a watertight bulkhead of a ship, designed to prevent the passage of water between adjacent compartments under specified pressure head conditions, while still permitting controlled passage of personnel, cargo, or piping.

According to **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 13** and the **IACS Unified Requirements (UR S21)**, a watertight door is any door that, when closed and secured, prevents the passage of water in any direction under a designed head of water. These doors are critical transverse or longitudinal subdivision components of a ship's damage stability strategy.

### Sub-Categories Defined by KTU/IACS:

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Watertight Door** | Prevents water passage under full design head pressure. |
| **Weathertight Door** | Prevents water ingress from external weather/sea spray (external doors only). |
| **Slamming Door** | Designed to withstand water impact from green seas slamming against the hull. |
| **Semi-Watertight / Oiltight Door** | Prevents passage of oil or limited water spray; not rated for full head. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Apartment Building in a Flood"**
> Imagine a high-rise apartment building where every wall is sealed. If a pipe bursts on Floor 5, the whole building floods. Now, imagine you install special **sealed emergency doors** between each apartment, with rubber gaskets and strong latches. If Floor 5 floods, you close the doors on Floor 4 and Floor 6 — only one apartment is ruined, not the whole building.
> A **ship's watertight door is exactly that — an emergency sealed hatch built into a wall (bulkhead) that you can walk through normally, but which seals watertight in seconds during an emergency** to keep the ship floating.

## 1.3 Standard Design Metrics (KTU/IACS Values)

- **Design head of water** for subdivision WTDs: based on **bulkhead deck** location — typically **2.5 m to 6.0 m** of water column.
- **Hose test pressure** for door seals: **0.2 MPa (2 bar)** minimum.
- **Operating cycle time** for power-operated WTDs (SOLAS): closure within **60 seconds** with the ship upright; **30 seconds** in a 15° heel.
- **Door clear opening** for personnel: minimum **600 mm × 1500 mm** (IACS S21).
- **Number of dogging levers** typically: 4 to 8, depending on door size and pressure rating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention the **SOLAS Chapter II-1 / IACS UR S21** reference when answering WTD questions — examiners reward this regulatory citation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Purpose of Watertight Doors

Watertight doors are installed to satisfy the following engineering objectives:

1. **Compartmentalization** — Divide the ship hull into **subdivision compartments** such that progressive flooding is contained (per SOLAS damage stability).
2. **Access** — Permit normal transit of crew, passengers, and machinery between compartments without compromising subdivision.
3. **Emergency Closure** — Provide rapid closure during flooding, fire, or damage incidents.
4. **Pressure Integrity** — Withstand the **hydrostatic head** of water on one side without leakage.
5. **Damage Control** — Interface with the ship's **damage control plan**, **flooding alarm systems**, and **indicator panels** on the bridge.

## 2.2 Classification of Watertight Doors

### A. By Operating Principle

| Type | Mechanism | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Hinged WTD** | Swings on hinges; dogs on perimeter | Small access doors, low pressure |
| **Sliding WTD** | Moves horizontally on rollers/guides | Most common in machinery spaces |
| **Rolling WTD** | Door panel rolls upward like a shutter | Cargo holds, engine rooms |
| **Quick-Acting WTD** | Single-handle closure with multiple dogs | Engine rooms, high-traffic zones |
| **Plug-Type WTD** | Circular door seals against circular frame | High-pressure applications (submarines, deep tanks) |

### B. By Mode of Operation

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Manually Operated** | Closed by crew using handles/handwheels; requires no power. |
| **Power Operated** | Hydraulic, electric, or pneumatic actuators. |
| **Remotely Operated** | Closed from the bridge or ECR via control panel (SOLAS Reg. 13.3). |
| **Fail-Safe / Self-Closing** | Closes automatically on loss of power or flooding sensor trigger. |

### C. By Pressure Rating

| Class | Designed Head |
|---|---|
| **Light WTD** | Up to 2.5 m water column |
| **Medium WTD** | 2.5 m to 4.5 m |
| **Heavy WTD** | Above 4.5 m (deep tank or collision bulkhead) |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Working — Sliding Watertight Door

The most common WTD in merchant ships is the **sliding, power-operated, quick-acting watertight door**. Its operating sequence is:

1. **Door Held Open** — Dogging levers retracted; door rests on its open position stops.
2. **Warning Signal Activated** — Audible alarm (typically 5–10 second pre-warning) and visual indicator.
3. **Door Slides Closed** — Hydraulic/electric drive moves the door on its track toward the closed frame.
4. **Dogs Engage** — Peripheral dogging levers (4 to 8) rotate into the frame notches simultaneously.
5. **Seal Compression** — Rubber gasket (often **neoprene** or **nitrile**) compresses against the frame, achieving watertight integrity.
6. **Indicator Confirmation** — Position sensors send **"closed & locked"** signal to bridge panel and ECR.
7. **Local Lockout** — Local controls are disabled to prevent opening during remote operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" Highlight:** The pre-warning alarm is a SOLAS-mandated safety feature — it prevents crew injury if someone is still in the door opening during closure. KTU questions often ask: *"Why is the alarm time critical?"* — answer: **to allow personnel evacuation and prevent entrapment**.

## 2.4 Constructional Details

### Door Panel
- Fabricated from **mild steel** plate (typically **6 mm to 12 mm** thick), stiffened with **T-section or flat-bar webs**.
- Internal stiffener spacing calculated to limit panel deflection under design head.
- **Lightening holes** may be cut in stiffeners to reduce weight.

### Frame
- Built up from heavy **bulb plate or angle section** welded to the bulkhead.
- Frame must be **flush with the bulkhead plating** to maintain the structural continuity.

### Sill (Coaming)
- A raised threshold of height **100 mm to 380 mm** depending on location and class.
- Critical in **sliding doors** to retain the door panel and prevent water passage under the door.
- **Sill height** is often a Part A (3-mark) KTU question.

### Gaskets
- **Neoprene rubber**, **nitrile**, or **silicone** — chosen for seawater resistance, oil resistance, and temperature tolerance.
- Continuous loop or segmented — segments allow replacement without full disassembly.
- Compression is achieved by dogging levers; **compression set** must be checked annually.

### Dogging Arrangement
- **4 to 8 eccentric levers** spaced around the perimeter.
- Each lever pivots into a **recessed notch** in the frame.
- Central handwheel drives all dogs simultaneously via **spider/cam** mechanism (mechanical synchronization).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Gold:** The following equations and constants are repeatedly tested. Memorize the table.

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic pressure on door | $p = \rho g h$ | Pa | $\rho$ = water density, $g$ = gravity, $h$ = head |
| Total force on door | $F = p \cdot A = \rho g h \cdot (W \cdot H)$ | N | $W$ = door width, $H$ = door height |
| Required gasket compression | $\delta = k \cdot p$ | m | Empirical; $k$ depends on gasket material |
| Sill height (SOLAS) | $h_{sill} \geq 380$ mm | mm | For subdivision bulkhead doors |
| Minimum clear opening | $W \geq 600$ mm, $H \geq 1500$ mm | mm | IACS S21 / SOLAS |
| Closure time (upright) | $t \leq 60$ s | s | SOLAS II-1/13 |
| Closure time (15° heel) | $t \leq 30$ s | s | SOLAS II-1/13 |
| Hose test pressure | $p_{test} = 0.2$ MPa (min) | MPa | Or $1.5 \times$ design head, whichever is greater |
| Number of dogs | $n = \lceil \text{perimeter} / 600 \text{ mm} \rceil$ | — | Rule of thumb spacing |
| Plate thickness (approx.) | $t_{plate} = k \cdot d \cdot \sqrt{p / \sigma_{allow}})$ | mm | $d$ = stiffener spacing |

> **Note on units:** $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ (seawater), $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\sigma_{allow} \approx 160$ MPa for mild steel.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Damage Stability:** WTDs are the active component of the ship's subdivision philosophy; without them, the subdivision bulkheads would be useless in operation.
- **SOLAS Compliance:** Mandatory for passenger ships and certain cargo ships (Reg. 22, 23).
- **Offshore Platforms:** WTD concept extends to blast doors, fire doors, and H₂S-resistant doors in FPSOs and drilling rigs.
- **Submarines:** Use the most extreme WTD design — **plug-type doors** withstanding 30+ bar pressure differentials.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Hydrostatic Force on a Watertight Door

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** A rectangular sliding watertight door, of clear dimensions **W = 800 mm** wide and **H = 1500 mm** tall, is fitted in a transverse subdivision bulkhead at the **bulkhead deck** of a cargo ship. The maximum design head of water on the door is **h = 4.5 m** of seawater. Determine:
> (a) The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the door.
> (b) The total resultant force acting on the door panel.
> (c) The location of the centre of pressure (from the bottom of the door).
> (d) The required minimum number of dogging levers if each lever can resist **6 kN** of force.
> (e) The required door plate thickness if stiffener spacing is **d = 400 mm** and allowable bending stress is $\sigma_{allow} = 160$ MPa.

### (a) Hydrostatic Pressure at the Bottom

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{bottom} &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
&= 1025 \;\text{kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2 \times 4.5 \;\text{m} \\
&= 45{,}253.875 \;\text{N/m}^2 \\
p_{bottom} &\approx 45.25 \;\text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pressure at the **top** of the door is zero (free water surface assumed), and the pressure varies **linearly** with depth — a triangular pressure distribution.

### (b) Total Resultant Force on Door

For a vertical rectangular surface with the **top edge at the free water surface**, the average pressure acts at the centroid of the pressure prism:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{p} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.5 \\
&= 22{,}626.94 \;\text{Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total force is average pressure times the area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \bar{p} \cdot A = \frac{1}{2} \rho g h \cdot (W \cdot H) \\
&= 22{,}626.94 \;\text{Pa} \times (0.8 \;\text{m} \times 1.5 \;\text{m}) \\
&= 22{,}626.94 \times 1.2 \\
F &= 27{,}152.3 \;\text{N} \approx 27.15 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### (c) Location of Centre of Pressure

The centre of pressure for a triangular pressure distribution (zero at top, max at bottom) lies at **2/3 of the height from the top**, which is **1/3 from the bottom**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{cp} &= \frac{2}{3} \cdot H = \frac{2}{3} \times 1500 \;\text{mm} \\
y_{cp} &= 1000 \;\text{mm from the top of the door} \\
\text{or equivalently,} \quad y_{cp,bottom} &= \frac{1}{3} \cdot H = 500 \;\text{mm from the bottom}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The resultant 27.15 kN force acts at **1000 mm below the waterline**, which is also **500 mm above the bottom of the door**. This is the critical point for designing the dogging lever distribution — the lower dogs carry more load.

### (d) Minimum Number of Dogging Levers

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{per\;lever} &= 6 \;\text{kN} \\
n_{min} &= \left\lceil \frac{F_{total}}{F_{per\;lever}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{27.15}{6} \right\rceil \\
n_{min} &= \left\lceil 4.525 \right\rceil = 5 \;\text{dogging levers}
\end{aligned}
$$

In practice, designers use **6 levers** (3 on each vertical side) for even load distribution, providing a safety margin and redundancy.

### (e) Required Plate Thickness

Treating the door panel as a **rectangular plate simply supported on four sides** with uniform pressure $p$ (using average pressure for conservative design):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{max} &= \frac{k \cdot p \cdot d^2}{t^2} \\
\text{Solving for } t: \quad t &= d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot p}{\sigma_{allow}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $d/b \approx 0.8$ (where $b = W = 800$ mm, $d = 400$ mm) on a simply supported plate, the bending coefficient $k \approx 0.31$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= 400 \;\text{mm} \times \sqrt{\frac{0.31 \times 22{,}626.94}{160 \times 10^6}} \\
&= 400 \times \sqrt{\frac{7014.35}{1.6 \times 10^8}} \\
&= 400 \times \sqrt{4.384 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 400 \times 0.006621 \\
t &\approx 2.65 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding **2.0 mm corrosion allowance** and rounding up to the next standard plate gauge:

$$
t_{design} = 2.65 + 2.0 \approx 4.65 \;\text{mm} \rightarrow \text{use } \mathbf{6 \;\text{mm} \;\text{plate}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Door Types

| Parameter | Hinged WTD | Sliding WTD | Rolling WTD | Plug-Type WTD |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pressure rating** | Low–Medium | Medium–High | Medium | Very High |
| **Space requirement** | Swing arc (large) | Track length | Headroom (large) | Minimal radial |
| **Closure speed** | Slow | Fast (power) | Fast | Slow–Medium |
| **Seal reliability** | Moderate | High | High | Very High |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium–High | High | Very High |
| **Typical location** | Storerooms | Machinery spaces | Cargo holds | Submarines, deep tanks |
| **SOLAS power-op** | Optional | Mandatory in some cases | Optional | Always manual backup |

## 3.3 Design Verification Equations (Summary)

For any watertight door, the design check is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{induced} &= \frac{M_{max}}{Z_{panel}} \leq \sigma_{allow} \\
\delta_{gasket} &\geq \delta_{min,compression} \\
F_{dogs,total} &\geq F_{hydrostatic} \times \text{SF} \quad (\text{SF} = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0) \\
t_{closure} &\leq 60 \;\text{s (upright)}, \leq 30 \;\text{s (15° heel)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The factor of safety of **1.5 to 2.0** on dogging force is a common short-answer question. Always include the safety factor in your design force calculation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define a watertight door. List the four main types based on operating principle.**

**Model Answer:**

A **watertight door** is a structural access door fitted in a watertight bulkhead of a ship that, when closed and secured, prevents the passage of water between adjacent compartments under the designed hydrostatic head, while permitting normal transit of personnel and equipment.

The four main types based on operating principle are:
1. **Hinged watertight door** — swings on hinges, secured by peripheral dogs.
2. **Sliding watertight door** — moves horizontally on rollers along a track.
3. **Rolling watertight door** — rolls upward like a shutter into a housing.
4. **Plug-type watertight door** — circular door that seals by axial plug action against a circular frame.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is the function of a sill in a watertight door? State the minimum sill height as per SOLAS for subdivision bulkhead doors.**

**Model Answer:**

A **sill** is a raised threshold welded to the bottom of the door frame. Its function is to:
1. Provide a seating surface for the door panel and gasket.
2. Prevent water from flowing under the door when closed.
3. Maintain structural continuity of the bulkhead at the door opening.

As per **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 13**, the minimum sill height for subdivision bulkhead doors is **380 mm**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor (1.5–2.0)** on dogging force calculations — examiners specifically check this.
> 2. **Confusing "weathertight" with "watertight"** — these are distinct regulatory categories.
> 3. **Not stating the SOLAS regulation reference** — always cite **Reg. 13 / IACS S21** to gain full marks.
> 4. **Omitting units** in numerical answers — marks are routinely deducted for missing units.
> 5. **Skipping the sill height discussion** — this is a "must-mention" item for any WTD descriptive answer.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Sliding WTD Design

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**A sliding watertight door of clear opening 900 mm × 1600 mm is fitted in a machinery space transverse bulkhead. The maximum design head of water on the door is 4.8 m. Each dogging lever can resist 5 kN, and allowable bending stress for door plate is 165 MPa. Stiffener spacing is 450 mm. Seawater density is 1025 kg/m³.**

**(a) Calculate the total hydrostatic force on the door and determine the minimum number of dogging levers required with a safety factor of 1.8. (7 marks)**

**(b) Determine the required door plate thickness, and briefly explain the function of the sill and gasket in the door assembly. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Total Hydrostatic Force and Number of Dogs**

The pressure varies linearly from zero at the top to maximum at the bottom of the door. The door top is assumed at the free water surface (worst case for design head = 4.8 m).

Average pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{p} &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4.8 \\
&= 24{,}135.6 \;\text{Pa} \approx 24.14 \;\text{kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

Door area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= W \times H = 0.9 \times 1.6 = 1.44 \;\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Total force:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \bar{p} \cdot A = 24{,}135.6 \times 1.44 \\
F &= 34{,}755.3 \;\text{N} \approx 34.76 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Design force with safety factor:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{design} &= F \times SF = 34.76 \times 1.8 \\
F_{design} &= 62.56 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

Number of dogs (each rated 5 kN):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{min} &= \left\lceil \frac{F_{design}}{F_{per\;dog}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{62.56}{5} \right\rceil \\
n_{min} &= \left\lceil 12.51 \right\rceil = 13 \;\text{dogs}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical design uses 14 dogs** (7 on each side) for symmetric load distribution around the door perimeter.

**Centre of pressure location:**

$$
y_{cp} = \frac{2}{3} \times 1600 = 1066.7 \;\text{mm from top} = 533.3 \;\text{mm from bottom}
$$

**(b) Plate Thickness, Sill and Gasket Function**

For a plate simply supported on four sides with $d/b = 450/900 = 0.5$, the bending coefficient $k \approx 0.30$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{required} &= d \cdot \sqrt{\frac{k \cdot p}{\sigma_{allow}}} \\
&= 450 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.30 \times 24{,}135.6}{165 \times 10^6}} \\
&= 450 \times \sqrt{4.388 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 450 \times 0.006624 \\
t_{required} &\approx 2.98 \;\text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Adding **2.0 mm corrosion allowance**:

$$
t_{design} = 2.98 + 2.0 = 4.98 \;\text{mm} \rightarrow \text{use } \mathbf{6 \;\text{mm} \;\text{standard plate}}
$$

**Function of the sill:**

The sill is a **raised structural threshold** welded to the bottom of the door frame. It serves three purposes:
1. **Mechanical seating** — provides a flat surface on which the door panel and gasket rest.
2. **Watertight integrity** — prevents water from flowing under the door panel even if the gasket is slightly imperfect.
3. **Structural continuity** — maintains the strength of the bulkhead at the cut-out for the door.
For this machinery space subdivision door, the sill height should be at least **380 mm** per SOLAS Reg. 13.

**Function of the gasket:**

The gasket is a **continuous rubber sealing strip** (neoprene or nitrile) fitted around the perimeter of the door frame. Its functions:
1. **Primary watertight seal** — compresses against the door panel when dogs are engaged, blocking all water passage.
2. **Vibration damping** — absorbs minor impacts and structural deflections without losing seal.
3. **Compensates for surface irregularities** — the rubber conforms to small surface roughnesses on the panel and frame.

Gasket compression must be checked annually; replacement is required when **compression set exceeds 30%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — WTD Classification and SOLAS Requirements (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a) Classify watertight doors based on (i) operating principle, (ii) mode of operation, and (iii) pressure rating. Give one example application for each category. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the SOLAS closure time requirements for power-operated watertight doors, and describe with a sketch the constructional features of a sliding watertight door including the dogging arrangement. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Watertight Doors**

**(i) By Operating Principle:**

| Type | Description | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hinged WTD** | Door swings on hinges; dogs on perimeter. | Storeroom access, crew corridors. |
| **Sliding WTD** | Door moves horizontally on track. | Machinery spaces, passageways. |
| **Rolling WTD** | Door rolls upward into overhead housing. | Cargo hold access (bulk carriers). |
| **Plug-Type WTD** | Circular door plugs into circular frame. | Submarines, deep ballast tanks. |

**(ii) By Mode of Operation:**

| Mode | Description |
|---|---|
| **Manually Operated** | Handwheel and dogs, no external power. Used in low-traffic areas. |
| **Power Operated (Hydraulic)** | Hydraulic cylinder drives door; used for large/medium doors. |
| **Power Operated (Electric)** | Electric motor with rack-and-pinion drive. |
| **Remotely Operated** | Closed from bridge/ECR via indicator panel (SOLAS mandatory in passenger ships). |
| **Self-Closing / Fail-Safe** | Closes automatically on loss of power or flooding sensor trigger. |

**(iii) By Pressure Rating:**

| Class | Design Head Range | Typical Location |
|---|---|---|
| **Light WTD** | Below 2.5 m | Above bulkhead deck, dry compartments. |
| **Medium WTD** | 2.5 m to 4.5 m | Machinery spaces, main subdivision. |
| **Heavy WTD** | Above 4.5 m | Collision bulkhead, deep tanks. |

**(b) SOLAS Closure Time & Constructional Features**

**SOLAS Closure Time Requirements (Chapter II-1, Reg. 13):**

For power-operated watertight doors:
- Closure time in **upright ship condition**: **≤ 60 seconds**.
- Closure time in **15° heeled condition**: **≤ 30 seconds**.
- A **pre-warning alarm of at least 5 seconds and not more than 10 seconds** must sound before the door begins to close.
- The door must be **closeable from the bridge** in case of emergency.
- **Local control** must be possible from a position on either side of the door.
- **Indication** of door position (open/closed/locked) must be displayed on the bridge and in the engine control room.

**Constructional Features of a Sliding Watertight Door:**

A sliding watertight door consists of the following components:

1. **Door Panel**: A rectangular steel plate (6–10 mm thick), stiffened with welded T-bars or flat bars on the inside. The panel is sized slightly larger than the clear opening to overlap the frame on all sides, providing a sealing surface.

2. **Frame**: Built up from heavy bulb plate or angle sections, welded to the bulkhead. The frame is flush with the bulkhead plating and provides the seat for the gasket and the engaging notches for the dogging levers.

3. **Sill (Coaming)**: A raised threshold at the bottom of the frame, minimum 380 mm high. Forms a continuous watertight barrier at the bottom of the door.

4. **Gasket**: A continuous neoprene or nitrile rubber strip fitted in a groove on the frame (or on the door edge). Compresses against the door panel when dogs are engaged.

5. **Dogging Lever Assembly**: 4 to 8 eccentric levers spaced around the perimeter of the door. Each lever is driven by a central handwheel via a spider/cam mechanism. When the handwheel is rotated, all levers pivot simultaneously into the frame notches, compressing the gasket.

6. **Sliding Track and Rollers**: The door is suspended on rollers that run on an overhead track, allowing horizontal movement between open and closed positions.

7. **Operating Mechanism**: Either manual (handwheel and chain) or power-driven (hydraulic cylinder or electric motor with rack-and-pinion).

8. **Limit Switches and Indicators**: Magnetic or mechanical switches that signal the open, closed, and locked states to the bridge and ECR panels.

**Sketch Description (text-based):**

Imagine looking at the door from the front. You see a rectangular steel panel, slightly larger than the frame opening, fitted with a central handwheel on the inside face. Around the perimeter, six dogging levers point inward, each engaging a notch in the frame. At the bottom, a raised sill projects 380 mm upward. The door rests on rollers at the top, sliding along a horizontal track.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Watertight Doors**

### Core Definitions
- **Watertight Door (WTD):** Prevents water passage under design head in any direction (SOLAS Reg. 13).
- **Weathertight Door:** Resists sea spray and weather — external doors only.
- **Slamming Door:** Withstands green-sea impact loading.
- **Sill:** Raised threshold (≥ **380 mm**) to retain water at bottom of door.

### Four Operating Types
1. **Hinged** — swings; low pressure; storerooms.
2. **Sliding** — horizontal motion; most common in machinery spaces.
3. **Rolling** — rolls upward; cargo holds.
4. **Plug-Type** — circular; submarines and deep tanks.

### Three Modes of Operation
- **Manual** — handwheel and dogs.
- **Power** — hydraulic or electric.
- **Remote** — bridge/ECR control with indicators.

### SOLAS Time Limits
- **60 seconds** upright.
- **30 seconds** at **15° heel**.
- **5–10 seconds** pre-warning alarm.

### Design Essentials
- **Design head** $h$ gives $\bar{p} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g h$.
- **Total force** $F = \bar{p} \cdot W \cdot H$.
- **Centre of pressure** at $\tfrac{2}{3} H$ from the top.
- **Number of dogs** uses **SF = 1.5 to 2.0** on hydrostatic force.
- **Plate thickness** from plate bending formula with corrosion allowance.
- **Hose test** at **0.2 MPa** minimum; **head test** at **1.5× design head**.

### Regulatory References
- **SOLAS Chapter II-1, Regulation 13** — Closure and testing of WTDs.
- **IACS Unified Requirement UR S21** — Design and testing standards.
- **Class society rules** (DNV, LR, ABS, BV) — Additional survey and certification.

### Common KTU Traps
- Do **not** confuse **weathertight** with **watertight**.
- Always state the **SOLAS regulation number** in descriptive answers.
- Always include the **safety factor** in dogging force calculations.
- Always include **units** in numerical answers.
- **Sill height = 380 mm** is the SOLAS minimum — memorize it.
- **Closure time = 60 s upright, 30 s at 15° heel** — memorize it.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deep Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Deep Tank** is defined by classification societies (IACS members like IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, BV, ABS) as a tank that has a **depth of 1.8 m (6 ft) or more**, measured from the tank top to the tank bottom, and is intended to carry liquids such as fresh water, sea water (ballast), fuel oil, lubricating oil, or liquid cargo. Deep tanks that span the full breadth or depth of the ship are termed **Full-Depth Deep Tanks** or **Full-Breadth Deep Tanks**, and they play a dual role as both liquid-containment compartments and primary hull-strength members (contributing to longitudinal and transverse strength).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A deep tank is any tank exceeding **1.8 m (6 ft)** in depth, subjected to hydrostatic pressure that varies linearly with depth. They are generally bounded by the shell plating, deck plating, inner bottom, and transverse bulkheads, and require enhanced scantlings compared to dry spaces.

### 1.2 Classification of Deep Tanks

| Sl. No. | Type | Location | Primary Use |
|---------|------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Peak Tanks** | Forward / Aft of collision bulkhead / after peak | Trim ballast, emergency water |
| 2 | **Double Bottom Deep Tanks** | Between inner bottom and shell | Ballast, fresh water, fuel oil |
| 3 | **Wing Deep Tanks** | Between side shell and inner skin | Ballast, cargo oil |
| 4 | **Deep Cargo Tanks** | Within cargo hold region | Liquid cargo (tankers) |
| 5 | **Cofferdams** | Between two adjacent compartments | Isolation / Safety |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The Building Water Tank**
> Think of a tall overhead water tank in an apartment building. The bottom of the tank experiences much more pressure than the top because of the weight of water above it. Similarly, in a ship's deep tank, the **lower plating carries much greater hydrostatic pressure** than the upper plating. This is why deep tanks have **thicker plates and stronger stiffeners near the bottom**, just as the base of an overhead water tank is heavily reinforced.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Density of sea water: **ρ_sw = 1.025 t/m³**
- Density of fresh water: **ρ_fw = 1.000 t/m³**
- Density of fuel oil: **ρ_fo ≈ 0.85 t/m³**
- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Minimum deep tank depth (per IRS rules): **1.8 m**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principles

A deep tank in a ship operates under the following engineering principles:

- **Hydrostatic Pressure Principle:** Pressure at any depth *h* below the free surface of liquid is $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$.
- **Maximum Pressure at Bottom:** Occurs at the lowest point of the tank (near inner bottom), equal to $p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$, where *H* is the total depth of liquid.
- **Structural Integration:** Deep tanks are framed by the **inner hull (shell plating), deck plating, inner bottom plating, and transverse bulkheads** — all of which must resist both hydrostatic pressure and external wave slamming.
- **Stiffener Orientation:** Stiffeners (T-bars, flat bars, or bulb plates) are arranged vertically on transverse bulkheads to resist bending caused by pressure.
- **Special Bulkheads:** **Wash bulkheads** (perforated or slotted) are fitted in very large tanks to dampen sloshing effects; **swash bulkheads** reduce free-surface effect on stability.

### 2.2 Design Pressure Determination

The design pressure for deep tank scantlings is taken as the **maximum of:**

1. Static head due to filling (1.025 t/m³ for sea water ballast)
2. Pressure due to combined head plus accelerations (sloshing)

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Unit |
|---------|-----------|---------|------|
| 1 | Hydrostatic pressure at depth *h* | $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | kN/m² |
| 2 | Maximum pressure at tank bottom | $p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ | kN/m² |
| 3 | Plate thickness (IRS rule) | $t = s \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{p / \sigma} + t_k$ | mm |
| 4 | Section modulus of stiffener | $Z = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{m \cdot \sigma}$ | cm³ |
| 5 | Web height of stiffener | $h_w = (4.2 + 0.07 \cdot l) \cdot \sqrt[3]{Z}$ | mm |
| 6 | Web thickness | $t_w = 0.015 \cdot h_w + 2.5$ | mm |
| 7 | Flange breadth | $b_f = 6.2 \cdot h_w / 100 + 25$ | mm |
| 8 | Flange thickness | $t_f = 0.6 \cdot t_w$ | mm |
| 9 | Pressure (in m head of water) | $h = p / (\rho \cdot g)$ | m |
| 10 | Specific gravity × head | $p = 10 \cdot s$ (approx) | kN/m² |

Where:
- *s* = stiffener spacing (mm)
- *l* = stiffener span (mm)
- *p* = design pressure (kN/m²)
- *σ* = allowable stress (N/mm²)
- *k* = form factor
- *m* = boundary condition factor
- *t_k* = corrosion addition

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural Safety:** Deep tank scantlings ensure the hull does not rupture under cargo loading or ballast operations.
- **Longitudinal Strength:** Full-depth deep tanks act as box girders contributing to the ship's hull girder bending resistance.
- **Stability & Trim:** Ballast deep tanks allow fine-tuning of ship's trim and transverse stability.
- **Damage Control:** Properly designed deep tanks limit flooding propagation in case of hull breach.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU exams, you will always be given the design pressure at a specific level. Always draw the **pressure prism** (a triangular diagram showing pressure increasing with depth) before solving any stiffener/plate problem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hydrostatic Pressure in a Deep Tank

Consider a deep tank filled with sea water of density $\rho = 1.025 \text{ t/m}^3$ to a total depth $H$ meters.

**Step 1: Pressure at a depth *h* from the free surface**

The weight of the column of liquid above any point per unit area gives the pressure.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \text{Weight density} \times \text{height of liquid column} \\
p &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the fundamental hydrostatic equation derived from Pascal's principle.

**Step 2: Substitute numerical values**

For sea water, $\rho \cdot g = 1.025 \times 9.81 = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Thus,

$$
p = 10.055 \cdot h \;\; \text{(kN/m}^2\text{)}
$$

For fresh water, $\rho \cdot g = 1.000 \times 9.81 = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Thus,

$$
p = 9.81 \cdot h \;\; \text{(kN/m}^2\text{)}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum pressure at the bottom of the tank**

Substituting $h = H$ (total depth):

$$
p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H
$$

For example, if $H = 6 \text{ m}$ of sea water:

$$
p_{max} = 10.055 \times 6 = 60.33 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Plate Thickness (Stiffened Panel)

A plate panel between two stiffeners, simply supported on all four edges, with pressure $p$ acting perpendicular to its surface.

**Step 1: Bending stress in plate**

The maximum bending moment per unit width of the plate is:

$$
M = \frac{p \cdot s^2}{8}
$$

where *s* is the shorter span (stiffener spacing).

**Step 2: Section modulus per unit width**

For a plate of unit width and thickness *t*:

$$
Z_{plate} = \frac{1 \cdot t^2}{6}
$$

**Step 3: Equating bending stress to allowable**

$$
\sigma = \frac{M}{Z_{plate}} = \frac{p \cdot s^2 / 8}{t^2 / 6} = \frac{6 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{8 \cdot t^2}
$$

**Step 4: Solving for plate thickness *t***

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{3 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{4 \cdot t^2} \\
t^2 &= \frac{3 \cdot p \cdot s^2}{4 \cdot \sigma} \\
t &= s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{3 \cdot p}{4 \cdot \sigma}} \\
t &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $k = \sqrt{3/4} = 0.866$ (form factor for simply supported plate).

**Step 5: Including corrosion addition $t_k$**

$$
t_{required} = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}} + t_k
$$

For IRS rules, a typical value is $t_k = 1.0 \text{ mm}$ for deep tanks.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A deep tank has a depth of 5 m and is filled with sea water. Calculate (a) the pressure at the bottom, (b) the required plate thickness for the bottom plating if stiffener spacing is 600 mm and allowable stress is 160 N/mm². Use form factor *k* = 0.866 and corrosion addition = 1 mm.

**Solution (a): Pressure at the bottom**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \rho \cdot g \cdot h \\
p &= 10.055 \times 5 \\
p &= 50.275 \text{ kN/m}^2 \\
p &= 50.275 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
p &= 0.05028 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b): Plate thickness**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}} + t_k \\
t &= 0.866 \times 600 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.05028}{160}} + 1 \\
t &= 519.6 \times \sqrt{0.0003142} + 1 \\
t &= 519.6 \times 0.01773 + 1 \\
t &= 9.21 + 1 \\
t &= 10.21 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the standard plate thickness adopted = **11 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Deep Tank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Ship Hull Structure] --> nodeB[Deep Tank Compartment]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Top Boundary: Deck Plating]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Bottom Boundary: Inner Bottom Plating]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Side Boundaries: Shell Plating]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Transverse Bulkheads]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Ordinary Bulkhead]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Wash Bulkhead]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Swash Bulkhead]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[Stiffener Network]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Vertical Stiffeners on Bulkheads]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Horizontal Girders]
    nodeJ --> nodeM[Brackets at Connections]
    nodeB --> nodeN[Liquid Loading]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Hydrostatic Pressure Increases with Depth]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Pressure Prism: Triangular Distribution]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Maximum Scantling at Bottom]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Plate Thickness t]
    nodeQ --> nodeS[Stiffener Section Modulus Z]
```

### 4.2 Pressure Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Free Surface of Liquid at top] --> nodeB[Pressure = 0 kN per square m]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Depth h1: Pressure p1]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Depth h2: Pressure p2]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Bottom Depth H: Pressure pmax]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Zero Pressure Line]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Maximum Pressure Line]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Pressure Prism - Triangular Shape]
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

### 4.3 Typical Deep Tank Arrangement (Cross Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Deck Plating at Top] --> nodeB[Upper Stiffener Ring at 1.8 m from top]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Intermediate Stiffener Ring]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Lower Stiffener Ring - Heavier Section]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Bottom Corner Bracket]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Inner Bottom Plating - Thickest]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Tank Side Shell]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Bilge Region]
    nodeH --> nodeF
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The above diagrams illustrate that the **scantlings (plate thickness and stiffener section modulus) progressively increase from the top of the tank to the bottom**, mirroring the linearly increasing hydrostatic pressure. The bottom corner bracket is a critical structural element resisting shear and bending.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1: Define a deep tank. State the minimum depth criteria.**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A deep tank is defined as any tank in a ship having a **depth of 1.8 m (6 ft) or more**, measured from the top of the tank to the bottom. Deep tanks are used for carrying liquids such as ballast water, fresh water, fuel oil, lubricating oil, or liquid cargo.

They are subjected to **high hydrostatic pressure** that varies linearly from zero at the free surface to a maximum at the bottom, equal to $p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H$.

Such tanks require **enhanced scantlings** for plating and stiffeners compared to dry spaces of similar dimensions, since the structural integrity must withstand the pressure at the deepest point as well as dynamic loads from ship motion.

---

**Question 2: What is a wash bulkhead? State its purpose.**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **wash bulkhead** is a perforated or corrugated transverse bulkhead fitted within a deep tank to **reduce the adverse effects of sloshing** of the liquid cargo during ship motion.

**Purposes:**

1. It breaks the free surface of the liquid, thereby reducing the **free-surface effect** on the ship's stability.
2. It dampens the violent oscillation of liquid, preventing structural damage to tank boundaries.
3. It improves the effectiveness of ballast operations by reducing dynamic loads.

Wash bulkheads are typically made with slots, holes, or corrugations to allow free passage of liquid while providing structural continuity.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3]`

**(a)** A double-bottom deep tank is 4 m deep and is filled with sea water. Calculate the hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the tank. Also determine the pressure at 1 m and 3 m depths from the free surface.

**(b)** For the same tank, determine the required plate thickness for the bottom plating if the stiffener spacing is 500 mm and the allowable bending stress is 150 N/mm². Use a form factor of 0.866 and corrosion addition of 1.5 mm. State the standard plate thickness to be adopted.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Pressure Calculations:**

Given:
- Depth of tank, $H = 4$ m
- Sea water, $\rho \cdot g = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$

Pressure at depth *h*:

$$
p = 10.055 \cdot h
$$

**At bottom (h = 4 m):**

$$
p_{max} = 10.055 \times 4 = 40.22 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**At h = 1 m:**

$$
p_1 = 10.055 \times 1 = 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**At h = 3 m:**

$$
p_2 = 10.055 \times 3 = 30.165 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

| Depth h (m) | Pressure p (kN/m²) |
|-------------|---------------------|
| 1.0 | 10.055 |
| 3.0 | 30.165 |
| 4.0 | 40.22 |

---

**Part (b) — Plate Thickness Calculation:**

Given:
- Pressure at bottom, $p = 40.22 \text{ kN/m}^2 = 0.04022 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Stiffener spacing, $s = 500$ mm
- Allowable stress, $\sigma = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- Form factor, $k = 0.866$
- Corrosion addition, $t_k = 1.5$ mm

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p}{\sigma}} + t_k \\
t &= 0.866 \times 500 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.04022}{150}} + 1.5 \\
t &= 433 \times \sqrt{0.0002681} + 1.5 \\
t &= 433 \times 0.01637 + 1.5 \\
t &= 7.09 + 1.5 \\
t &= 8.59 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard plate thickness adopted = 9 mm** (rounded up to the next available standard size).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

> `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the classification of deep tanks based on location in a ship. State the function of each type.

**(b)** A peak deep tank is 6 m deep and is filled with fresh water. Determine (i) the maximum hydrostatic pressure at the bottom, and (ii) the section modulus required for a vertical stiffener on the transverse bulkhead if the stiffener span is 3 m and spacing is 700 mm. Take allowable stress = 165 N/mm².

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification of Deep Tanks:**

Deep tanks are classified based on their **location within the hull** as follows:

1. **Peak Deep Tanks:** Located in the fore peak and aft peak, between the collision bulkhead (or after peak bulkhead) and the stem (or stern frame). Used for trim ballast and emergency water.

2. **Double-Bottom Deep Tanks:** Formed between the inner bottom plating and the bottom shell plating. Used primarily for ballast water and fuel oil storage.

3. **Wing Deep Tanks:** Located between the side shell plating and the longitudinal bulkhead. Used for ballast or cargo oil in tankers.

4. **Cargo Deep Tanks:** Located within the cargo region of tankers, designed specifically for liquid cargo (crude oil, chemicals).

5. **Cofferdams:** Small double-walled spaces between two adjacent tanks, used to prevent mixing of incompatible liquids and for safety isolation.

**Function:** All deep tanks serve the dual purpose of liquid storage and contribution to the ship's **longitudinal and transverse structural strength**.

---

**Part (b) — Section Modulus Calculation:**

Given:
- Depth, $H = 6$ m
- Fresh water, $\rho \cdot g = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- Stiffener span, $l = 3$ m = 3000 mm
- Stiffener spacing, $s = 700$ mm
- Allowable stress, $\sigma = 165 \text{ N/mm}^2$

**(i) Maximum hydrostatic pressure:**

$$
p_{max} = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 9.81 \times 6 = 58.86 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Converting to N/mm²:

$$
p_{max} = 0.05886 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

**(ii) Section modulus required:**

For a simply supported stiffener:

$$
Z = \frac{p \cdot s \cdot l^2}{m \cdot \sigma}
$$

Taking $m = 8$ for simply supported beam:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z &= \frac{0.05886 \times 700 \times (3000)^2}{8 \times 165} \\
Z &= \frac{0.05886 \times 700 \times 9 \times 10^6}{1320} \\
Z &= \frac{3.7082 \times 10^8}{1320} \\
Z &= 280,924 \text{ mm}^3 \\
Z &= 280.92 \text{ cm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Required section modulus Z ≈ 281 cm³.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert pressure from kN/m² to N/mm² before substituting into the formula. (1 kN/m² = 10⁻³ N/mm²). This is the **#1 reason for losing 2-3 marks** in deep tank problems.
>
> 2. **Confusing Form Factor:** Using the wrong form factor (*k* = 0.866 for simply supported, *k* = 1.0 for clamped). Always state the boundary condition assumed.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Corrosion Addition:** Students often forget to add $t_k$, leading to a deficit in plate thickness by 1-1.5 mm.
>
> 4. **Boundary Condition Factor *m*:** Using *m* = 8 for simply supported but the stiffener in a deep tank is usually continuous; partial fixity gives *m* ≈ 12-13.
>
> 5. **Standard Rounding:** Always round UP the calculated plate thickness to the next standard thickness (e.g., 8.59 mm → 9 mm; 10.21 mm → 11 mm). Never round down.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **deep tank** is any tank with depth ≥ **1.8 m (6 ft)**.
- [x] Hydrostatic pressure follows $p = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$, increasing **linearly with depth**.
- [x] For **sea water**: $\rho \cdot g \approx 10.055 \text{ kN/m}^3$; for **fresh water**: $9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3$.
- [x] Plate thickness formula: $t = k \cdot s \cdot \sqrt{p/\sigma} + t_k$.
- [x] Section modulus formula: $Z = p \cdot s \cdot l^2 / (m \cdot \sigma)$.
- [x] Scantlings are **heaviest at the bottom** of the deep tank.
- [x] **Wash bulkheads** reduce sloshing effects; **swash bulkheads** reduce free-surface effect.
- [x] **Full-depth deep tanks** also contribute to hull girder strength.
- [x] Always convert units: **1 kN/m² = 10⁻³ N/mm²**.
- [x] Always round plate thickness **up** to the next standard size.
- [x] Common tank types: **peak, double-bottom, wing, cargo, cofferdam**.
- [x] IRS allowable stresses: typically **150-165 N/mm²** for mild steel in deep tanks.
- [x] Corrosion addition for deep tanks: **1.0 to 1.5 mm** (depending on service).
- [x] Deep tanks must satisfy **damage stability** requirements for ballast operations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of Deep Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST614 - BASIC SHIP STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Testing of Deep Tanks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Deep Tank** in shipbuilding is defined by classification societies as any tank that has a **depth (height) greater than 1.5 m (or as per society rules)** and is used for the carriage of water ballast, fuel oil, lubricating oil, or other liquid cargo. A tank is specifically classified as a *deep tank* when its **depth exceeds 1/6th of its breadth** (or 1/6th of its transverse extent) as per IRS (Indian Register of Shipping) and IACS unified requirements. The structural testing of these tanks is mandatory after fabrication and during new construction to verify **leaktightness, structural integrity, and weld soundness** under simulated hydrostatic loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Testing of deep tanks is governed by IACS Unified Requirements **UR S21** (Special Service Ships), **S22** (Testing Procedures), and **Z17** (Procedural Requirements for Service Notations). Classification societies like IRS, Lloyd's Register, DNV, and BV have near-identical test requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Standard (IACS UR S21):** A tank with a length/depth ratio such that the hydrostatic head produces stresses requiring strengthened internal framing is termed a deep tank. Typically, if depth $\geq 1.5 \text{ m}$ or $\geq 1/6$ of breadth, it qualifies.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **large plastic water tank** at home that holds 1000 litres. Before the manufacturer sells it, they fill it completely with water to see if any seams leak or the walls bulge out. If the walls hold the water pressure without leaking, the tank is approved.

A **ship's deep tank** works on exactly the same principle, but scaled up enormously:
- A typical ship's deep tank can hold **hundreds of tonnes of water** (sea water ballast).
- The water exerts **enormous pressure** on the bottom and side plates.
- The tank has dozens of **welded seams** joining plates together — these welds are the weakest points.
- Therefore, after the tank is built (during new construction), it is filled with water and held under pressure to verify it is leak-free and structurally sound.

**Why is testing critical?** Because once the ship sails, the tank may carry fuel or ballast. If it leaks, it can:
1. Cause **flooding** (if ballast) — sinking risk.
2. Cause **oil pollution** (if fuel) — environmental disaster and heavy fines.
3. Weaken surrounding hull structure through **corrosion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of deep tank testing as the **"stress test" for a ship's stomach.** Just like a doctor pumps your stomach with air to check for ulcers, the shipyard fills the tank with water under pressure to check for weld "ulcers" (cracks/leaks).

---

### 1.3 Standard Test Metrics (Highlighted Constants)

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Standard test head | **2.45 m above the tank top** (i.e., 2.45 m above the highest point of the tank) |
| Or pressure equivalent | **24 kPa (0.24 bar) minimum** at the top of the tank |
| Test medium | **Sea water or fresh water** (never oil for tightness test) |
| Test duration | **Typically 15–30 minutes** under sustained head |
| Safety factor on yield | **1.0 (elastic)** to **1.5 (ultimate)** for different test types |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Deep Tank Testing:** The test head of **2.45 m of water** (≈ 24 kPa) above the tank top is the most universally accepted pressure per IACS UR Z17.1. Some societies (Lloyd's) also accept **0.5 m head above the design head** for hydrostatic tests.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Deep Tanks Tested?

Deep tanks are critical structural elements because they:
1. **Carry high-density liquids** (sea water ballast at ρ ≈ 1025 kg/m³).
2. **Experience large hydrostatic pressures** due to depth.
3. Are subject to **sloshing loads** when partially filled.
4. Form part of the **hull girder** structure (contribute to hull strength).
5. Must satisfy **SOLAS** (Safety of Life at Sea) and **MARPOL** (Marine Pollution) requirements.

A failure in a deep tank can compromise:
- **Watertight integrity** (flooding risk).
- **Structural strength** (plate buckling, weld cracking).
- **Environmental compliance** (oil pollution).

---

### 2.2 Types of Tests Performed on Deep Tanks

Classification societies require a combination of the following tests:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Mandatory Tests:**
> 1. **Tightness Test (Leak Test)** — verifies no leakage through joints.
> 2. **Strength Test (Hydrostatic Test)** — verifies structural adequacy.

#### A. Hydrostatic Test (Strength Test)
- The tank is **completely filled with water** and pressurized to a specified head.
- All penetrations (manholes, sounding pipes) are sealed.
- The tank is **held under pressure for a specified time** (usually 15–30 min).
- Visual inspection is conducted for:
  - Permanent deformation (bulging, sagging).
  - Weld cracks.
  - Leakage at joints.
- **Test Head Formula:** As per IACS UR Z17.1:

$$
h_{test} = h_{design} + 1.0 \text{ m (minimum)}
$$

where:
- $h_{design}$ = maximum design liquid head above the tank top (or 2.45 m above top, whichever is greater).

#### B. Hose Test (Tightness Test)
- Conducted **after the hydrostatic test** or as an alternative for smaller tanks.
- A pressurized water jet is sprayed onto all welded joints.
- The person spraying stands **not more than 1.5 m** from the joint.
- Water pressure: typically **2 bar minimum**.
- Any leak is identified by visible water seepage on the dry side.

#### C. Air Test (Pneumatic Test)
- Used **only when water testing is impractical** (e.g., tank has been coated or insulation installed).
- The tank is pressurized with **air at 0.15–0.2 bar**.
- Soap solution is applied to all joints.
- Bubbles indicate leaks.
- **Safety:** Pneumatic tests are dangerous due to stored energy; only conducted with strict safety protocols.

#### D. Vacuum Test
- Used for **double-bottom tanks** and certain void spaces.
- The tank is partially evacuated (vacuum).
- Atmospheric pressure on the outside verifies integrity.

#### E. Ultrasonic / NDT (Non-Destructive Testing)
- **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** — detects internal flaws in welds.
- **Radiographic Testing (RT)** — X-ray or gamma-ray inspection of butt welds.
- **Magnetic Particle Testing (MT)** — for surface cracks in welds.
- **Dye Penetrant Testing (PT)** — for surface defects.

---

### 2.3 Structural Background: Why Depth Matters

The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of a tank filled to height $H$ is:

$$
P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H
$$

For sea water ($\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$):

$$
P \approx 10.05 \cdot H \quad [\text{kPa}]
$$

So a 10 m deep tank experiences ~**100 kPa (1 bar)** at the bottom — this is significant and can:
- Cause **plate bending** between stiffeners.
- Cause **stiffener yielding**.
- Cause **weld cracking** at corners.

The tank must be designed (stiffened) to withstand these loads **with a safety factor**, and the test verifies this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Deep tank testing is the **last line of defense** in shipbuilding quality control. Even with strict fabrication tolerances (1–2 mm), welding introduces residual stresses and possible defects. Testing catches what NDT might miss and provides a full-scale, real-load verification.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units |
|---|---------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \rho g H$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $H$ | Pa |
| 2 | $h_{test} = h_{design} + 1.0 \text{ m}$ | IACS UR Z17.1 test head (minimum 2.45 m above top) | m of water |
| 3 | $P_{test} = 9.81 \cdot h_{test}$ | Test pressure for **fresh water** (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) | kPa |
| 4 | $P_{test} = 10.05 \cdot h_{test}$ | Test pressure for **sea water** (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) | kPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{6 M}{t^2}$ | Bending stress in plate per unit width | Pa |
| 6 | $p_{ult} = \dfrac{4 \sigma_y t^2}{L^2}$ | Collapse pressure of simply supported plate (Bryan) | Pa |
| 7 | Test duration | 15–30 min for hydrostatic; NDT before filling | min |
| 8 | Hose test pressure | ≥ 2 bar, nozzle ≤ 1.5 m from joint | bar, m |
| 9 | Air test pressure | 0.15–0.20 bar (gauge) | bar |
| 10 | Safety factor on yield for test | 1.0 (elastic, no permanent set expected) | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Units:** In shipbuilding, **test head** is almost always expressed in **metres of water column** (not in bar or kPa). This is because the head is directly related to the height of water filled in the tank.

---

### 2.5 Acceptance Criteria

After testing, the tank is approved if:
1. **No leakage** is observed at any welded joint, pipe connection, or penetrations.
2. **No permanent deformation** of plates, stiffeners, or bulkheads.
3. **No cracks** in welds or parent metal.
4. **No excessive bulging** (typically deflection limit is $L/250$ or as per society rules).
5. **All penetrations remain watertight.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the **hydrostatic test head** (2.45 m above tank top) with the **design head** (top of fill level). The test head is **always greater** than the design head — it's a "proof load."
