# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define the term "Stern Tube Bearing" and state the standard length-to-diameter ratio used in its design. Why is this ratio important?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A stern tube bearing is a **white metal (Babbitt) lined bearing** fitted inside the stern tube to support the propeller shaft where it passes through the hull. The standard KTU/IACS ratio is **L = 2 D_s**, where L is the bearing length and D_s is the propeller shaft diameter.

This ratio is important because:
1. It ensures **hydrodynamic oil film formation** between the shaft and bearing liner, preventing metal-to-metal contact.
2. It limits the **bearing pressure** to ≤ 1.0 N/mm², providing adequate safety margin against wear and seizure.
3. It keeps the bearing length within practical limits (longer bearings require larger stern tubes and reduce hull space).

**(Full 3 marks)** — Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for ratio, 1 mark for the three importance points (any 1 accepted).

---

### Question A2
**Q: Differentiate between a P-Bracket and an A-Bracket. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | P-Bracket | A-Bracket |
|---|---|---|
| Arms | Single (one arm) | Two (or more) |
| Shape | Letter "P" (side view) | Letter "A" (aft view) |
| Load handling | Mainly vertical | Vertical + lateral |
| Drag | Lower | Higher |

**P-Bracket Advantage:** Lower hydrodynamic drag, simpler fabrication.
**P-Bracket Disadvantage:** Poor lateral load handling — single arm bends sideways under side force.

**A-Bracket Advantage:** Symmetric load distribution — handles lateral forces via balanced couple.
**A-Bracket Disadvantage:** Higher drag, more complex fabrication, more cavitation risk.

**(Full 3 marks)** — Award 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark each for advantage and disadvantage.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Q: (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and functions of a **Stern Tube Assembly** of a modern single-screw merchant vessel. Explain the lubrication and sealing arrangements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ship has a propeller shaft of diameter **420 mm**. The bearing reaction load on the stern tube bearing is **520 kN**. Calculate the required bearing length, assuming a permissible bearing pressure of **1.0 N/mm²**. Verify if the L/D ratio conforms to the KTU/IACS 2:1 standard. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — Construction and Functions (7 marks)

**Stern Tube — Definition (1 mark):**
The stern tube is a watertight, cylindrical casing fitted through the aft part of the hull, supporting the propeller shaft and housing the after-most shaft bearing.

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Material**: Cast steel (older) or fabricated rolled steel plates welded (modern). Inside is lined with a **white metal (Babbitt)** bearing sleeve.
2. **Length & Diameter**: Typically 5–15 m long, outer diameter = 1.4 to 1.8 × shaft diameter.
3. **Connections**: Forward end connects to the plummer block foundation; aft end connects to the stern frame via fitted bolts and a sealing ring.
4. **Access**: Inspection covers at forward and aft ends for bearing condition checks.

**Functions (1 mark):**
1. Supports the propeller shaft and aligns it with the thrust block and intermediate shaft.
2. Provides a watertight passage through the hull.
3. Houses the after-most shaft bearing.

**Lubrication (1 mark):**
- **Oil-lubricated** system: Gravity-feed tank above the waterline supplies oil to the bearing. Oil returns via a drain, settles in a sump tank, is pumped, cooled, and filtered. Two independent pumps (duty + standby) provide redundancy per SOLAS.
- **Water-lubricated** system: Sea water from a sea chest enters the forward end, passes through the bearing, and exits aft. Used for environmental compliance (US EPA VGP).

**Sealing (1 mark):**
- **Forward Seal (Stuffing Box)**: Soft packing rings tightened via a gland follower; prevents oil leak into engine room.
- **Aft Seal**: Modern **mechanical face seal** (e.g., Burgmann, John Crane) with two barriers for redundancy; prevents sea water ingress.

**Sketch (place a labelled side-elevation drawing of the stern tube assembly showing all components — please draw on your answer paper; minimum 8 labels required for full marks).**

#### Model Solution (b) — Bearing Length Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Shaft diameter $D_s = 420$ mm
- Bearing reaction $W_b = 520$ kN $= 520{,}000$ N
- Permissible bearing pressure $P_{v,\text{perm}} = 1.0$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Bearing Pressure Formula (1 mark):**

$$P_v = \frac{W_b}{L \times D_s}$$

**Step 2: Rearrange for L (1 mark):**

$$L = \frac{W_b}{P_{v,\text{perm}} \cdot D_s}$$

**Step 3: Substitute and calculate (2 marks):**

$$L = \frac{520{,}000}{1.0 \times 420} = 1238.1 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Compute the L/D ratio (1 mark):**

$$\frac{L}{D_s} = \frac{1238.1}{420} = 2.95$$

**Step 5: Conclusion (2 marks):**
- The KTU/IACS 2:1 standard specifies **L ≥ 2 D_s** as the **minimum** for hydrodynamic film formation.
- Since $L/D_s = 2.95 \ge 2.0$, the design **conforms** to the standard.
- The bearing is **over-designed** by ~47% in length, providing extra safety margin for shock loads and wear.
- The actual bearing pressure achieved is:
$$P_v = \frac{520{,}000}{1238.1 \times 420} = 1.00 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (matches design value — consistent)}$$

**Final Answer: L = 1238 mm (adopt 1240 mm standard size); L/D = 2.95, conforms to ≥ 2 standard.**

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**Q: (a)** With neat sketches, explain the **P-Bracket** and **A-Bracket** supports for a propeller shaft. Compare their suitability for different vessel types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A P-bracket arm is **1.6 m long**, supporting a vertical load of **90 kN** at the shaft boss. The arm has a rectangular cross-section **180 mm wide × 280 mm deep**. The material is **cast manganese bronze** with permissible bending stress of **100 N/mm²**. Check if the section is safe. If not, suggest a revised depth. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a) — P-Bracket vs. A-Bracket (7 marks)

**P-Bracket — Description (1.5 marks):**
- A single streamlined strut projecting from the shaft bossing of a single-screw vessel.
- Located approximately **15–20% of the shaft overhang** forward of the propeller boss.
- The arm has an **airfoil/elliptical cross-section** to minimize drag.
- Resembles the letter "P" when viewed from the side.
- The strut is angled slightly downward so thrust reaction puts it in pure compression.

**A-Bracket — Description (1.5 marks):**
- A two-armed (or four-armed) symmetrical strut projecting from the shaft bossing.
- Each arm is hydrodynamically faired (airfoil section).
- The arms support the shaft at the **intersection point** just forward of the propeller.
- Resembles the letter "A" when viewed from astern.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Parameter | P-Bracket | A-Bracket |
|---|---|---|
| Drag | Lower | Higher |
| Lateral load | Poor (single arm bends) | Excellent (balanced) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Cavitation | Less | More |

**Suitability (2 marks):**
- **P-Bracket** is best for **warships, high-speed craft, and single-screw vessels** where minimizing drag is critical.
- **A-Bracket** is best for **merchant vessels, tankers, bulk carriers, and twin-screw with center shaft** where handling large lateral forces and providing a robust bearing is critical.

**Sketches (place drawings on answer paper; minimum 2 views each for P and A brackets, with labels).**

#### Model Solution (b) — P-Bracket Strength Check (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Strut length: $\ell = 1.6$ m $= 1600$ mm
- Load: $W = 90$ kN $= 90{,}000$ N
- Cross-section: $b = 180$ mm, $d = 280$ mm
- Material: Cast Mn-Br, $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 100$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Bending Moment (1 mark):**
$$M = W \times \ell = 90{,}000 \times 1600 = 1.44 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Section Modulus (1 mark):**
$$Z = \frac{b \cdot d^2}{6} = \frac{180 \times 280^2}{6} = \frac{180 \times 78{,}400}{6} = 2.352 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Bending Stress (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^8}{2.352 \times 10^6} = 61.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Compare with permissible (1 mark):**
- $\sigma_b = 61.2 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $\sigma_{\text{perm}} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- $\sigma_b < \sigma_{\text{perm}}$ → **Section is SAFE**.
- Factor of safety = 100 / 61.2 = **1.63** (slightly low for marine; recommended FoS ≥ 2.0; KTU examiners may ask you to revise).

**Step 5: If a revised depth is needed (assuming we want FoS = 2.0, so $\sigma_{\text{perm,design}} = 50$ N/mm²) (3 marks):**

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{6 W \ell}{b \cdot \sigma_{b,\text{design}}}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 90{,}000 \times 1600}{180 \times 50}}$$

$$d = \sqrt{\frac{8.64 \times 10^8}{9000}} = \sqrt{96{,}000} = 309.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $d = 310$ mm** (standard size).

**Verification with revised depth:**
$$Z_{\text{new}} = \frac{180 \times 310^2}{6} = 2.883 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$\sigma_{b,\text{new}} = \frac{1.44 \times 10^8}{2.883 \times 10^6} = 49.95 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 50 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer: Original section (180 × 280) is safe with FoS = 1.63, but recommended revised depth = 310 mm for FoS = 2.0.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (and Where Marks Are Lost):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kN to N** in numerical problems. If the formula expects N and you substitute kN, your answer is **off by 1000× — zero marks for that part**.
> 2. **Mixing up "2:1 ratio" interpretation.** Some students think L = D/2. The correct rule is **L = 2 D_s**, where L is bearing length and D_s is shaft diameter. Confusing this loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing P-Bracket and A-Bracket placement.** P-bracket is **single-arm (lower)**, A-bracket is **multi-arm (upper-lower or N-S-E-W)**. Examiners award 0 for swapped identification.
> 4. **Sketches without labels.** A sketch without leader-line labels (with name of each component) gets **at most 50% marks** for the sketch portion. Always label the bossing, seal, bearing, stuffing box, etc.
> 5. **Omitting the section modulus derivation.** KTU loves to see the formula $Z = b d^2 / 6$ **derived**, not just substituted. Skipping the derivation loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the units in final answers.** Always state **mm** or **N/mm²** explicitly.
> 7. **Stern Tube vs. Plummer Block confusion.** Plummer block is **inside the engine room** (intermediate shaft bearing); Stern Tube is **inside the hull aft** (propeller shaft bearing). Don't mix them up.
> 8. **Gudgeon vs. Pintle confusion.** Gudgeon is on the **stern frame**; Pintle is on the **rudder**. They fit together (pintle drops into gudgeon).
> 9. **Not mentioning SOLAS / IACS in theory answers.** Examiners award bonus marks for classification-society awareness.
> 10. **Shaft Bossing ≠ Stern Frame.** Shaft bossing is the **faired hull extension**; Stern Frame is the **rearmost frame casting**. Different parts!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Print This for Last-Minute Review!**

### Definitions (Must Know Verbatim)
- ☐ **Stern Tube** = watertight cylindrical casing supporting the propeller shaft where it exits the hull.
- ☐ **Stern Tube Bearing** = white metal liner inside the stern tube; $L = 2 D_s$.
- ☐ **Stuffing Box** = forward seal of stern tube (oil side); packing gland.
- ☐ **After Seal** = aft seal of stern tube (sea water side); mechanical face seal.
- ☐ **Shaft Bossing** = faired, doubler-plated hull extension around the propeller shaft aperture.
- ☐ **P-Bracket** = single-arm strut supporting the shaft forward of the propeller; "P"-shape side view.
- ☐ **A-Bracket** = two- or four-arm symmetrical strut; "A"-shape aft view.
- ☐ **Stern Frame (Aframe)** = rearmost frame; has main piece, propeller post, horseshoe, gudgeon.
- ☐ **Gudgeon** = socket on stern frame; holds the rudder pintle.
- ☐ **Pintle** = pin on the rudder; drops into the gudgeon.
- ☐ **Thrust Block** = absorbs propeller thrust and transfers it to the hull.

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- ☐ $L_{\text{bearing}} = 2 D_s$
- ☐ $P_v = \dfrac{W_b}{L \cdot D_s}$
- ☐ $M = W \times \ell$ (cantilever)
- ☐ $Z = \dfrac{b d^2}{6}$
- ☐ $\sigma_b = \dfrac{6 W \ell}{b d^2}$
- ☐ $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi D_s^3}$
- ☐ $L_o / D_s = 2.5$ to $4.0$ (shaft overhang ratio)
- ☐ $t_{\text{wall, stern tube}} = 0.05 D_{\text{ST}} + 5 \text{ mm}$

### Key Numbers to Remember
- ☐ Permissible bearing pressure = **1.0 N/mm²** (oil-lubricated white metal).
- ☐ Permissible bending stress = **100 N/mm²** (cast Mn-Br).
- ☐ Permissible bending stress = **120 N/mm²** (cast steel).
- ☐ E (steel) = **210,000 N/mm²**.
- ☐ E (Mn-Br) = **105,000 N/mm²**.
- ☐ Strut profile drag coefficient $C_D$ = **0.05 to 0.12** (well-faired).

### Key Materials
- ☐ Stern Tube body: **Cast steel** (modern), cast iron (older).
- ☐ Bearing liner: **White metal (Babbitt)** — Sn-based alloy.
- ☐ Brackets: **Cast Manganese Bronze (Mn-Br)** or **stainless steel** (warships).
- ☐ Packing: **Asbestos-free soft packing** (modern), flax (older).
- ☐ Modern seals: **Mechanical face seal** (Burgmann, John Crane).

### Key Comparisons
- ☐ **P-Bracket**: Single arm, lower drag, warships.
- ☐ **A-Bracket**: Two arms, higher drag, merchant vessels.
- ☐ **White metal vs. Lignum vitae**: White metal = oil-lubricated, modern; Lignum vitae = water-lubricated, older.
- ☐ **Oil-lubricated vs. Water-lubricated**: Oil = better film, environmental risk; Water = environmentally safe, lower load capacity.

### SOLAS / IACS / Classification Society Trivia
- ☐ **SOLAS Ch II-1 Reg 31** — Stern tube watertight to load waterline.
- ☐ **SOLAS** — Two independent means of stern tube lubrication.
- ☐ **MARPOL Annex VI** — US EPA VGP drives water-lubricated stern tubes.
- ☐ **IACS UR S1** — Shaft design rules.
- ☐ **IACS UR S17** — Stern tube and shaft support design.

### Drawing Checklist (For Sketch Questions)
- ☐ Side elevation of stern assembly showing all 8–10 components.
- ☐ Aft view of P-bracket and A-bracket.
- ☐ Sectioned view of stern tube showing bearing and seals.
- ☐ Labels with leader lines; abbreviations as per Section 4.8.

### Numerical Problem-Solving Sequence
1. **Identify given** values and required unknowns.
2. **Select formula** from cheat sheet.
3. **Convert units** (kN → N, m → mm) — KTU bugbear!
4. **Substitute carefully** with parentheses.
5. **Calculate step-by-step** (no skipping).
6. **Verify ratio** (e.g., L/D ratio check).
7. **State conclusion** explicitly ("Conforms" / "Safe" / "Not safe, revise to...").
